ref: In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth.
text: Unimbɔti nín kíĺ ki náań kutagbɔŋu nì kitiŋee,

ref: The earth was barren, with no form of life; it was under a roaring ocean covered with darkness. But the Spirit of God was moving over the water. The First Day
text: kitiŋ kaa nìn cáá dikíl ní tiba nì tiba kaa nìn bí ki-pu ní dibɔmbɔndi nìn bīī inyiŋfi pu. Kitiŋ nìn sá nnyim ŋma ní, ní Unimbɔti Fam nìn dàn̄ nnyimee pu.

ref: God said, “I command light to shine!” And light started shining.
text: Ní Unimbɔti bí yii: «Ŋwalifi ń nyā!» Ní ŋwalifi nyáń.

ref: God looked at the light and saw that it was good. He separated light from darkness
text: Ní Unimbɔti ká yii ŋwalifi ŋān̄, ní u cúú ŋwalifi nì dibɔmbɔndi ki yākatì tɔbin.

ref: and named the light “Day” and the darkness “Night.” Evening came, then morning—that was the first day. The Second Day
text: Ní Unimbɔti yíiń ŋwalifi yii ŋwiimpu ní ki yíiń dibɔmbɔndi yii kunyeeu. Ní kunyeeu mɔ́ ní kutaa dūu wúntì, ŋwimpeepeekaa dal dee.

ref: God said, “I command a dome to separate the water above it from the water below it.”
text: Ní Unimbɔti bí yii: «Kugbɔŋu kubɔ ń nyā kí dàntī kí yàkatī inyiŋfi mili!»

ref: and named it “Sky.” Evening came, then morning—that was the second day. The Third Day
text: Ní Unimbɔti yíiń kugbɔŋuee yii kutagbɔŋu. Ní kunyeeu mɔ́ ní kutaa dūu wúntì, ŋwinliliiti dal dee.

ref: God said, “I command the water under the sky to come together in one place, so there will be dry ground.” And that's what happened.
text: Ní Unimbɔti bí yii: «Nnyim min dɔ̄ taapuee ń kóoǹ kí kpāfì dijandi diba, dijandi din kíǹee ń nīn sá ŋkulimmi!» Ní an ŋá míǹ.

ref: God named the dry ground “Land,” and he named the water “Ocean.” God looked at what he had done and saw that it was good.
text: Ní u yíiń dijandi din kūńee yii kitiŋ, ní ki yíiń nnyim min kóoǹ ki kpáfèe yii tiŋku. Ní u ká yii míǹ ŋān̄.

ref: God said, “I command the earth to produce all kinds of plants, including fruit trees and grain.” And that's what happened.
text: Unimbɔti bí yii: «Kumɔsɔŋu ń nyā kitiŋ pu, kumoou kamaa ń lòoǹ ku-boolii ní isufi mun ń nyā, busubu kamaa ń lòoǹ bu-boolii!» Ní an ŋá míǹ.

ref: The earth produced all kinds of vegetation. God looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: Ní kumɔsɔŋu nyáń kitiŋ pu, ní timool tin làá lòoǹ ibee lòon̄ tì nín sá dimɔcil diŋyee boolii, ní busubu kamaa mun lòon̄ bu nín sá disucil diŋyee boolii. Ní Unimbɔti ká yii míǹ ŋān̄.

ref: Evening came, then morning—that was the third day. The Fourth Day
text: Ní kunyeeu mɔ́ ní kutaa dūu wúntì, ŋwintataati dal dee.

ref: God said, “I command lights to appear in the sky and to separate day from night and to show the time for seasons, special days, and years.
text: Ní Unimbɔti bí yii: «Iwalfi ń nyā kí nīn bí kutagbɔŋu pu kí yàkatī ŋwiimpu nì kunyeeu, bí nīn cáa dāantí iyunti, kí nīn cáa kàaǹ iwiin nì abin nì iwiŋkpaan,

ref: I command them to shine on the earth.” And that's what happened.
text: iwalfi gbantee ń nīn bí kutagbɔŋu pu ki ŋmíntí kitiŋ pu.» Ní an ŋá míǹ.

ref: God made two powerful lights, the brighter one to rule the day and the other to rule the night. He also made the stars.
text: Míǹ ní Unimbɔti ŋá iwalfikpaan ili: ŋkpaŋkpaan di sá ŋwìiń, kutaa yaa wúntèe, ŋú nīn bí ki ŋmíntí. Kiwaai di sá uŋmal, kunyeeuee, ú nīn bí ki ŋmíntí, ní ki ŋá iŋmalbijaa mun.

ref: Then God put these lights in the sky to shine on the earth,
text: Ní Unimbɔti dū i-kɔkɔ ki kāaǹ kutagbɔŋu pu í nīn ŋmíntí kitiŋ pu,

ref: to rule day and night, and to separate light from darkness. God looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: ŋubɔ ń nīn bí ki ŋmíntí ŋwiimpu, ŋun kíǹee ń nīn bí ki ŋmíntí kunyeeu. Unimbɔti ká yii míǹ ŋān̄.

ref: Evening came, then morning—that was the fourth day. The Fifth Day
text: Ní kunyeeu mɔ́ ní kutaa dūu wúntì, ŋwinnanaati dal dee.

ref: God said, “I command the ocean to be full of living creatures, and I command birds to fly above the earth.”
text: Ní Unimbɔti bí yii: «Tiwan tin cáá kufoouee ń kpátí kí gbí nnyim poon dàaa, inoon mun ń nīn bí kutaa pu ki ŋàaǹ.»

ref: So God made the giant sea monsters and all the living creatures that swim in the ocean. He also made every kind of bird. God looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: Ní Unimbɔti náań tiwambilindi tin kóò nnyiminee nì tin kɔkɔ cáá kufoou ki kpátìi gbí nnyim poonee nì ti-buluku ti-buluku. U náań inoon kɔkɔ nì i-buluku i-buluku. Unimbɔti ká yii míǹ ŋān̄.

ref: Then he gave the living creatures his blessing—he told the ocean creatures to increase and live everywhere in the ocean and the birds to increase everywhere on earth.
text: Ní Unimbɔti bīiǹ ti-boon ki bí-tì yii: «Ní bùtī kí gbí tiŋku, inoon mun ń bùtī kí gbí kitiŋ!»

ref: Evening came, then morning—that was the fifth day. The Sixth Day
text: Ní kunyeeu mɔ́ ní kutaa dūu wúntì, ŋwiŋŋmooŋmooti dal dee.

ref: God said, “I command the earth to give life to all kinds of tame animals, wild animals, and reptiles.” And that's what happened.
text: Ní Unimbɔti bí yii: «Tiwan tin fòotēe kɔkɔ kubuluu kubuluu ń nyā kí nīn bí kitiŋ pu, an dàkà yii tiwaŋkuti nì tin fù ti-biŋŋi puee nì ipeel ń nyā!» Ní an ŋá míǹ.

ref: God made every one of them. Then he looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: Ní Unimbɔti nyántì kumooun peelii kɔkɔ kubuluu kubuluu nì tiwaŋkuti kɔkɔ kubuluu kubuluu nì tin kɔkɔ fù ti-biŋŋi puee kubuluu kubuluu. Ní Unimbɔti ká yii míǹ ŋān̄. Unimbɔti náań unil

ref: God said, “Now we will make humans, and they will be like us. We will let them rule the fish, the birds, and all other living creatures.”
text: Ní Unimbɔti bí yii: «Tí náań unil kí nántíǹ ti-ba, ú nīn náań nì ti, kí nīn cáá mpɔn ijɔm nì inoon pu, kí nīn cáá mpɔn tiwaŋkuti nì ipeel kɔkɔ nì tin kɔkɔ fù ti-biŋŋi pu ki bí kitiŋ puee pu!»

ref: So God created humans to be like himself; he made men and women.
text: Ní Unimbɔti náań unil ki nántiǹ u-ba, u náań-u ki nántiǹ úǹ Unimbɔti u-ba ní. U nìn náań uninja nì unimpu ní.

ref: God gave them his blessing and said: Have a lot of children! Fill the earth with people and bring it under your control. Rule over the fish in the ocean, the birds in the sky, and every animal on the earth.
text: Ní Unimbɔti bīiǹ bi-boon ki bí-bi yii: «Ní màal̀ kí bùtī, binib ń gbí kitiŋ. Ní nīn cáá mpɔn kitiŋ pu nì ijɔm nì inoon nì tin kɔkɔ pée fù ti-biŋŋi pu ki bí kitiŋ puee pu!»

ref: And I have given the green plants as food for everything else that breathes, including animals, both wild and tame, and birds. And so it was.
text: Míǹ mbaantiim, m dū timool ki tīī tiwaŋkuti tin bí kitiŋ puee kɔkɔ nì inoon kɔkɔ nì tin kɔkɔ pée fù ti-biŋŋi pu, ki bí kitiŋ pu ki cáá kufoouee án nīn sá tì-jinti.» Ní an ŋá míǹ.

ref: God looked at what he had done. All of it was very good! Evening came, then morning—that was the sixth day.
text: Unimbɔti ká yii tin kɔkɔ u ŋée ŋān̄ tikpil. Ní kunyeeu mɔ́ ní kutaa dūu wúntì, ŋwinluluuti dal dee.

ref: So the heavens and the earth and everything else were created. The Seventh Day
text: Míǹ ní Unimbɔti náań kutagbɔŋu nì tin kɔkɔ bí ku-puee, ní ki náań kitiŋ nì tin kɔkɔ bí ki-puee ki dóò.

ref: By the seventh day God had finished his work, and so he rested.
text: Unimbɔti túń u-tuuŋŋi ki dóò, ní ŋwinluluútee, u kāl̀ ki ŋūŋfì atuun ŋin kɔkɔ u ŋée pu.

ref: God blessed the seventh day and made it special, because on that day he rested from his work.
text: Míǹ puee Unimbɔti bīiǹ ŋwinluluútee pu ní ki gāntì-ŋu ki síiǹ u-ba, kun puee ŋwiin gbanti ní u kāl̀ ki ŋūŋfì atuun ŋin kɔkɔ u ŋáaǹee pu.

ref: That's how God created the heavens and the earth. The Garden of Eden When the Lord God made the heavens and the earth,
text: Kutagbɔŋu nì kitiŋ náantam nín kíĺ puee dee. Eedeen kpaabun bɔti Ŋyunti ŋun ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti nyántì kitiŋ nì kutagbɔŋuee,

ref: no grass or plants were growing anywhere. God had not yet sent any rain, and there was no one to work the land.
text: kisubiki báà kiba kaa nìn dáá nyáń kitiŋ pu, ní kumoou báà kubɔ mun kaa nìn dáá nyáń, kun puee ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti kaa nìn dáá yāntì utaa nìn̄. Unil mun nìn kpá kí kpɔ̀ tikpaal kitiŋ pu.

ref: But streams came up from the ground and watered the earth.
text: Ama kitiŋ taapu ní nnyim nìn nyēeń ki sɔ̀ŋkī an kɔkɔ.

ref: The Lord God took some soil from the ground and made a man. God breathed life into the man, and the man started breathing.
text: Ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti dū ntam ní ki máń unil, ní ki púfì ŋfam ki ŋá u-maŋfataabuŋyin, ní u kpántì unifoo.

ref: The Lord made a garden in a place called Eden, which was in the east, and he put the man there.
text: Ní ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti náań isufi kpaabu Eedeen tiŋki poon, bu bí ŋwìiń nyalaŋki pú, ní ki dū unil un u máńee ki kāaǹ bukpaayee poon.

ref: The Lord God filled the garden with all kinds of beautiful trees and fruit trees. Two other trees were in the middle of the garden. One of these gave life—the other gave the wisdom to know the difference between right and wrong.
text: Ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti yāntì isufi in biliŋ ŋān̄ ŋkpèé nì diŋmɔlee nyáń ki bí i-ba i-ba, ní busubu bun tìì dimaŋfalee nì bun yàntī bi béetí tiŋan nì tikpitilee yú bukpaayee kansikin.

ref: From Eden a river flowed out to water the garden, then it divided into four rivers.
text: Ní mbun ŋubɔ nyēeń Eedeen tiŋkin ki sɔ̀ŋkī bukpaayee. Bukpaayee poon ní mbunee cúu yākatì abuntaa anaa.

ref: The first one is the Pishon River that flows through the land of Havilah,
text: Dibuntaapeekaal ní bi yíì Piisɔn. Díǹ di māntìi tú Afiila tiŋki kin poon ní sindaariya bée.

ref: where pure gold, rare perfumes, and precious stones are found.
text: Kitiŋ gbanti sindaariyau sá umɔnti ní, ní talaalu un bi yíì bideeliyoomee nì ataŋkpamɔnti ŋin bi yíì soowamee bí niin.

ref: The second is the Gihon River that winds through Ethiopia.
text: Dibuntaaliliitiilee ní bi yíì Giiyɔn. Díǹ di māntìi tú Kusa tiŋki kɔkɔ.

ref: The Tigris River that flows east of Assyria is the third, and the fourth is the Euphrates River.
text: Bi nìn yíì dibuntaatataatiilee yii Tiiga. Díǹ di pùubìń Asuur tiŋkin, ŋwìiń nyalaŋki pú. Dibuntaananaatiil ní bi yíì Eefiraati.

ref: The Lord God put the man in the Garden of Eden to take care of it and to look after it.
text: Ní ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti dū unil ki kāaǹ Eedeen kpaabun ú nīn kù-bu kí nīn kpèé-bu.

ref: But the Lord told him, “You may eat fruit from any tree in the garden,
text: Ní ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti síiǹ unil mara nee yii: «A ŋūǹ kí ŋmɔ́ isufi in yú bukpaanee kɔkɔ biliŋ,

ref: except the one that has the power to let you know the difference between right and wrong. If you eat any fruit from that tree, you will die before the day is over!”
text: ama busubu bun bil a yaa ŋmɔ́ a làá béé tiŋan nì tikpitilee ní á taa ŋmɔ́. Daliŋkin a yaa ŋmɔ́ busubu gbanti bilee, a làá kpú ní.»

ref: The Lord God said, “It isn't good for the man to live alone. I will make a suitable partner for him.”
text: Ní ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti bí yii: «An kaa ŋān̄ unil ń nīn bí u-baba míǹ. M làá náań un làá nīn tútúǹ-uee kí ŋàl̄-u.»

ref: So the Lord God made him fall into a deep sleep, and he took out one of the man's ribs. Then after closing the man's side,
text: Ní ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti yāntì ŋgeembiim miba dɔ́miń ki cúú unil ní u gèen̄. Ŋyunti ŋun u bíi gèeǹee ní ti-Dindaan lēē u-takakalkpabil diba ní ki fātìi dūu bīiǹ.

ref: the Lord made a woman out of the rib. The Lord God brought her to the man,
text: Ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti dū ditakakalkpabil din u lēē unilinee ki máń unimpu ní ki cáań-u unil cee.

ref: and the man exclaimed, “Here is someone like me! She is part of my body, my own flesh and bones. She came from me, a man. So I will name her Woman!”
text: Ní unil bí yii: «Kpèè min bí nee, m-kpabiŋ kpabil sèé, m-wunnanti nanti sèé. Un bí nee ní bi làá nīn yíì unimpu. Kun puee uninjan ní bi lēeń-u.»

ref: That's why a man will leave his own father and mother. He marries a woman, and the two of them become like one person.
text: An pu ní uninja dāa nyēē u-ba kaa ti bí u-baa nì u-na cee ní u nì u-nimpuu ń kɔ́ tɔbin, ní kí kpántí ŋwuŋgbambaanti.

ref: Although the man and his wife were both naked, they were not ashamed.
text: Uninja nì unimpu nìn pée bí dicancaŋgbeel ní kaa nìn cáá tɔb pu icoo.

ref: The snake was sneakier than any of the other wild animals that the Lord God had made. One day it came to the woman and asked, “Did God tell you not to eat fruit from any tree in the garden?”
text: Ukoobu di nìn nyí diŋmatindi ipeel in ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti nìn náańee kɔkɔ kansikin. Dalbee ní u dɔ́miń ki bí unimpu yii: «Tiŋman ní Unimbɔti lafun lī yii ní taa ŋmɔ́ bukpaa nee poon subiliŋaaa?»

ref: The woman answered, “God said we could eat fruit from any tree in the garden,
text: Ní unimpu kíí-u yii: «Ti ŋūǹ kí ŋmɔ́ isufi in yú bukpaa poonee biliŋ.

ref: except the one in the middle. He told us not to eat fruit from that tree or even to touch it. If we do, we will die.”
text: Ama busubu bun yú bukpaa kansikinee bil ní Unimbɔti bí yii tí taa ŋmɔ́ kí taa gbēē-bu, an yaa sá fám ti làá kpú.»

ref: “No, you won't!” the snake replied.
text: Niinee ní ukoobu bí unimpu yii: «Kaa sá míǹ, naa làá kpú.

ref: “God understands what will happen on the day you eat fruit from that tree. You will see what you have done, and you will know the difference between right and wrong, just as God does.”
text: Di sá Unimbɔti nyí yii daliŋkin ni yaa ŋmɔ́ busubu gbanti bilee, ni-nimbiliŋ làá ŋúbití ní ní nīn kíǹ ki nyí tiŋan nì tikpitil kii úǹ Unimbɔti nín nyí puee.»

ref: The woman stared at the fruit. It looked beautiful and tasty. She wanted the wisdom that it would give her, and she ate some of the fruit. Her husband was there with her, so she gave some to him, and he ate it too.
text: Unimpu mun ká yii busubuee bil ŋān̄ ki mɔ̄ diŋmɔl, ki cáá nnimbitaataam, ki lafun máaǹ kí ŋúbití unil nimbiliŋ, ní u kītì ki ŋmɔ́. U-cal bí u-cee ní u tīī u mun ní u ŋmɔ́.

ref: At once they saw what they had done, and they realized they were naked. Then they sewed fig leaves together to cover themselves.
text: Niinee ní bi-liitil nimbiliŋ cúu ŋúbitì ní bi ká yii bi bí dicancaŋgbeel, ní bi tīfì busubu bun bi yíì fiigee faati ki ŋáĺ ki dūu lōoǹ.

ref: Late in the afternoon, when the breeze began to blow, the man and woman heard the Lord God walking in the garden. So they hid behind some trees. Sin Brings a Curse
text: Kujoou jóotì kubuŋu bíi jītí fílfilee ní bi gbìl̄ ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti pu u bí ki còom̄ bukpaan. Ní bi sáǹ ki bál̀-u bukpaayee sufii kansikin.

ref: The Lord God called out to the man and asked, “Where are you?”
text: Ní ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti yíiń uninja yii: «A bí la ní?»

ref: The man answered, “I was naked, and when I heard you walking through the garden, I was frightened and hid!”
text: Ní u kíí-u yii: «M nín bí bukpaan ki gbìl̄ a-puee ní m fāŋkì, kun puee m bí dicancaŋgbeel, an pu ní m bál̀.»

ref: “How did you know you were naked?” God asked. “Did you eat any fruit from that tree in the middle of the garden?”
text: Ní ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti bí-u yii: «Ŋma tùkū-si yii a bí dicancaŋgbeel? Àá a ŋmɔ́ busubu bun bil m kpáafì-si yii á taa ŋmɔ́ee ní?»

ref: “It was the woman you put here with me,” the man said. “She gave me some of the fruit, and I ate it.”
text: Ní uninja kíí-u yii: «Unimpu un a cáań ki ŋàl̄-mee di kītì disubil gbanti ki tīī-mi ní m ŋmɔ́.»

ref: The Lord God then asked the woman, “What have you done?” “The snake tricked me,” she answered, “and I ate some of that fruit.”
text: Ní ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti bí unimpu yii: «Ba di yì-tì ní a ŋá míǹ?» Ní unimpu kíí-u yii: «Ukoobu di kíil̀-mi ní m ŋmɔ́.»

ref: So the Lord God said to the snake: “Because of what you have done, you will be the only animal to suffer this curse— For as long as you live, you will crawl on your stomach and eat dirt.
text: Ní ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti bí ukoobu yii: «An nín ŋá pu ní a ŋá mimmee, m tɔ́-si mbusu tiwaŋkuti nì ipeel kɔkɔ kansikin. A làá nīn kíǹ ki fù a-bindi pu ní. A-jinti di làá nīn kíǹ ki sá ŋkpɔŋkpɔm kí tin sāā a-kúm dal.

ref: You and this woman will hate each other; your descendants and hers will always be enemies. One of hers will strike you on the head, and you will strike him on the heel.”
text: M làá náań kudɔ̀ŋu kí bíĺ sii nì unimpu kansikin, kí bíĺ a-maaliŋu nì u-maaliŋu kansikin. U-maaliŋu làá dāá tāā kí mátí a-yil, ní sii ń dāá jú úǹ tagbíndi.»

ref: Then the Lord God said to the woman, “You will suffer terribly when you give birth. But you will still desire your husband, and he will rule over you.”
text: Ní u bí unimpu yii: «A nín ŋá miŋyee, m làá gbéeǹ-si timatíǹdi gìńdi. Nì falaa ní a làá nīn màal̀ a-biyaamu. A-cal neeliŋu làá nīn pée cáá-si, ama úǹ di làá nīn cáá sii pu mpɔn.»

ref: The Lord said to the man, “You listened to your wife and ate the fruit I told you not to eat. And so, the ground will be under a curse because of what you did. As long as you live, you will have to struggle to grow enough food.
text: Ní ki bí uninja yii: «A nín ŋá pu ki pílifì a-nimpuu nyɔkɔ ní ki ŋmɔ́ busubu bun bil man kpáafì-si yii á taa ŋmɔ́ee, a-puee m tɔ́ kitiŋ mbusu. A làá nīn ŋáań falaa ní kí nín ká tijin kí jī. Míǹ ní án làá nīn bí-si áli kí tin sāā a-kúm dal.

ref: Your food will be plants, but the ground will produce thorns and thistles.
text: «Apɔn nì abɔjanaakun di làá nīn nyēē a-kpaabun, a-kpaajinti ní a làá nīn jīǹ.

ref: You will sweat all your life to earn a living; you were made out of soil, and you will once again turn into soil.”
text: See á tɔ̄ ditundi kí káaĺ kutuntuŋu a-yigbeel pu ní kí wàatī kí nín ká tijin kí jī. Án làá nīn pée bí-si míǹ ní, áli á dāá fàtīí gítí ntam min m lēē ki máń-see poon. Kun puee a sá ŋkpɔŋkpɔm ní ki làá fàtīí gítí ŋkpɔŋkpɔm.»

ref: The man Adam named his wife Eve because she would become the mother of all who live.
text: Ní uninja pú u-nimpuu diyindi yii Awa, taapu di sá yii un bí dimaŋfalinee, kun puee úǹ di sá binifoobi kɔkɔ na.

ref: Then the Lord God made clothes out of animal skins for the man and his wife.
text: Ní ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti dū tigban ki lōoǹ Adam nì u-nimpuu.

ref: The Lord said, “They now know the difference between right and wrong, just as we do. But they must not be allowed to eat fruit from the tree that lets them live forever.”
text: U ŋá míǹ ki dóòyee ní ki bí yii: «Kɔŋkɔnnee, unil kpántì kii ti-kansikin ubɔ dee, ki kíǹ ki nyí tiŋan nì tikpitil. Tí taa yàntī ú ŋmɔ́ dimaŋfal subil kí nīn bí jaanjaan kaa kúù.»

ref: So the Lord God sent them out of the Garden of Eden, where they would have to work the ground from which the man had been made.
text: Niinee ní ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti jìn̄-u Eedeen kpaabun ú cù kí nīn kù kitiŋ kin pu bi lēē ki máń-uee.

ref: Then God put winged creatures at the entrance to the garden and a flaming, flashing sword to guard the way to the life-giving tree.
text: U jìn̄ unil mimmee ní ki cáań keerubimbi ki yóoǹ bukpaayee nyɔkɔpu ŋwìiń nyalaŋki pú, bi ŋúb́ kitàataajiki ki fīintí an ŋmóò làka laka, ki yú dimaŋfal subu saŋŋu pu ki cí-ŋu.

ref: Adam and Eve had a son. Then Eve said, “I'll name him Cain because I got him with the help of the Lord.”
text: Adam béè u-nimpuu Awa ní u cáá dipool ki màl̄ Kaayeen, ní ki bí yii: «Ti-Dindaan tútúǹ-mi m màl̄ kibininjabiki.»

ref: Later she had another son and named him Abel. Abel became a sheep farmer, but Cain farmed the land.
text: Míǹ boonee ní u gíti cáá dipool ki màl̄ Kaayeen naal Abeel. Abeel wíiǹee ní ki bíi kpàá tiwaŋkuti, ní u-maan Kaayeen úǹ kù tikpaal.

ref: One day, Cain gave part of his harvest to the Lord,
text: An dāa bāań ŋyunti ŋubɔee ní Kaayeen lēeń u-kpaajinti ki cáań ki ŋá ti-Dindaan saraa.

ref: and Abel also gave an offering to the Lord. He killed the first-born lamb from one of his sheep and gave the Lord the best parts of it. The Lord was pleased with Abel and his offering,
text: Ní Abeel mun lēeń apibil ŋin u-piii iba péé ki màl̄ee ní ki dū ŋi-nanti tin cáá ŋkpamee ki ŋá ti-Dindaan saraa. Ní ti-Dindaan fōō Abeel nì u-saraau,

ref: but not with Cain and his offering. This made Cain so angry that he could not hide his feelings.
text: ama ní ki fātì u-boon ki tīī Kaayeen nì u-saraau. An puee ní Kaayeen fōō diŋuul tikpil, u-nimbiin cúu bíil̀.

ref: The Lord said to Cain: What's wrong with you? Why do you look so angry?
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí Kaayeen yii: «Ba ŋá ní a fōō diŋuul, a-nimbiin cúu bíil̀ mimmee?

ref: If you had done the right thing, you would be smiling. But you did the wrong thing, and now sin is waiting to attack you like a lion. Sin wants to destroy you, but don't let it!
text: A yaa bàà ŋá tin ŋān̄ee, a-yil bàà gáań ní. A yaa kaa ŋá tin ŋān̄ee, ikpiti kpátì ki dɔ̄ a-punyɔkɔpu ki nyàab̀-si, ama sii pɔ̄ɔkiǹ-i.»

ref: Cain said to his brother Abel, “Let's go for a walk.” And when they were out in a field, Cain attacked and killed him.
text: Niinee ní Kaayeen sòon̄ nì u-naal Abeel. Ŋyunti ŋun bi bí kusaaunee ní Kaayeen cúú u-naal Abeel ki kūū.

ref: Afterwards the Lord asked Cain, “Where is Abel?” “How should I know?” he answered. “Am I supposed to look after my brother?”
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bālifì Kaayeen yii: «A-naal Abeel bí la?» Ní u kíí-u yii: «Maa nyí. Man di cí m-naalaaa?»

ref: Then the Lord said: Why have you done this terrible thing? You killed your own brother, and his blood flowed onto the ground. Now his blood is calling out for me to punish you.
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí-u yii: «Ba ní a ŋée! A-naal fatikuu di gbáàń ibiil taapu ki dāa bāań man nee.

ref: When you try to farm the land, it won't produce anything for you. From now on, you'll be without a home, and you'll spend the rest of your life wandering from place to place.
text: A yaa ti kùn̄ee, kitiŋ kaa làá ti lòoǹ. A làá nīn kíǹ ki tú ki yìiǹ an kɔkɔ ní kitiŋ pu.»

ref: “This punishment is too hard!” Cain said.
text: Ní Kaayeen bí ti-Dindaan yii: «Óo, m-yìntitam gìńdi nyìmiǹ-mi maa ŋūǹ kí túń.

ref: “You're making me leave my home and live far from you. I will have to wander about without a home, and just anyone could kill me.”
text: A yaa jìn̄-mi díǹ nee kitiŋ pu an sá yii ḿ nīn cá ndandati kí taa nín̄ pìlīǹ-si, kí nīn tú ki yìiǹee, ubɔ yaa ká-mee, see ú kpɔ̀-mi.»

ref: “No!” the Lord answered. “Anyone who kills you will be punished seven times worse than I am punishing you.” So the Lord put a mark on Cain to warn everyone not to kill him.
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí-u yii: «Aayee! Un di yaa kūū Kaayeeŋyee, bi làá téeǹ udaan min min u ŋée miluli pu ní.» Ní ti-Dindaan ŋá u-pu kudaaŋŋu kubɔ yii ubɔ yaa ká-uee, ú taa kpɔ̀-u.

ref: But Cain had to go far from the Lord and live in the Land of Wandering, which is east of Eden. More and More People
text: Ní Kaayeen búntì ndandati kaa ti pìl̄ ti-Dindaan, ki tin kóòń kitiŋ kin bi yíì Noodee. A yaa bí Eedeenee, kì bí ŋwìiń nyalaŋki pú ní. Kaayeen naantiibi bɔti

ref: Later, Cain and his wife had a son named Enoch. At the time Cain was building a town, and so he named it Enoch after his son.
text: Kaayeen cáá unimpu, ní u cáá dipool ki màl̄ Eenɔki. Boonee ní u máń adiin ní an dūu kpántì kitiŋ, ní u dū u-jipɔɔn yindi Eenɔki ki pú-kì.

ref: Then Enoch had a son named Irad, who had a son named Mehujael, who had a son named Methushael, who had a son named Lamech.
text: Ní Eenɔki màl̄ Yiraadi, ní Yiraadi màl̄ Meewujayeel, ní Meewujayeel màl̄ Meetukayeel, ní Meetukayeel màl̄ Leemeeki.

ref: Lamech and Zillah had a son named Tubal Cain who made tools out of bronze and iron. They also had a daughter, whose name was Naamah.
text: Ní Siila mun màl̄ Tubaal-Kaayeen. Úǹ di nìn sá ucaa ki cáá tikúti nì tasab ki mɔ́ɔ̀ tiwan nì titɔntɔŋwan kɔkɔ. Naama di nìn sá Tubaal-Kaayeen niisal.

ref: One day, Lamech said to his two wives, “A young man wounded me, and I killed him.
text: Ní Leemeeki bí u-nimpoobi yii: «Ada nì Siila, pīlifìmaan-mi! M-nimpoobi, kpòom̄maan ni-tafaliŋ kí pīlifì tin m làá sòoǹee! Bi ŋá-mi tiweendi ní m kūū unil. Tiweendi tin bi ŋá-mee puee, m kūū kibiki.

ref: Anyone who tries to get even with me will be punished ten times more than anyone who tries to get even with Cain.”
text: Bi làá tɔ̀ Kaayeen pɔntooŋyi ŋfunyɔm miluli pu, ní kí tɔ̀ man Leemeeki pɔntooŋyi ŋfunyɔm mita kpá imuŋku inaa pu.»

ref: Adam and his wife had another son. They named him Seth, because they said, “God has given us a son to take the place of Abel, who was killed by his brother Cain.”
text: Adam nì u-nimpuu gíti kpáfì ní u-nimpuu cáá dipool ki màl̄ kibininjabiki ní ki pú-kì diyindi yii Seeti, ki bí yii: «Unimbɔti gíti tīī-mi kibininjabiki ki téeǹ Abeel un Kaayeen kūūyee yil pu.»

ref: Later, Seth had a son and named him Enosh. About this time people started worshiping the Lord.
text: Ní Seeti mun màl̄ kibininjabiki ní ki pú-kì diyindi yii Eenɔsi. Ŋyunti gbanti ní binib kíĺ ki kíǹ ki yíì Unimbɔti yii ti-Dindaan.

ref: God created men and women to be like himself. He gave them his blessing and called them human beings. The following is a list of the descendants of Adam, the first man:
text: Uninja nì unimpu ní u nìn náań. U nìn bīiǹ bi-boon ní ki pú-bi diyindi yii unil.

ref: and God took him away at the age of
text: Eenɔki nìn nóó Unimbɔti deedee, an puee Unimbɔti yōoǹ-u baa ti ká-u.

ref: Lamech said, “I'll name him Noah because he will give us comfort, as we struggle hard to make a living on this land that the Lord has put under a curse.”
text: U nìn pú-kì diyindi yii Noowa ní ki bí yii: «Un bí nee di làá cáań-ti dipɔɔsɔŋkil ti-Dindaan nín tɔ́ kitiŋ mbusu ti kɔ́ agìŋ́kpaan ŋin poonee.»

ref: Then the Lord said, “I won't let my life-giving breath remain in anyone forever. No one will live for more than
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí yii: «Maa làá yàntī kufoou kun m ŋá unilinee ń nīn bí u-ni ki cá jaanjaan. Kun puee u sá tiwun ní ki bí iwɔfin. Waa làá yúntí kí jītí abin dilaataal nì mmuŋku pu.»

ref: The children of the supernatural beings who had married these women became famous heroes and warriors. They were called Nephilim and lived on the earth at that time and even later.
text: Tinikpaandi nìn bí kitiŋ pu ŋyunti ŋun Unimbɔti jipɔmbi dɔ́miń ki cáá binifoobi sapɔmbi ki màl̄ mbiyaamuee. Míǹ boonee bi nìn gbíntì ki bí. Bíǹ di nìn sá bijanjaliibi bin nìn nyáń diyindi cannaakuee.

ref: The Lord saw how bad the people on earth were and that everything they thought and planned was evil.
text: Ti-Dindaan nìn ká yii binib kpitii mɔ̀tīi wīintí kitiŋ pu. Baabadalee tibɔkpitil ŋma di nìn gbí bi-pɔbiliŋ poon bi dàkafī.

ref: He was sorry that he had made them,
text: Ní an wùn̄ ti-Dindaan ki kɔ́-u kunimbaau u nín náań unil kitiŋ puee.

ref: and he said, “I'm going to destroy every person on earth! I'll even wipe out animals, birds, and reptiles. I'm sorry I ever made them.”
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí yii: «See m kúntì binib bin m náańee kitiŋ pu. M làá kúntí-bi kí kpáaǹ tiwaŋkuti nì tin kɔkɔ fù ti-biŋŋi puee nì inoon kɔkɔ, kun puee an wùn̄-mi m nín náań-bee pu.»

ref: But the Lord was pleased with Noah,
text: Ama Noowa baba di nìn fáatì ti-Dindaan. Noowa kpáá meela

ref: and this is the story about him. Noah was the only person who lived right and obeyed God.
text: Noowa nì u-biyaamu bɔti sɔ̀. Noowa nìn sá unimɔnti ní ki bí deedee u-yuntiŋu nib kansikin. U nìn nóó Unimbɔti mmɔntiim.

ref: He had three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth.
text: U nìn màl̄ mbininjabiyaamu muta. Bi-yiŋŋi di sá Seem nì Kaam nì Jaafeeti.

ref: God knew that everyone was terribly cruel and violent.
text: Unimbɔti nìn ká yii ikpiti jíń kitiŋ, di sá yii binib nìn dū bi-ba ki líì nì tikpitil.

ref: Get some good lumber and build a boat. Put rooms in it and cover it with tar inside and out.
text: Á dū busubu bun bi yíì goofeeree dɔi kí kpáá meela, kí pìtī poon adiin kí bɔ̀ntī poon nì boon aŋɔ.

ref: Noah did everything God told him to do.
text: Ní Noowa lafun ŋá kii Unimbɔti nín bí yii ú ŋá puee deedee.

ref: I'm going to send a flood that will destroy everything that breathes! Nothing will be left alive.
text: Man làá yàntī utaa ń nì, ní nnyiŋgbeen ń kúntí tin kɔkɔ pée cáá kufoou ki bí kitiŋ puee, tin kɔkɔ bí kitiŋ puee làá céetí.

ref: But I solemnly promise that you, your wife, your sons, and your daughters-in-law will be kept safe in the boat.
text: Ama m làá pòon̄ sii dipoondi. A làá dāá kɔ́ meelan, sii nì a-nimpuu nì a-jipɔmbi nì a-coopoobi.

ref: Take into the boat with you a male and a female of every kind of animal and bird, as well as a male and a female of every reptile. I don't want them to be destroyed.
text: Á dāá lēē unoon kamaa buluku uja nì usal, nì kuwaŋkuluu kamaa buluku uja nì usal, nì kun kamaa pée fù kitiŋ puee buluku uja nì usal, bi-kɔkɔ ń dāá dāan̄ a-cee kí ŋmátí.

ref: The Lord told Noah: Take your whole family with you into the boat, because you are the only one on this earth who pleases me.
text: Noowa kpáá meela ki dóòyee ní ti-Dindaan bí-u yii: «Á kɔ́ meelan, sii nì a-dicaŋku kɔkɔ, kun puee a-yuntiŋu nib nee kansikinee, sii ní m ká yii a bí deedee.

ref: Take seven pairs of every kind of animal that can be used for sacrifice and one pair of all others.
text: Á kɔ́ǹ tiwaŋkuti tin kɔkɔ ŋān̄ bí dūú ŋá saraayee tiluli tiluli, ijaa nì isal, ní kí kɔ́ǹ tin kaa ŋān̄ bí dūú ŋá saraayee tili tili, uja nì usal.

ref: Also take seven pairs of every kind of bird with you. Do this so there will always be animals and birds on the earth.
text: Á kɔ́ǹ inoon kɔkɔ mun míǹ, ijaa iluli isal iluli, kí ŋmā kí kpāafì i-boolii kitiŋ pu.

ref: He obeyed God and took a male and a female of each kind of animal and bird into the boat with him.
text: kubuluu kamaa uja nì usal nìn dɔ́miń ki kɔ́ meela poon Noowa cee kii Unimbɔti nín nìn pée tùkū-u puee.

ref: Seven days later a flood began to cover the earth.
text: Iwiin iluli boonee, ní utaa nìn̄, ní nnyiŋgbeen dɔ́miń ki bīiǹ kitiŋ pu.

ref: On that day Noah and his wife went into the boat with their three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth, and their wives.
text: An wiiŋŋu ní Noowa nì u-nimpuu nì u-jipɔmbi Seem nì Kaam nì Jaafeeti nì bi-nimpoobi bitee kɔ́ meelan.

ref: Noah took a male and a female of every living creature with him,
text: Tiwan tin kɔkɔ pée cáá kufoouee nóoń tɔb tili tili, ki kɔ́ Noowa cee, meela poon.

ref: just as God had told him to do. And when they were all in the boat, the Lord closed the door.
text: Tin kɔkɔ pée cáá kufoouee, uja nì usal nìn kɔ́ meelan kii Unimbɔti nín nìn pée tùkū Noowa puee. Ní ti-Dindaan dūu pīiǹ u-pu.

ref: Finally, the mighty flood was so deep that even the highest mountain peaks were about seven meters below the surface of the water.
text: Nnyim gbí ki bīiǹ ajoon pu ki páaǹ kii meetab biluli pu.

ref: A hundred fifty days later, the water started going down.
text: Nnyim nìn gbí ki dɔ̄ míǹ ki tin sāā iwiin dilaataal nì imuŋku ili nì saalaa.

ref: God did not forget about Noah and the animals with him in the boat. So God made a wind blow, and the water started going down.
text: Unimbɔti kaa nìn súntì Noowa nì ipeel nì tiwaŋkuti tin kɔkɔ nìn bí u-cee meela poonee pu. Di sá u nìn yāntì kubuŋu kubɔ dɔ́miń ki jítì kitiŋ pu ní nnyim sīntì.

ref: Then on the seventeenth day of the seventh month of the year, the boat came to rest somewhere in the Ararat mountains.
text: Uŋmaluluúti poon iwiin saalku nì ili dal ní meela sīntì ki tɔ́kɔǹ ajoon ŋin bi yíì Araratee pu.

ref: The water kept going down, and the mountain tops could be seen on the first day of the tenth month.
text: Nnyim nìn mɔ̀tīi sìntī ki cá ki tin cáa sāā uŋmasaalaati. Uŋmasaalaati gbanti wimpeepeekaaŋu dalee, ní ajoon gbīitìń nnyimin.

ref: Forty days later Noah opened a window to send out a raven, but it kept flying around until the water had dried up.
text: U nìn dū kulaagaau ki líì taapu, ní ku cá ki gítiń ki cá ki gítiń áli nnyim dāa fóotì ní kitiŋ kūń.

ref: Noah wanted to find out if the water had gone down, so he sent out a dove.
text: U nìn tí dū dibeenanjil ki líì kí kpèè yii nnyim yaa bātì kitiŋ pu.

ref: Deep water was still everywhere, and when the dove could not find a place to land, it flew back to the boat. Then Noah held out his hand and helped it back in.
text: Ama dibeenanjil kaa ká tin pu di làá tɔ́kɔńee ní ki fātìi nín gítí u-cee meela poon, kun puee nnyim nìn dáá gbíntìi bí kitiŋ kɔkɔ pu. Ní Noowa tāntì u-ŋal ki cúú-dì ki kɔ́ǹ u-cee meela poon.

ref: Seven days later Noah sent the dove out again.
text: Ní u gíti kāl̀ iwiin iluli boonee ní ki tí nyántì dinanjil ki líì.

ref: It returned in the evening, holding in its beak a green leaf from an olive tree. Noah knew the water was finally going down.
text: Kujoouee ní dibeenanjil fātìi gítì u-cee ki bàb̄ ɔliifi faasɔŋku. Niinee ní Noowa béè yii nnyim fóotì ki bātì kitiŋ pu.

ref: He waited seven more days before sending the dove out again, and this time it did not return.
text: Ní u gíti tí kāl̀ iwiin iluli, boonee ní u fātìi tí nyántì dibeenanjil ki líì ní dàa ti gítì u-cee.

ref: By the twenty-seventh day of the second month, the earth was completely dry.
text: Uŋmaliliiti iwiin mmuŋku nì iluli dalee ní kitiŋ kūń.

ref: God said to Noah,
text: Niinee ní Unimbɔti bí Noowa yii:

ref: “You, your wife, your sons, and your daughters-in-law may now leave the boat.
text: «Nyā meelan, sii nì a-nimpuu nì a-jipɔmbi nì a-coopoobi.

ref: Let out the birds, animals, and reptiles, so they can mate and live all over the earth.”
text: Nyāntí tin kɔkɔ pée cáá kufoou ki bí a-ceeyee, an dàkà yii inoon nì tiwaŋkuti nì tin kɔkɔ fù kitiŋee mun, tí kpátí kí gbí kitiŋ pu kí màal̀ kí bùtī kí gbí kitiŋ.»

ref: After Noah and his family had left the boat,
text: Ní Noowa nyáń meela poon u nì u-nimpuu nì u-jipɔmbi nì u-coopoobi.

ref: the living creatures left in groups of their own kind. The Lord's Promise for the Earth
text: Ní tiwaŋkuti nì ipeel nì tin fù ti-biŋŋi puee nì tin kɔkɔ fù kitiŋ puee, ti-kɔkɔ nì ti-buluku ti-buluku nyáń meela poon.

ref: Noah built an altar where he could offer sacrifices to the Lord. Then he offered on the altar one of each kind of animal and bird that could be used for a sacrifice.
text: Noowa nyáńee, ní ki máń ti-Dindaan nsaraafan, ní ki lēē tiwaŋkuti tin kɔkɔ ŋān̄ bí dūú ŋá saraayee poon tiba, nì inoon in kɔkɔ ŋān̄ bí dūú ŋá saraayee poon iba ki dūu wùn̄ ŋŋmi nsaraafanee pu ki ŋá saraa ki tīī ti-Dindaan.

ref: The smell of the burning offering pleased the Lord, and he said: Never again will I punish the earth for the sinful things its people do. All of them have evil thoughts from the time they are young, but I will never destroy everything that breathes, as I did this time.
text: U-saraauee sɔnsɔfiŋu kɔ́ ti-Dindaan ki mɔ́ɔkiǹ-u ní u lī u-pɔbilin yii: «Kí yòon̄ díǹ kí nīn cée, maa làá ti bīil̀ kitiŋ unil pu. Báà nì min doooo unil biyatin ní u-pɔbil sá dikpitilee, maa làá ti dáaĺ tin kɔkɔ cáá kufoouee tafal kii m nín dáaĺ pu nee.

ref: As long as the earth remains, there will be planting and harvest, cold and heat; winter and summer, day and night.
text: Iyunti in kɔkɔ kitiŋ cáá kí nīn bée, tiwan dibɔldi nì tijin dical, tiwool nì kutuntuŋu, dipeel nì kuseeu, ŋwiimpu nì kunyeeu kpá ki gbèen̄.»

ref: God said to Noah and his sons: I am giving you my blessing. Have a lot of children and grandchildren, so people will live everywhere on this earth.
text: Unimbɔti bīiǹ Noowa nì u-jipɔmbi boon ní ki bí-bi yii: «Ní màal̀ kí bùtī kí gbí kitiŋ.

ref: All animals, birds, reptiles, and fish will be afraid of you. I have placed them under your control,
text: Kitiŋ pu waŋkuti kɔkɔ, nì kitiŋ pu peelii kɔkɔ, nì inoon kɔkɔ, nì tin kɔkɔ fù kitiŋ puee nì ijɔm kɔkɔ làá nīn fàŋkí-ni ní yɔ́kɔ́ yɔkɔ. Tiwan gbanti kɔkɔee, m dūu ŋá ni-ŋaan.

ref: and I have given them to you for food. From now on, you may eat them, as well as the green plants that you have always eaten.
text: M tīī-ni tin kɔkɔ pée bí ki cáá kufoouee án nīn sá-ni tijin, kii m nín nìn tīī-ni timool tin kɔkɔ lòoǹ ibee puee. M dū kɔkɔ ki tīī-ni án nīn sá ni-jinti.

ref: But life is in the blood, and you must not eat any meat that still has blood in it.
text: Ama ní taa ŋmɔ́ kuwaŋkuluu kun baa lāntì fatiku nyáńee nanti, kun puee kuwaŋkuluu fatikuu di yì ku-maŋfal.

ref: I want you and your descendants to have many children, so people will live everywhere on earth.
text: Ama nimbee, ní màal̀ kí bùtī kí gbí kitiŋ pu.»

ref: Again, God said to Noah and his sons:
text: Ní Unimbɔti gíti bí Noowa nì u-jipɔmbi yii:

ref: I promise every living creature that the earth and those living on it will never again be destroyed by a flood.
text: Dipoondi din m pōoǹ-nee sɔ̀. Maa làá ti yàntī nnyiŋgbeen ń dāá kúntí tiwan tin kɔkɔ cáá kufoouee tiba kitiŋ pu.»

ref: The rainbow that I have put in the sky will be my sign to you and to every living creature on earth. It will remind you that I will keep this promise forever.
text: «M dū m-kuntinyindi ki làl̄ atagban pu án nīn sá kudaaŋŋu yii dipoondi dɔ̄ man nì kitiŋ kansikin.

ref: When I send clouds over the earth, and a rainbow appears in the sky,
text: M yaa láa pée yāntì utaa yíiń ní dikuntinyindi nyáń ki làl̄ atagban puee,

ref: I will remember my promise to you and to all other living creatures. Never again will I let floodwaters destroy all life.
text: m làá dāá téetí dipoondi din dɔ̄ man nì ni nì tin kɔkɔ cáá kufoouee kansikinee pu. Di sá yii nnyiŋgbeen kaa ti bí kí dāá kúntí tin kɔkɔ cáá kufoouee.

ref: When I see the rainbow in the sky, I will always remember the promise that I have made to every living creature.
text: Dikuntinyindee yaa nyáń ki làl̄ atagban pu m wál̀-dèe, ní ḿ téetí dipoondi din dɔ̄ kí nīn cá jaanjaan, man Unimbɔti nì tin kɔkɔ cáá kufoou ki bí kitiŋ puee kansikinee pu.»

ref: The rainbow will be the sign of that solemn promise. Noah and His Family
text: Ní Unimbɔti bí Noowa yii: «Dikuntinyindi di sá dipoondi din dɔ̄ man nì tin kɔkɔ cáá kufoou ki bí kitiŋ puee kansikinee daaŋku.» Noowa nì u-jipɔmbi bɔti

ref: Noah and his sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth, left the boat. Ham later had a son named Canaan.
text: Noowa jipɔmbi bin nìn nyáń meela poonee di sá Seem nì Kaam nì Jaafeeti. Kaam di màl̄ Kanaan.

ref: All people on earth are descendants of Noah's three sons.
text: Noowa jipɔmbi bitee di màl̄ ki gbéeǹ kitiŋ.

ref: Noah farmed the land and was the first to plant a vineyard.
text: Noowa kíĺ tikpaal dikpɔ̀l ní ki cóoǹ fiinyi.

ref: One day he got drunk and was lying naked in his tent.
text: Dalbee ní u nyùn̄ fiinyi subiliŋ daam ki gbí ní ki dɔ̄ u-tantiu poon dicancaŋgbeel.

ref: Ham entered the tent and saw him naked, then went back outside and told his brothers.
text: Kaam un sá Kanaan baayee ká u-baa nín dɔ̄ dicancaŋgbeel puee, ní u nyáń ki tùkū Seem nì Jaafeeti.

ref: Shem and Japheth put a robe over their shoulders and walked backwards into the tent. Without looking at their father, they placed it over his body.
text: Niinee ní Seem nì Jaafeeti kpàlīń tɔb bili, ki dū dibɔkutikpaandi ki páaǹ bi-gbigbipati pu ki túlifìń bi-boon ki cáań ki bīiǹ bi-baa pu. Baa fātì bi-nimbiin kí kpèè bi-baa, ní kaa nín wál̀ u-cooi.

ref: When Noah sobered up and learned what his youngest son had done,
text: Ndaam dāa cēetì Noowa pu u fīntèe ní ki béè u-bijoolkaaki nín ŋá-u miŋyee.

ref: he said, “I now put a curse on Canaan! He will be the lowest slave of his brothers.
text: Niinee ní u bí yii: «Mbusu ń kɔ́ Kanaan. Iyumbu yumbuu ní u làá nīn sá ki tīī u-mambi!»

ref: I ask the Lord my God to bless Shem and make Canaan his slave.
text: Ní ki gíti bí yii: «Tí nyɔ̄ŋkì ti-Dindaan un sá Seem Nimbɔtiuee! Kanaan úǹ ń nīn sá Seem yumbuu!

ref: I pray God will give Japheth more and more land and let him take over the territory of Shem. May Canaan be his slave.”
text: Unimbɔti ń yàntī Jaafeeti ń bùtī kí nīn kóò Seem tantibin, Kanaan úǹ ń nīn sá bi-yumbuu!»

ref: After the flood Shem, Ham, and Japheth had many descendants. The Descendants of Japheth
text: Nnyiŋgbeen boonee, Noowa jipɔmbi Seem nì Kaam nì Jaafeeti màl̄ ki būtì. Bi-maaliŋu biyaamu sɔ̀.

ref: Japheth's descendants had their own languages, tribes, and land. They were Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras. Gomer was the ancestor of Ashkenaz, Riphath, and Togarmah. Javan was the ancestor of Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim, who settled along the coast. The Descendants of Ham
text: Bíǹ maaliŋu gbanti di yàl̄ kitiŋ kɔkɔ pu ki kíǹ ki kóò akiti akiti ki nóó nì dinikitil kamaa sooŋyi nì di-naakooku nì di-tiŋki nì di-timbundi.

ref: This completes the list of Noah's descendants. After the flood their descendants became nations and spread all over the world.
text: Tinaakɔti tin kɔkɔ nyánní Noowa maaliŋunee dee, kunaakoou kamaa nì ku-maaliŋu nì ku-timbuŋŋi. Ki dī bíǹ pu ní atimbun kɔkɔ yàl̄ ki gbí kitiŋ kɔkɔ pu nnyiŋgbeen boon.

ref: they said: Let's build a city with a tower that reaches to the sky! We'll use hard bricks and tar instead of stone and mortar. We'll become famous, and we won't be scattered all over the world.
text: Bi nìn gíti lī yii: «Ní càmaan tí náań kitiŋ, kí mā kudifɔkɔu kubɔ. Tí ŋámaan míǹ kí nyā diyindi kí taa yàl̄ ti-ba ti-ba kitiŋ kɔkɔ pu.»

ref: But when the Lord came down to look at the city and the tower,
text: Ní ti-Dindaan sīntìń kí kpèè kitiŋ nì kudifɔkɔu kun binib bí ki māāyee.

ref: he said: These people are working together because they all speak the same language. This is just the beginning. Soon they will be able to do anything they want.
text: Ní ki bí yii: «Kpèèmaan! Binib nee sá unibaanti ní ki sòoǹ isoombaanti. Bi nín kíĺ ditundi din nee, tiba kpá kí lìkin̄-bi bí gbèen̄ tin bi bóob́ yii bi làá ŋée.

ref: Let's go down and confuse them! We'll make them speak different languages, and they won't be able to understand each other.
text: An puee tí cù kí ŋmàlim̀ bi-sooŋyi fìkil fikil ń kɔ́, bí taa ti nín̄ gbìl̀ tɔb liŋfii.»

ref: So the people had to stop building the city, because the Lord confused their language and scattered them all over the earth. That's how the city of Babel got its name. The Descendants of Shem
text: An pu ní bi pú kitiŋ gbanti diyindi yii Babeel, kun puee niin ní ti-Dindaan kpántì binib sooŋyi bí taa ti nín̄ gbìliǹ tɔb, ní ki yāaǹ binib bi yàl̄ ki gbí kitiŋ kɔkɔ pu. Seem naantiibi bɔti

ref: Serug had more children and died at the age of
text: Naakɔɔr màaltam boonee, Seerugi gíti ŋá abin alaataa ali ní. U nìn gíti màl̄ mbininjabiyaamu nì nnimpubiyaamu ki kútì.

ref: Terah decided to move from Ur to the land of Canaan. He took along Abram and Sarai and his grandson Lot, the son of Haran. But when they came to the city of Haran, they settled there instead.
text: Ní Teeraki yōoǹ u-jipɔɔn Abram nì u-naantiiu Looti, Aran jipɔɔn nì u-coopu Saarayi, Abram nimpuu. Bi-kɔkɔ yíkì Uur, Kaaldee tiŋkin ki nín cá Kanaan tiŋkin. Bi bàn̄ Kaaran tiŋkinee ní ki gbíntì niin.

ref: The Lord said to Abram: Leave your country, your family, and your relatives and go to the land that I will show you.
text: Dalbee ní ti-Dindaan bí Abram yii: «Yīkì a-tiŋki pu, a-tincaliiki yab kansikin nì a-baa cindin kí nīn cá kitiŋ kin poon m làá dàkà-see.

ref: I will bless you and make your descendants into a great nation. You will become famous and be a blessing to others.
text: M làá dī a-pu ní kí náań ditimbuŋkpaandi ní kí bìin̄ a-boon. M làá yòon̄ a-yindi yilpu. A-pu ní m làá dī kí bìin̄ inibool kɔkɔ pu.

ref: I will bless those who bless you, but I will put a curse on anyone who puts a curse on you. Everyone on earth will be blessed because of you.
text: Bin yaa ŋá-si tiŋanee, m làá ŋá bidambi tiŋan, bin yaa tɔ́-si mbusuee, m làá tɔ̄-bi mbusu. A-puee, m làá ŋá kitiŋ pu nibuliŋ kɔkɔ tiŋan.»

ref: Abram went as far as the sacred tree of Moreh in a place called Shechem. The Canaanites were still living in the land at that time,
text: ní bi yìin̄ kitiŋ gbanti poon ki tin bàn̄ laaba bi yíì niin yii Siikeem, ní ki tin nyáń Mooree subu pu. Ŋyunti gbantee, Kanaan yab di nìn dá láá kóò kitiŋ gbantin.

ref: but the Lord appeared to Abram and promised, “I will give this land to your family forever.” Abram then built an altar there for the Lord.
text: Ní ti-Dindaan nyántì u-ba ki dàkā Abram ki lī yii: «A-niboolii ní m làá dāá dū kitiŋ nee kí tīī.» Ní Abram máń nsaraafan niin ki tīī ti-Dindaan un nìn nyántì u-ba ki dàkā-uee.

ref: Later, Abram started out toward the Southern Desert. Abram in Egypt
text: Míǹ boonee ní Abram tí yíkì ki còom̄ ki kàal̀ ntimu ki nìkì ki cá Neegeeb watil pú.

ref: The crops failed, and there was no food anywhere in Canaan. So Abram and his wife Sarai went to live in Egypt for a while. But just before they got there, Abram said, “Sarai, you are really beautiful!
text: Bi tóob́ kí kɔ́ Eejipiti tiŋkinee ní u bí u-nimpuu Saarayi yii: «M nyí yii a baasii ŋān̄ unimpu.

ref: When the Egyptians see you, they will murder me because I am your husband. But they won't kill you.
text: Eejipiti yab yaa ká-see, bi làá lì yii m-nimpuu di sá-si, ní kí cúú-mi, kí kpɔ̀-mi ní kí yàntī sii ń nīn bí.

ref: As soon as Abram and Sarai arrived in Egypt, the Egyptians noticed how beautiful she was.
text: Bi dūu bàn̄ Eejipitee, niin yab ká yii u-nimpuu ŋān̄ tikpil.

ref: The king's officials told him about her, and she was taken to his house.
text: Faaroo kàliŋkpilib mun nìn ká u-nimpuu gbanti ní ki cūtìi máafì Faaroo unimpuee nyaaŋku pu, ki yōoǹ-u ki cáa cūnnì Faaroo cindi.

ref: The king was good to Abram because of Sarai, and Abram was given sheep, cattle, donkeys, slaves, and camels.
text: U-puee, Faaroo fōō Abram tiŋan, ki tīī-u ipii nì inaa nì mmamu nì bitɔntɔnliibi bininjab nì binimpoobi, nì mmansalimu nì igutaamu.

ref: Because of Sarai, the Lord struck the king and everyone in his palace with terrible diseases.
text: Ama ní Abram nimpuu Saarayi puee, ti-Dindaan yāntì iweeŋkpaan iba dɔ́miń ki jéetì Faaroo nì u-cindi yab kɔkɔ pu.

ref: Finally, the king sent for Abram and said to him, “What have you done to me? Why didn't you tell me Sarai was your wife?
text: Niinee ní Faaroo yíiń Abram ní ki bālifì-u yii: «Ba ŋá ní a ŋá-mi mimmee? Ba pu ní saa tùkū-mi yii unimpu nee sá a-nimpuu?

ref: Why did you make me believe she was your sister? Now I've married her. Take her and go! She's your wife.”
text: Ba pu ní a bí yii u sá a-niisal ki yāntì m dū-u ki kpántì m-nimpuu? Too, a-nimpuu sèé! Fōō a-wanti kí kpáàá yīkì.»

ref: So the king told his men to let Abram and Sarai take their possessions and leave.
text: Ní Faaroo pú u-yab kinyɔkɔ yii bí nyāntí u nì u-nimpuu, ú kpáaǹ u-wanti tin u cááyee kɔkɔ nì u-nimpuu kí nīn cá.

ref: Abram and Sarai took everything they owned and went to the Southern Desert. Lot went with them.
text: Niinee ní Abram yíkì Eejipiti tiŋkin ki túĺ Neegeeb watil pú, ki kpáaǹ u-nimpuu nì tin kɔkɔ u nìn pée cááyee, ní Looti nóó-u. Abram nì Looti yākatì tɔbin

ref: Abram was very rich. He owned many cattle, sheep, and goats, and had a lot of silver and gold.
text: Abram nìn sá ugaajakpaan ní. U nìn cáá tiwaŋkuti tikpil ki cáá animbil nì sindaariya.

ref: Abram moved from place to place in the Southern Desert. And finally, he went north and set up his tents between Bethel and Ai,
text: Ní u yíkì Neegeeb ki còom̄ ki kàal̀ ntimu ntimu ki nìkì, ní ki tin bàn̄ Beeteel, mpaan ŋun pu u nìn péé péé ki gāā u-tantiu Beeteel nì Aayi kansikinee.

ref: where he had earlier camped and built an altar. There he worshiped the Lord.
text: Niin ní u nìn máń nsaraafan mpeekaam. Niin ní u jáam̀ ti-Dindaan.

ref: Lot, who was traveling with him, also had sheep, goats, and cattle, as well as his own family and slaves.
text: Looti un nìn dī-uee mun nìn cáá u-ba nì u-piii nì u-ŋoobui nì u-naai, ní ki cáá u-ba nì u-tantiu ki kóò.

ref: Abram said to Lot, “We are close relatives. We shouldn't argue, and our men shouldn't be fighting one another.
text: Niinee ní Abram bí Looti yii: «Kujaau kaa máaǹ kí nīn bí man nì sii kansikin, kaa máaǹ kí nīn bí man pikpaaliibi nì sii yab kansikin, kun puee ti pēē mmaal ní.

ref: There is plenty of land for you to choose from. Let's separate. If you go north, I'll go south; if you go south, I'll go north.”
text: Kpèè, kitiŋ di kaa dɔ̄ a-nimbiin pàaa neeee? Tí yàkatī tɔbin. Á lēē laakin pú a lèe kí nīn cá. Sii yaa lēē ŋgan púɔɔɔ, man ń túĺ ŋgiil pú. Sii yaa tí lēē ŋgiil púɔɔɔ, man ń túĺ ŋgan pú.»

ref: This happened before the Lord had destroyed the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah. And when Lot looked around, he saw there was plenty of water in the Jordan Valley. All the way to Zoar the valley was as green as the garden of the Lord or the land of Egypt.
text: Niinee ní Looti fātìi kpēē kitiŋ kin dɔ̄ Jɔɔdan buŋŋu watil púee ki ká timool nín pée sɔ̀mìń niin pú fílfil ki tin sāā Soowaar tiŋkee. An nìn sɔ̀m̀ kii ti-Dindaan kpaabuee, ki nìn sɔ̀m̀ kii Eejipiti tiŋkee. An yuntiŋuee, ti-Dindaan kaa nìn dá láá kúntì Soodoom nì Goomoora timmu.

ref: So Lot chose the whole Jordan Valley for himself, and as he started out toward the east, he and Abram separated.
text: Niinee ní Looti lēē kitiŋ kin dɔ̄ Jɔɔdan buŋŋu watil púee kɔkɔ, ní ki búntì ŋwìiń nyalaŋki pú. Bi-yàkatitam nín jítì miŋyee dee.

ref: Abram stayed in the land of Canaan. But Lot settled near the cities of the valley and put up his tents not far from Sodom,
text: Di sá Abram gbíntì Kanaan tiŋkin, ní Looti úǹ cūtì ki kɔ́ ntimu mun bí Jɔɔdan tantakalinee, ní ki gāā u-tantib Soodoom tiŋki caŋin.

ref: where the people were evil and sinned terribly against the Lord. Abram Moves to Hebron
text: Ama Soodoom tiŋki gbanti nib nìn sá binikpitib ní ki nìn ŋáań tin kaa pée ŋān̄ ti-Dindaan ceeyee.

ref: After Abram and Lot had gone their separate ways, the Lord said to Abram: Look around to the north, south, east, and west.
text: Looti nì Abram nín yākatì tɔbin Looti búntèe, ní ti-Dindaan bí Abram yii: «Laakin a yú nee, fàtī kí kpèè ŋgiil pú nì ŋgan pú, kí kpèè ŋwìiń nyalaŋki pú nì ŋwìiń jeetilaŋki pú.

ref: I will give you and your family all the land you can see. It will be theirs forever!
text: Kitiŋ kin kɔkɔ a wāĺ nee, m làá dū-kì kí tīī sii nì a-niboolii ní, ní nīn yì kí nīn pée cáa cá jaanjaan.

ref: I will give you more descendants than there are specks of dust on the earth, and someday it will be easier to count those specks of dust than to count your descendants.
text: M làá yàntī a-niboolii ń bùtī kii dibulee ní. Un yaa láa ŋúń ki kàan̄ dibulee di dāa ŋūǹ kí kàaǹ a-niboolii.

ref: Now walk back and forth across the land, because I am going to give it to you.
text: Yīkì kí còom̀ kí màntīí tū kitiŋ nee, kun puee sii ní m làá dū-kì kí tīī.»

ref: Abram took down his tents and went to live near the sacred trees of Mamre at Hebron, where he built an altar in honor of the Lord.
text: Ní Abram yíkì niin ki cūtìi gāā u-tantib Mamiree sufii in bí Eebrɔn tiŋki caŋinee ki kóò, ní ki máń ti-Dindaan nsaraafan niin.

ref: About this time, King Amraphel of Babylonia, King Arioch of Ellasar, King Chedorlaomer of Elam, and King Tidal of Goiim
text: Ŋyunti gbantee Amiirafeel nìn sá Siinaar bɔtiu, ní Ariyɔɔki sá Eelasaar bɔtiu, ní Keedoolawoomeer sá Eelam bɔtiu, ní Tiidaal sá Gooyiim bɔtiu.

ref: attacked King Bera of Sodom, King Birsha of Gomorrah, King Shinab of Admah, King Shemeber of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela, a city also known as Zoar.
text: Bibɔtiibi gbanti nìn kpáfì ki cūtìi jáń nì Beera, Soodoom bɔtiu, nì Biirisa, Goomoora bɔtiu, nì Siinaab, Adima bɔtiu, nì Seemeebeer, Seebooyiim bɔtiu, nì Beela tiŋki kin bi gíti yíì Soowaaree mun bɔtiu.

ref: King Chedorlaomer and his allies had ruled these last five kings for twelve years, but in the thirteenth year the kings rebelled and joined forces in Siddim Valley, which is now covered by the southern part of the Dead Sea.
text: Bi nìn bí ubɔti Keedoolawoomeer taapu ní ki tin sāā abin saalaa nì ali. Abin saalaa nì ata poonee ní bi yíkì kaa kíí bi-ba pu.

ref: A year later King Chedorlaomer and his allies attacked and defeated the Rephaites in Ashteroth-Karnaim, the Zuzites in Ham, and the Emites in Shaveh-Kiriathaim.
text: Dibindi diba kpá saalku poon ní ubɔti Keedoolawoomeer nì bibɔtiibi bin u nì bi pēeǹ kinyɔkɔee jáń ki pɔ́ɔkiǹ Reefa yab Asitarooti-Kaanayiim tiŋkin, ki jáń ki pɔ́ɔkiǹ Suusi yab Aami tiŋkin ní ki jáń ki pɔ́ɔkiǹ Eem yab Saafee Kiiriyatayiim tiŋkin.

ref: They also defeated the Horites in the hill country of Edom, as far as El-Paran, near the desert.
text: Bi nìn jáń ki pɔ́ɔkiǹ Kɔɔr yab bin kóò Seeyiir jooŋŋi puee mun, ki jìn̄-bi ki bàn̄ Eel-Paraan, kitiŋ kin pìlīǹ kuteeuee.

ref: They went back to the city of Enmishpat, better known as Kadesh. Then they captured all the land that belonged to the Amalekites, and they defeated the Amorites who were living in Hazazon-Tamar.
text: Míǹ boonee ní bi pɔ́ĺ ki búntì Eeni Miisipati, kitiŋ kin bi yíì kɔŋkɔnnee Kadeesee. Niin ní bi jáń ki pɔ́ɔkiǹ Amaleeki yab bi-timmu kɔkɔ poon, ki kpáaǹ Amɔɔr yab bin kóò Asaasɔn-Taamaar tiŋkinee.

ref: At Siddim Valley, the armies of the kings of Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Bela fought the armies of King Chedorlaomer of Elam, King Tidal of Goiim, King Amraphel of Babylonia, and King Arioch of Ellasar. The valley
text: kí já nì Keedoolawoomeer, Eelam bɔtiu nì Tiidaal, Gooyiim bɔtiu nì Amiirafeel, Siinaar bɔtiu nì Ariyɔɔki, Eelasaar bɔtiu. An nìn sá bibɔtiibi binaa di nìn kpáfì kí já nì bibɔtiibi biŋmoo.

ref: was full of tar pits, and when the troops from Sodom and Gomorrah started running away, some of them fell into the pits. Others escaped to the hill country.
text: Siidiim tantakal gbanti nìn sá aŋɔ lɔkɔgiŋŋi ŋma ní. Kujaau pɔ́ɔkì Soodoom nì Goomoora bɔtiibi bíi sáńee, ní ki tin jéetì alɔkɔginee poon, ní bin kíǹee sáǹ ki dàb̄ dijool.

ref: Their enemies took everything of value from Sodom and Gomorrah, including their food supplies.
text: Bin nìn jáń ki pɔ́ɔkiǹ-bee dɔ́miń ki kóoǹ Soodoom nì Goomoora timmu yab wanti kɔkɔ nì bi-jinti kɔkɔ ki kpáàa búntì.

ref: They also captured Abram's nephew Lot, who lived in Sodom. They took him and his possessions and then left.
text: Bi nìn cúú Looti, Abram nabiki jipɔɔn ki kpáaǹ u-wanti kɔkɔ ki cáa búntì, kun puee Looti nìn kóò Soodoom ní.

ref: At this time Abram the Hebrew was living near the oaks that belonged to Mamre the Amorite. Mamre and his brothers Eshcol and Aner were Abram's friends. Someone who had escaped from the battle told Abram
text: Kijapaai kiba nìn sáǹ ki ŋmátì kujaau gbantin ní ki dɔ́miń Abram, ueebruja cee ki tùkū-u yii binannanliibi cúú Looti ki cáa búntì kuyuŋu. An yuntiŋuee, Abram nìn dáá kóò ki pìlīǹ Mamiree, uamɔɔrja sufii ní. Mamiree gbanti nì u-nabiyaamu Eeskool nì Aneer nìn kpáfì nì Abram bi sòon̄ ki ŋá ubaanti.

ref: that his nephew Lot had been taken away. Three hundred and eighteen of Abram's servants were fighting men, so he took them and followed the enemy as far north as the city of Dan.
text: Abram nín gbìl̄ yii bi cúú u-nabiki ki kpáàa búntì kuyuŋuee, ní u bɔ́ntì u-tɔntɔnliibi bin sá bibaatandambi bi màl̄-bi u-cindinee, binib alaataa ata nì saalku nì bita, ní bi kùl̄ ki bīiǹ bibɔtiibee nì bi-janjaliibi boon ki tin bàn̄ kitiŋ kin bi yíì yii Daanee.

ref: That night, Abram divided up his troops, attacked from all sides, and won a great victory. But some of the enemy escaped to the town of Hobah north of Damascus,
text: Kunyeeuee ní u nì u-naacimbee cúu yākatì akiti akiti ní ki lùkū ki tú bi-nannanliibi. Kujaau kíĺ, ní Abram nì u-yab jáń ki pɔ́ɔkiǹ-bi ní ki jìn̄-bi ki tin bàn̄ Ooba tiŋki kin bí Damaasi tiŋki ŋgan púee.

ref: and Abram went after them. He brought back his nephew Lot, together with Lot's possessions and the women and everyone else who had been captured. Abram Is Blessed by Melchizedek
text: Ní Abram fōoń tin kɔkɔ bi nìn dɔ́miń ki kóoǹee, ki fōō ki kpáaǹní u-nabiki Looti nì u-wanti kɔkɔ, nì binimpoobi nì binib bin bi nìn pée cúúyee kɔkɔ.

ref: Abram returned after he had defeated King Chedorlaomer and the other kings. Then the king of Sodom went to meet Abram in Shaveh Valley, which is also known as King's Valley.
text: Abram nín nìn jáń ki pɔ́ɔkiǹ Keedoolawoomeer nì bibɔtiibi bin nìn dɔ́miń ki kútì u-puee ki fātìi kùn̄ee ní Soodoom bɔtiu túŋkìń-u Saafee tantakalin, an dàkà yii ubɔti tantakalin.

ref: King Melchizedek of Salem was a priest of God Most High. He brought out some bread and wine
text: Meelkiiseedeeki un sá Saleem bɔtiuee mun nìn dɔ́miń niin. U nìn sá Yilpu Fɔ́ŋŋŋ Nimbɔtiu saraaŋaliu ní. Meelkiiseedeeki gbanti nìn cáań kpɔnɔ nì ndamam ki tīī Abram.

ref: and said to Abram: “I bless you in the name of God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth.
text: Meelkiiseedeeki nìn mēē ki bīiǹ u-pu ki lī yii: «Yilpu fɔ́ŋŋŋ Nimbɔtiu un náań yilpu nì taapuee ń bìin̄ Abram boon.

ref: All praise belongs to God Most High for helping you defeat your enemies.” Then Abram gave Melchizedek a tenth of everything.
text: Yilpu fɔ́ŋŋŋ Nimbɔtiu un dū a-nannanliibi ki ŋá a-ŋaan a jáń ki pɔ́ɔkiǹ-bee ń ká tinyool!» Niinee ní Abram lēē tiwan tin kɔkɔ u nìn jáń ki fātìi fōońee, nì tin kɔkɔ u cááyee saalaa kamaa puee kubɔ kubɔ ki tīī Meelkiiseedeeki.

ref: The king of Sodom said to Abram, “All I want are my people. You can keep everything else.”
text: Ní Soodoom bɔtiu bí Abram yii: «Sii yòon̄ tiwan tin a jáń ki fōōyee kɔkɔ kí dū binib kí tīī man.»

ref: Abram answered: The Lord God Most High made the heavens and the earth. And I have promised him
text: Ní Abram kíí-u yii: «M yōoǹ m-ŋal ki pūtì ti-Dindaan, Yilpu Fɔ́ŋŋŋ Nimbɔtiu un náań yilpu nì taapuee

ref: that I won't keep anything of yours, not even a sandal strap or a piece of thread. Then you can never say that you are the one who made me rich.
text: yii maa làá pée gbēē a-wanti báà tiba. Báà gulfa kpaŋkpan gba, maa làá dáb́, ki dāa nín nyàab̀ inaataaŋmii. M pūtì yii m kpá kí fōō a-cee tiba nì tiba, á dāá gāā a-bindi kí lì yii: <Man di yì-tì ní Abram kpántì ugaaja!>

ref: Let my share be the food that my men have eaten. But Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre went with me, so give them their share of what we brought back.
text: Ubɔ ń taa pée tīī-mi tiba. Tijin tin m-naacimbi jíńee ŋmāntì, ama Aneer nì Eeskool nì Mamiree bin céeǹ-mee, bíǹ ń yòon̄ bi-yati.»

ref: Later the Lord spoke to Abram in a vision, “Abram, don't be afraid! I will protect you and reward you greatly.”
text: Míǹ jítì boonee, ní ti-Dindaan lēē u-ba ki dàkā Abram ki bí-u yii: «Abram, taa fàŋkì tiba, man di wúń a-pu. Tiŋan tin m làá ŋá-see làá nīn wīikí tikpil.»

ref: But Abram answered, “Lord All-Powerful, you have given me everything I could ask for, except children. And when I die, Eliezer of Damascus will get all I own.
text: Ní Abram kíí yii: «Ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti, ba ní a làá tīī-mi ní? Man dee, maa cáá kibiki, ní Eeliyeeseer udamaasja di làá dāá jī m-fakal.»

ref: You have not given me any children, and this servant of mine will inherit everything.”
text: Ní Abram gíti bí yii: «Man dee, aa tīī-mi kibiki, ní uyumbu un bi màl̄ m-cindinee di làá jī m-fakal.»

ref: The Lord replied, “No, he won't! You will have a son of your own, and everything you have will be his.”
text: Niinee ní ti-Dindaan bí-u yii: «An kaa sá Eeliyeeseer di làá jī a-fakal, sisi a-ba poon biki di làá jī a-fakal.»

ref: Then the Lord took Abram outside and said, “Look at the sky and see if you can count the stars. That's how many descendants you will have.”
text: Ti-Dindaan lī-u mimmee ní ki nyántì-u mmɔŋki, ní ki bí-u yii: «Wáaǹ kí kpèè yilpu a yaa ŋūǹ kí kàaǹ iŋmalbijaa nee.» Ní ki kútì-u yii: «Míǹ ní a-biyaamu làá dāá nīn wīikí.»

ref: Abram believed the Lord, so the Lord was pleased with him and accepted him. The Lord Makes Another Promise to Abram
text: Niinee ní Abram fōō ti-Dindaan nín lī puee ki kíí. Míǹ pu ní ti-Dindaan yíiń-u deedeedaan.

ref: The Lord said to Abram, “I brought you here from Ur in Chaldea, and I gave you this land.”
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí-u yii: «Man di sá a-Dindaan un nyántìń-si Uur, Kaaldee tiŋkin kí dū kitiŋ kin poon a bí nee kí tīī-si á nīn kíǹ ki yèe.»

ref: Abram asked, “Lord God, how can I know the land will be mine?”
text: Ní Abram bí yii: «Ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti, m làá ŋá mana kí béé yii man di làá nīn lafun yì kitiŋ gbanti?»

ref: Then the Lord told him, “Bring me a three-year-old cow, a three-year-old female goat, a three-year-old ram, a dove, and a young pigeon.”
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí-u yii: «Cúú unasal un cáá abin atee nì uŋɔsal un cáá abin atee, nì upìja un cáá abin atee, nì kinanjilii nì dibeenanjipɔmbil kí cáań.»

ref: Abram obeyed the Lord. Then he cut the animals in half and laid the two halves of each animal opposite each other on the ground. But he did not cut the doves and pigeons in half.
text: Ní Abram nyàabìń an waŋkutee ní ki cúú-tì ki gífì ki nābitì mili mili, ní ki dū aginee ki bíl̀ ki túĺ tɔb. See kinanjilii nì dibeenanjipɔmbil ní waa gífì ki nābitì mili.

ref: And when birds came down to eat the animals, Abram chased them away.
text: Ní ikaabu kpákatìń kí jī an nanti ní Abram jìn̄-i.

ref: As the sun was setting, Abram fell into a deep sleep, and everything became dark and frightening.
text: Ŋwìiń cá kí jēetée ní ŋgeem miba dɔ́miń ki cúú Abram. Niinee ní tifaandi kɔ́-u ní dibɔmbɔŋkpaandi diba jéetì u-pu.

ref: Then the Lord said: Abram, you will live to an old age and die in peace. But I solemnly promise that your descendants will live as foreigners in a land that doesn't belong to them. They will be forced into slavery and abused for
text: Siiyee, a làá bà a-naanjab nì nsɔŋfi poon ní. A làá púĺ kí bà titikpiti laŋki nì dipɔɔsɔŋkil ní bí nín síb́-si.

ref: Four generations later, your descendants will return here and take this land, because only then will the people who live here be so sinful that they deserve to be punished.
text: A-naantitɔntɔkɔŋ dinanaatiil di làá dāá fàtīí gítiń doo, di sá yii kí dāá sāā ŋyunti gbanti ní Amɔɔr yab kpitii làá wíiǹ kí bà i-nyɔkɔm.»

ref: Sometime after sunset, when it was very dark, a smoking cooking pot and a flaming fire passed between the two halves of each animal.
text: Ŋwìiń jéetì kunyeeu mɔ́ee ní ŋfan ŋubɔ nì ŋŋminyiim nì ŋŋminaapool dɔ́miń ki jítì tinan tin Abram gífì ki bíl̀ee kansikin.

ref: At that time the Lord made an agreement with Abram and told him: I will give your descendants the land east of the Shihor River on the border of Egypt as far as the Euphrates River.
text: Ŋwiin gbanti dal ní ti-Dindaan pūtì ki pōoǹ Abram ki bí-u yii: «A-naantiibi ní m làá dū kitiŋ nee kí tīī, kí yòon̄ Eejipiti mɔɔl kí tin cáá sāā Eefiraati mɔɔkpaandi.

ref: They will possess the land of the Kenites, the Kenizzites, the Kadmonites,
text: An dàkà yii kitiŋ kin pu Keeni yab nì Keeniisi yab nì Kadimoon yab

ref: the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Rephaites,
text: nì Eeti yab nì Peeriisi yab nì Reefa yab

ref: the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Girgashites, and the Jebusites.
text: nì Amɔɔr yab nì Kanaan yab nì Giirgaasi yab nì Jeebusi yab kóòyee.»

ref: Abram's wife Sarai had not been able to have any children. But she owned a young Egyptian slave woman named Hagar,
text: Abram nimpuu Saarayi kaa nìn màl̄ kibiki. U nìn cáá uyumbusapɔɔn ubɔ, u sá Eejipiti tiŋkin bi yíì-u yii Agaar.

ref: Later, when Hagar knew she was going to have a baby, she became proud and treated Sarai hatefully.
text: Ní Abram nì Agaar kpáfì, ní u cáá dipool. Agaar nín ká yii u cáá dipoolee ní waa ti dū u-na Saarayi ki yíiń tiba.

ref: Then Sarai said to Abram, “It's all your fault! I gave you my slave woman, but she has been hateful to me ever since she found out she was pregnant. You have done me wrong, and you will have to answer to the Lord for this.”
text: Ní Saarayi bí Abram yii: «Sii di yì-tì ní bi sìí-mi nee. Man di dū m-yumbusapɔɔŋwu ki tīī-si, ama u nín ká yii u kpántì upɔdaanee ní man kaa ti sá tiba u-cee. Ti-Dindaan di làá sòoǹ man nì sii bɔti!»

ref: Abram said, “All right! She's your slave—do whatever you want with her.” Then Sarai began treating Hagar so harshly that she finally ran away.
text: Ní Abram bí-u yii: «A-yumbuu dee, a cáá u-pu mpɔn. Ŋá-u min di fáatì-see.» Niinee ní Saarayi kíĺ ki bíi sàantí Agaar ní úǹ dāa sáǹ ki wāatì-u.

ref: Hagar stopped to rest at a spring in the desert on the road to Shur. While she was there, the angel of the Lord came to her
text: Ní ti-Dindaan tuuŋŋu tin ká-u u kā nnyimbul ŋubɔ caŋin kuteeun. Nnyimbulee nìn pìlīǹ Suur dandi ní.

ref: and asked, “Hagar, where have you come from, and where are you going?” She answered, “I'm running away from Sarai, my owner.”
text: Ní ntuunee bí-u yii: «Agaar, Saarayi yumbusapɔɔŋwu, a nyánní la ní ki kùǹ la»? Ní u kíí-ŋu yii: «M sáń m-na Saarayi ní».

ref: The angel said, “Go back to Sarai and be her slave.
text: Ní ti-Dindaan tuuŋŋuee bí-u yii: «Gítí a-na gbanti cee. A yaa bàn̄ee, á sìntī a-ba taapu kí tīī-u».

ref: I will give you a son, who will be called Ishmael, because I have heard your cry for help. And someday I will give you so many descendants that no one will be able to count them all.
text: Ní ki gíti tí bí-u yii: «Kpèè, dipool din a cáá nee, a làá dāá màal̀ kibininjabiki ní. Á dāá pɔ̄-kì diyindi yii Isimaayeel, kun puee ti-Dindaan ká a-falaau, ki gbìl̄ a-timinsii.

ref: But your son will live far from his relatives; he will be like a wild donkey, fighting everyone, and everyone fighting him.”
text: A-biki gbanti làá dāá nīn pɔ̄ɔ̄ kii kumooun maŋkee ní. U làá dāá nīn jáà nì báà ŋma, ní báà ŋma mun làá dāá nīn jáà nì u. U làá dāá nīn bí u-baba, kaa làá nīn bí u-nabiyaamu kansikin.»

ref: Hagar thought, “Have I really seen God and lived to tell about it?” So from then on she called him, “The God Who Sees Me.”
text: Ní Agaar yíiń ti-Dindaan un sòon̄ nì uee yii: «Ata Eel-Rooyi», taapu di sá yii <A sá Unimbɔti un wāĺ-mee.> Di sá u lī u-ban yii: «Unimbɔti un wāĺ-mee ní m lafun ká nee.»

ref: That's why people call the well between Kadesh and Bered, “The Well of the Living One Who Sees Me.”
text: An pu ní bi yíì lɔkɔee yii: «Beer Lakayi Rooyi», taapu di sá yii <Un bí dimaŋfalin ki wāĺ-mee lɔkɔu.> Lɔkɔ gbanti dɔ̄ Kadeesi nì Beereedi timmu kansikin.

ref: I will keep my solemn promise to you and give you more descendants than can be counted.”
text: M làá lafun ŋá tin m pōoǹ yii m làá ŋá kí tīī-see, kí bùtī a-maaliŋu tikpil tikpil.»

ref: Abram bowed with his face to the ground, and God said:
text: Ní Abram tātìi dōoǹ dicincibil, ní Unimbɔti bí-u yii:

ref: I promise that you will be the father of many nations. So now I'm changing your name from Abram to Abraham.
text: Baa làá nīn gíti yíì-si Abram. A-yindi di làá nīn kíǹ ki sá Abraam, kun puee m dū-si ki kpántì atimbun kupaau baa.

ref: I will give you a lot of descendants, and they will become great nations. Some of them will even be kings.
text: M làá bùtī a-maaliŋu tikpil tikpil. A-maaliŋu gbanti poon ní m làá yàntī atimbun nì bibɔtiibi ń nyā.

ref: I will always keep the promise I have made to you and your descendants, because I am your God and their God.
text: Dipoondi din m pōoǹ-si nee, dì sá ki tīī sii nì a-maaliŋu kɔkɔ ní kí nīn cáa cá jaanjaan, dì kpá kí gbíntí. Di-pu ní m làá nīn sá a-Nimbɔtiu nì a-naantitɔntɔkɔŋ Nimbɔtiu.

ref: I will give you and them the land in which you are now a foreigner. I will give the whole land of Canaan to your family forever, and I will be their God.
text: M làá dū Kanaan tiŋki kin poon a bí kunicaŋu nee kɔkɔ kí tīī sii nì a-naantiibi bin làá páanní a-boonee, kí nīn cá jaanjaan. Ní man di làá nīn pée sá bi-Nimbɔtiu.»

ref: Abraham, you and all future members of your family must promise to obey me.
text: Ní Unimbɔti nìkī ki bí Abraam yii: «Siiyee, á nīn ŋúb́ m-poondi, kí nīn ŋáań min m lèe, sii nì a-naantiibi bin dáá bíń boon ki dòońee kɔkɔ.

ref: As the sign that you are keeping this promise, you must circumcise every man and boy in your family.
text: Digiil gbanti di sá dipoondi din m pōoǹ-nee daaŋku man nì ni kansikin.

ref: Any man who isn't circumcised hasn't kept his part of the promise and cannot be one of my people.
text: Uninja un kaa gíí dikpancandee, ní nyāntí-u ni-nibul kansikin, kun puee udaan kpītì m-poondi.»

ref: Abraham, from now on your wife's name will be Sarah instead of Sarai.
text: Ní Unimbɔti gíti bí Abraam yii: «Á taa ti yíiń a-nimpuu yii Saarayi, á nīn kíǹ ki yíì-u yii Saara.

ref: I will bless her, and you will have a son by her. She will become the mother of nations, and some of her descendants will even be kings.
text: M làá bìin̄ u-boon kí yàntī ú màal̀-si kibininjabiki, kí bìin̄ u-boon ú kpántí anibul kupaau na. U-maaliŋu poon ní atimbun tikpil bɔtiibi làá nyā.»

ref: Then he asked God, “Why not let Ishmael inherit what you have promised me?”
text: Ní Abraam bí Unimbɔti yii: «Too, á pɔ̄ Isimaayeel mmaŋfafɔkɔn!»

ref: But God answered: No! You and Sarah will have a son. His name will be Isaac, and I will make an everlasting promise to him and his descendants.
text: Ní Unimbɔti bí-u yii: «M gbìl̄, ama an kaa sá Isimaayeel bɔti ní m bíi sòoǹ, an sá a-nimpuu Saara di làá màal̀-si kibininjabiki. Á dāá pɔ̄-kì diyindi yii Isaaki. Úǹ nì u-naantiibi bin làá páanní boonee di làá fōō dipoondi din m pōoǹ dí nīn bí kí nīn cá jaanjaanee.

ref: However, I have heard what you asked me to do for Ishmael, and so I will also bless him with many descendants. He will be the father of twelve princes, and I will make his family a great nation.
text: A nín mēē-mi Isimaayeel pu miŋyee, m gbìl̄. M làá bìin̄ u-boon kí yàntī ú màal̀ ki bùtī tikpil tikpil. U làá dāá màal̀ bibɔtiibi saalaa nì bili, ní u-maaliŋu ń dāá nīn sá ditimbuŋkpaandi.

ref: But your son Isaac will be born about this time next year, and the promise I am making to you and your family will be for him and his descendants forever.
text: Ama an yaa kíǹ m-poondee, dì sá ki tīī Isaaki un Saara làá dāá màal̀ jiil naŋkɔnampuee ní.»

ref: God finished speaking to Abraham and then left.
text: Unimbɔti sòon̄ nì Abraam ki dóòyee, ní ki yíkì u-cee ki nín jɔ́ḿ.

ref: On that same day Abraham obeyed God by circumcising Ishmael. Abraham was also circumcised, and so were all the other men and boys in his household, including his servants and slaves. He was
text: Ŋwimbaanti gbanti ní u gíí ki kpáaǹ bin kɔkɔ pée sá bininjab ki bí u-cindinee, iyumbu nín màl̄ biŋyee nì bin u dáal̀ binicambi ceeyee.

ref: One hot summer afternoon while Abraham was sitting by the entrance to his tent near the sacred trees of Mamre, the Lord appeared to him.
text: Dalba kuwiŋkaŋu puee, Abraam nyáń ki kā u-tantiu kasarkau taapu, Mamiree sukpaaŋyi caŋin, ní ti-Dindaan nyántì u-ba ki dàkā-u niin.

ref: Abraham looked up and saw three men standing nearby. He quickly ran to meet them, bowed with his face to the ground,
text: U dāa yáatì ki kpēē nyɔkɔpuee, bininjab bita di yú kaa dátiǹ-uee. U ká-bee ní ki yíkì u-tantiu nyɔkɔpu ki sáǹ ki túŋkì-bi. U bàn̄ bi-ceeyee, ní ki gbāaǹ ki jáam̀-bi.

ref: and said, “Please come to my home where I can serve you.
text: Ní ki bí bi-kansikin ubɔ yii: «M-dindaan, m-bɔti yaa fáatì-see, á taa càatī man a-tɔntɔnliu pu míǹ.

ref: I'll have some water brought, so you can wash your feet, then you can rest under a tree.
text: Ní yàntī bí cáań nnyim ní fíntí ni-taaŋi ŋkpɔŋkpɔm, ní kí kàl̄ busubu taapu yéè kí ŋùŋfì.

ref: Let me get you some food to give you strength before you leave. I would be honored to serve you.” “Thank you very much,” they answered. “We accept your offer.”
text: M làá cù kí nyàabìń tijin ní tíkiń ni-fam kí nín nìkī ni-saŋŋu. Ni nín dīń man ni-tɔntɔnliu cee puee, ní yàntī ḿ ŋá míǹ kí tīī-ni.» Ní bi kíí-u yii: «Ŋá a nín lī puee.»

ref: Abraham went quickly to his tent and said to Sarah, “Hurry! Get a large sack of flour and make some bread.”
text: Ní Abraam tóotì ki nín kóò u-tantiun u-nimpuu Saara cee, ní ki bí-u yii: «Malaa! Yáfí ŋyimmɔntiim kí búuń kí tɔ̄ kalaa!»

ref: After saying this, he rushed off to his herd of cattle and picked out one of the best calves, which his servant quickly prepared.
text: Abraam tùkū u-nimpuu mimmee, ní ki sáǹ ki cūtìi kɔ́ u-nacaŋkpuŋkun, ki cúú dinajapɔmbil din bí màlmalee ki tīī u-tɔntɔnliu ní úǹ kūū-dì ki ŋɔ́fì malaa malaa.

ref: He then served his guests some yogurt and milk together with the meat. While they were eating, he stood near them under the tree,
text: Ní Abraam dū waagaasii nì nnaabiim ki kútì tinan tin bi ŋɔ́fèe pu ki cáa yóoǹ-bi, ní ki yú u-ba bi-caŋin busubu taapu mboonni, ní bíǹ kā ki jīǹ.

ref: and they asked, “Where's your wife Sarah?” “She is right there in the tent,” Abraham answered.
text: Niinee ní bi bālifì Abraam yii: «A-nimpuu Saara bí la?» Ní u bí-bi yii: «U bí tanti poon».

ref: Abraham and Sarah were very old, and Sarah was well past the age for having children.
text: Abraam nì u-nimpuu nìn púĺ tikpil, u-nimpuu nìn jítì mmaal pu.

ref: So she laughed and said to herself, “Now that I am worn out and my husband is old, will I really know such happiness?”
text: An puee Saara nìn láń u-neen, ní ki lī u-ban yii: «Man nín púĺ min uninja neeliŋu kaa ti cútí-mi nee! M-cal mun sɔ̀ ki púĺ!»

ref: The Lord asked Abraham, “Why did Sarah laugh? Does she doubt that she can have a child in her old age?
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí Abraam yii: «Ba pu ní Saara láń ki bí yii an kaa sá tiŋman, úǹ púĺ waa ti ŋūǹ kí màal̀ kibiki?

ref: I am the Lord! There is nothing too difficult for me. I'll come back next year at the time I promised, and Sarah will already have a son.”
text: Tiba bí ki pɔ̄ɔ̄ nì Unimbɔtaaa? Jiil, ŋyunti ŋun m yóoǹee yaa láa bāańee, m làá gítiń a-cee. Ŋyunti gbantee a-nimpuu Saara dāa màl̄-si kibininjabiki dee.»

ref: Sarah was so frightened that she lied and said, “I didn't laugh.” “Yes, you did!” he answered. Abraham Prays for Sodom
text: Niinee Saara fāŋkì ní ki néè yii: «Maa láń». Ní u bí-u yii: «A mɔ́ntí, a láń».

ref: When the three men got ready to leave, they looked down toward Sodom, and Abraham walked part of the way with them.
text: Bininjabee yíkèe, Abraam bíi cēentí-bi ní bi túĺ Soodoom watil pú.

ref: The Lord said to himself, “I should tell Abraham what I am going to do,
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí yii: «Maa máaǹ kí báĺ Abraam tin m là kí ŋée!

ref: since his family will become a great and powerful nation that will be a blessing to all the other nations on earth.
text: Di sá Abraam làá dāá nīn sá ditimbuŋkpaandi din cáá mpɔnee baa ní. Úǹ pu ní m làá dāá dī kí bìin̄ atimbun ŋin kɔkɔ bí kitiŋ puee boon.

ref: I have chosen him to teach his family to obey me forever and to do what is right and fair. If they do, I will give Abraham many descendants, just as I promised.”
text: Kun puee m lēē-u ú dàkà u-biyaamu nì u-dicaŋku, u-kpátam boonee, bí nīn nóó m-saŋŋu kí nīn còom̄ deedee. Bi yaa ŋáań mimmee, m làá ŋá tin kɔkɔ m pōoǹ Abraamee kí tīī-bi.»

ref: ?” “Because of them, I won't destroy the city,” was the Lord's answer.
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí Abraam yii: «Bi nín sòoǹ Soodoom nì Goomoora yab kpitii pu miŋyee wīikí tikpil, bi-kpitii nyāŋkì.

ref: Now I am going down to see for myself if those people really are that bad. If they aren't, I would like to know.”
text: Man m-ba làá sìntī kí kpèè bi yaa lafun ŋáań tin bi sòon̄ m gbìl̄ee, ḿ béé, an yaa kaa tí sá mimmaaa, ḿ béé.»

ref: The men turned and started toward Sodom. But the Lord stayed with Abraham.
text: Niinee ní bininjabee bili yíkì niin ki búntì Soodoom watil pú, ní Abraam gbíntì ki yú ti-Dindaan cee.

ref: And Abraham asked him, “Lord, when you destroy the evil people, are you also going to destroy those who are good?
text: Ní Abraam sútì ki pìl̄ ti-Dindaan ní ki bí-u yii: «Mimmee, a làá kúntí binikpitib kí kpáaǹ deedeedambaaa?

ref: You surely wouldn't let them be killed when you destroy the evil ones. You are the judge of all the earth, and you do what is right.”
text: Saa kíikí kí ŋá míǹ kpataaa pu, kí kúntí unikpiti kí kpáaǹ deedeedaan. Sii un sá kitiŋ kɔkɔ bɔsoonliuee, aa làá sòoǹ báà ŋma bɔti deedeeee?»

ref: Abraham answered, “I am nothing more than the dust of the earth. Please forgive me, Lord, for daring to speak to you like this.
text: Ní Abraam bí yii: «Kpèè, man un sá ntampukum ki sá ŋfantamee mun di pée yú ki sòoǹ nì m-Dindaan nee.

ref: Then Abraham said, “I don't have any right to ask you, Lord, but what would you do if you find only
text: Ní Abraam gíti bí yii: «M làá tí màkan̄ kí sòoǹ nì m-Dindaan. An yaa yíì deedeedambi mmuŋku di bí niin.» Ní ti-Dindaan bí yii: «Bi yaa sá binib mmuŋku, maa làá kúntí-kì binib mmuŋkuee pu.»

ref: After speaking with Abraham, the Lord left, and Abraham went back home.
text: Ti-Dindaan sòon̄ nì Abraam míǹ ki dóòyee ní ki búntì, ní Abraam mun nín fàtī u-dumpu.

ref: That evening, while Lot was sitting near the city gate, the two angels arrived in Sodom. When Lot saw them, he got up, bowed down low,
text: Ituun ilee dūu bàn̄ Soodoom tiŋkin kujoou. Looti nìn nyáń ki kā Soodoom tiŋki punyɔkɔpu. U kēe ní ki ká bi dòoń, ní u yíkì ki túŋkì-bi. U bàn̄ bi-ceeyee ní ki gbāaǹ ki jáam̀-bi,

ref: and said, “Gentlemen, I am your servant. Please come to my home. You can wash your feet, spend the night, and be on your way in the morning.” They told him, “No, we'll spend the night in the city square.”
text: ní ki bí-bi yii: «M-dindambi, m gbāam̀-ni ní, pàatīmaan man ni-tɔntɔnliu dumpu kí fíntí ni-taaŋi kí dòon̄ m-cee, ku yaa fu wúntì kutaafaau pɔ̀lpɔlee, ní fu nìkī ni-saŋŋu.» Ní bi yíì yii bi làá dòon̄ mmɔŋki laaba ní.

ref: But Lot kept insisting, until they finally agreed and went home with him. He quickly baked some bread, cooked a meal, and they ate.
text: Ní u páb́-bi tikpil ní bi dāa pāatì ki kɔ́ u-dumpu, ní u púĺ kpɔnɔ un baa ŋá ŋkpɔnɔnyɔkɔee ki ŋá-bi tican ní bi jíń.

ref: Before Lot and his guests could go to bed, every man in Sodom, young and old, came and stood outside his house
text: Bi dáá kā kaa láá kɔ́ ki dōoǹ, ní Soodoom tiŋki yab kɔkɔ dɔ́miń ki māntìi tú bi-diiku, kí yòon̄ binaacimpɔmbi kí cáá sāā bitikpilib, bininjab kɔkɔ, ugàntil kpá.

ref: and started shouting, “Where are your visitors? Send them out, so we can have sex with them!”
text: Ní bi yíiń Looti ki bí-u yii: «Bininjab bin kɔ́ a-pu kunyeeu neeyee bí la? Nyāntiń-bi doo timbi ń dòon̄ nì bi.»

ref: Lot went outside and shut the door behind him.
text: Ní Looti nyáń ki yúl̀ kipunyɔkɔpu ki gíiǹ punuu ki pīiǹ, ní ki bí-bi yii:

ref: Then he said, “Friends, please don't do such a terrible thing!
text: «M-nabiyaamu, m gbàam̄-ni, ní taa ŋámaan ikpiti gbanti.

ref: I have two daughters who have never had sex. I'll bring them out, and you can do what you want with them. But don't harm these men. They are guests in my home.”
text: M cáá bisapɔmbi bili, baa nyí uninja. M làá cáań-bi kí tīī-ni ní ŋá-bi ni yaa là puee. Ama an yaa sá bininjab neeyee, ní taa ŋá-bi tiba, kun puee bi kɔ́ m-pu ní.»

ref: “Don't get in our way,” the crowd answered. “You're a foreigner. What right do you have to order us around? We'll do worse things to you than we're going to do to them.” The crowd kept arguing with Lot. Finally, they rushed toward the door to break it down.
text: Ní bi kíí Looti yii: «Sútí niin kí wàatī timbi.» Ní ki kútì ki bí yii: «Unil nee sá unicaan ní ki kɔ́ timbi ní ki là kí nīn sá ti-bɔsoonliu. Too, ti làá ŋá-si tikpitil kí jītíǹ a-nicambi gba!» Niinee ní bi téé Looti mpɔn kí bàà cúú punuu kí wīī kí kɔ́ bininjabee pu.

ref: But the two angels in the house reached out and pulled Lot safely inside.
text: Ní bininjabee tāntì bi-ŋalii ki cúú Looti ŋal ki dáaĺ-u ki kɔ́ǹ bi-cee, ní ki dū punuu ki pīiǹ.

ref: Then they struck blind everyone in the crowd, and none of them could even find the door.
text: Ní ki dū ijɔfi ki tɔ́ bin kɔkɔ nìn dɔ́miń ki yú kudii nyɔkɔpuee, ki yōoǹ binaacimpɔmbi ki tin cáa sāā bitikpilib, ní baa ti wāĺ ní ki nyàab̄ kipunyɔkɔ ki gīī.

ref: The two angels said to Lot, “The Lord has heard many terrible things about the people of Sodom, and he has sent us here to destroy the city. Take your family and leave. Take every relative you have in the city, as well as the men your daughters are going to marry.”
text: Di sá yii ti làá kúntí kitiŋ nee, kun puee ti-Dindaan nín gbìl̄ bi sòoǹ ki-kpitii pu miŋyee wīikí tikpil, ní u túnní-ti yii tí dāá kúntí-kì.»

ref: Lot went to the men who were engaged to his daughters and said, “Hurry up and get out of here! The Lord is going to destroy this city.” But they thought he was joking, and they laughed at him.
text: Ní Looti nyáń ki tùkū u-coobi bin làá dāá cáá u-bisalibee yii: «Yīkìmaan kí nyā kitiŋ nee poon, kun puee ti-Dindaan làá kúntí-kì.» Ama bíǹ nìn dākafì yii an sá tinaacin ní.

ref: Early the next morning the two angels tried to make Lot hurry and leave. They said, “Take your wife and your two daughters and get away from here as fast as you can! If you don't, every one of you will be killed when the Lord destroys the city.”
