text: Ní Leeya yumbusapɔɔŋwu Siilpa màl̄ Jaakɔb kibininjabiki.

ref: When they had another son,
text: Ní Leeya yumbusapɔɔŋwu Siilpa fātìi màl̄ Jaakɔb kibininjabiki kililiitii.

ref: Leah exclaimed, “I'm happy now, and all the women will say how happy I am.” So she named him Asher. Love Flowers
text: Ní Leeya bí yii: «Ba mɔŋŋu bulifi poon ní m bí nee! Bisapɔmbi làá dāá yíiń-mi yii mmɔn daan.» Ní ki pú-kì diyindi yii Aseer, taapu di sá yii <Mmɔndaan.>

ref: During the time of the wheat harvest, Reuben found some love flowers and took them to his mother Leah. Rachel asked Leah for some of them,
text: Ŋyunti ŋun bi cēē tijimpinee, Rubeen cūtì dalba kusaau ní ki ká kufaapibiku kun bi yíì yii mandragɔɔree biliŋ ní ki kītìń ki cáań ki tīī u-na Leeya. Ní Raseel bí Leeya yii: «M gbāam̀-si, lēē mandragɔɔr biliŋ ŋin a-jipɔɔn cáań-see kí tīī-maa!»

ref: but Leah said, “It's bad enough that you stole my husband! Now you want my son's love flowers too.” “All right,” Rachel answered. “Let me have the flowers, and you can sleep with Jacob tonight.”
text: Ní Leeya kíí-u yii: «A fōō m-calee, an kaa yá ki kɔ́-si ní a gíti là kí fōō m-jipɔɔn mun mandragɔɔr biliŋ kí kūtàaa?» Ní Raseel bí yii: «An yaa sée, tí kpántí. Á lēē mandragɔɔr biliŋ ŋin a-jipɔɔn cáań-see kí tīī-mi, uninja ń dòon̄ a mun cee díǹ nyeeku!»

ref: That evening when Jacob came in from the fields, Leah told him, “You're sleeping with me tonight. I hired you with my son's love flowers.” They slept together that night,
text: Kujoou Jaakɔb nyánní kusaau ki kùnnée ní Leeya túŋkì-u nsanni ní ki bí-u yii: «Man cee ní a làá dòon̄ díǹ. Kí ŋmā kí ká-si á dòon̄ m-ceeyee, m-jipɔɔn mandragɔɔr biliŋ ní m dūu pàn̄ m-yɔnti.» Ní Jaakɔb lafun dōoǹ u-cee kunyeeu gbanti.

ref: and God answered Leah's prayers by giving her a fifth son.
text: Ní Unimbɔti fōō Leeya wimmeem ní u cáá dipool ki màl̄ Jaakɔb kibininjabiki kiŋmooŋmootii.

ref: Leah shouted, “God has rewarded me for letting Jacob marry my servant,” and she named the boy Issachar.
text: Ní Leeya bí yii: «Unimbɔti pàn̄-mi m nín dū m-yumbusapɔɔŋwu ki tīī m-cal u dūu kpántì u-nimpuuee pu.» Ní u pú-kì diyindi yii Isakaar, taapu di sá yii <M-paati.>

ref: she exclaimed, “God has given me a wonderful gift, and my husband will praise me for giving him six sons.” So she named the boy Zebulun.
text: Niinee ní Leeya bí yii: «Unimbɔti pú-mi dipɔl dimɔntiil. Min bí nee, m-cal làá tīī-mi m-nyooti, kun puee m màl̄-u mbininjabiyaamu muluu dee.» Míǹ puee ní u pú kibikee diyindi yii Sabulɔn, taapu di sá yii <Tinyool.>

ref: Later, Leah had a daughter and named her Dinah.
text: Boonee ní u màl̄ kibinimpubiki ní ki pú-kì diyindi yii Diina.

ref: Finally, God remembered Rachel—he answered her prayer by giving her a son. “God has taken away my disgrace,” she said.
text: Ní Raseel cáá dipool ki màl̄ kibininjabiki ní ki bí yii: «Unimbɔti lēē m-pu icoo.»

ref: “I'll name the boy Joseph, and I'll pray that the Lord will give me another son.” Jacob and Laban
text: Ní u pú-kì diyindi yii Jooseefi ki lī yii: «Ti-Dindaan ń gíti kūtì-mi kibininjabiki kiba!» Jaakɔb kpántì ugaaja

ref: After Joseph was born, Jacob said to Laban, “Release me from our agreement and let me return to my own country.
text: Raseel nín màl̄ Jooseefi boonee ní Jaakɔb bí Laaban yii: «Pɔ̄-mi nsan ḿ fàtīí nīn kùǹ m-tiŋkin.

ref: You know how hard I've worked for you, so let me take my wives and children and leave.”
text: Dū m-biyaamu nì m-nimpoobi bin pu m túń-si ditundee kí tīī-mi ḿ nīn cá, di sá yii sii a-ba nyí ditundi din bulifi m túń ki tīī-see.»

ref: Jacob answered: You've seen how hard I've worked for you, and you know how your flocks and herds have grown under my care.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bí-u yii: «Sii a-ba nyí m nín túń ditundi din ki tīī-see nì m nín ŋúb́ a-waŋkuti tì būtì miŋyee.

ref: You didn't have much before I came, but the Lord has blessed everything I have ever done for you. Now it's time for me to start looking out for my own family.
text: Di sá yii kí wàatī ḿ bàńee, tin a nìn cááyee kaa nìn náaǹ báà fiii. Ama kí yòon̄ ŋwiin ŋun m-taal tāā a-dumpuee, a-wanti wíiǹ tikpil ní ti-Dindaan bīiǹ a-boon. Too, kɔŋkɔnnee ŋyunti ŋulɔŋu ní man làá gíti dūú tɔ̄ ditundi kí ká tin làá ŋmàntī ḿ kpèè m-dicaŋkuee?»

ref: “How much do you want me to pay you?” Laban asked. Then Jacob told him: I don't want you to pay me anything. Just do one thing, and I'll take care of your sheep and goats.
text: Ní Laaban bí-u yii: «Ba ní m máaǹ kí tīī-si ní?» Ní Jaakɔb bí yii: «Taa pée tīī-mi tiba. Ama a yaa kíí kí ŋá min m làá màkà-si neeyee, m làá gíti kpàà a-waŋkuti.

ref: Let me go through your flocks and herds and take the sheep and goats that are either spotted or speckled and the black lambs. That's all you need to give me.
text: Díǹ nee, m làá yìiǹ tipicaŋkpun nì tiŋɔcaŋkpun poon kí gàntī ipii in cáá atan atanee nì in bɔ̀ǹee, ní kí gàntī iŋoobu in mun cáá atan atanee, án di làá nīn sá man paati.

ref: In the future you can easily find out if I've been honest. Just look and see if my animals are either spotted or speckled, or if the lambs are black. If they aren't, they've been stolen from you.
text: Fool boon a yaa láa dɔ́miń ki kpēē m-paati ní ki yaa láa ká iŋoobu kansikin in kaa cáá atanee nì ipii kansikin in kaa bɔ̀ǹee, mimmee m yùn̄ dee, maa cáá mbaamɔn.»

ref: “I agree to that,” was Laban's response.
text: Ní Laaban kíí-u yii: «An ŋān̄, a nín lī puee, án ŋá míǹ.»

ref: Before the end of the day, Laban had separated his spotted and speckled animals and the black lambs from the others and had put his sons in charge of them.
text: Yaadal yaadal ní Laaban lútì iŋɔjaa nì iŋɔsal in cáá atan atanee, an dàkà yii in kɔkɔ pée cáá mpimee, nì ipibɔn ki dūu tīī u-jipɔmbi.

ref: Then Laban made Jacob keep the rest of the sheep and goats at a distance of three days' journey.
text: U nìn yāntì bi cáá-i ki búntì ndandati bi nì Jaakɔb kansikin sá kii iwiin ita saŋkindi nyɔkɔm, ní Jaakɔb gbíntìi bíi kpàá Laaban waŋkuti tin kíǹee.

ref: Jacob cut branches from some poplar trees and from some almond and evergreen trees. He peeled off part of the bark and made the branches look spotted and speckled.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bátì isufi in bi yíì peepiliiyee nì amandi nì plataanee pambiyaamu nsɔmu, ki cùkū cùkū-mu ki māntìi tú ki dāa kátì taapu, ní ki pɔ́kɔtì ajan ŋin u cùkūee, ní ndɔsɔmuee kíǹ ki cáá mpim mpim.

ref: Then he put the branches where the sheep and goats would see them while they were drinking from the water trough. The goats mated there
text: Ní u dū ndɔsɔmu mun u cùkūee ki ŋá tiwan tin poon tiwaŋkuti nyùee ki túĺ tiwaŋkuti nimbiin, ní tì yaa dòoń ki nyùee, tì jɔ́ḿ tɔb.

ref: in front of the branches, and their young were spotted and speckled.
text: Ndɔsɔmu mun u cùkūee cee ní tiwaŋkutee jɔ́ḿ tɔb ní ki màal̀ mbiyaamu cáá atan atan.

ref: Some of the sheep that Jacob was keeping for Laban were already spotted. And when the others were ready to mate, he made sure that they faced in the direction of the spotted and black ones. In this way, Jacob built up a flock of sheep for himself and did not put them with the other sheep.
text: Jaakɔb nìn gāntì ipisalee ní ki yāntì i kpèé Laaban waŋkuti tin cáá atan atanee nì tin bɔ̀ǹee. Míǹ ní u ŋáaǹ ní ki ká tipicaŋkpun nì tiŋɔcaŋkpun, ní ki gāntì-tì ki yóoǹ u-ba kaa dū tíǹ ki kpáfì nì Laaban yati.

ref: When the stronger sheep were mating near the drinking place, Jacob made sure that the spotted branches were there.
text: Ŋyunti ŋun ní tiwaŋkuti tin yáàyee yaa làá jɔ̄m̀ tɔbee, ní Jaakɔb ń yòon̄ní ndɔsɔmu mun u cùkūee kí ŋá tiwan tin poon tiwaŋkuti nyùee kí túĺ ti-nimbiin, ní tì jɔ́ḿ tɔb ndɔsɔmu mun u cùkūee cee.

ref: But he would not put out the branches when the weaker animals were mating. So Jacob got all of the healthy animals, and Laban got what was left.
text: Ama tiwaŋkuti tin sá ŋkaŋkalimuee yaa bāańee, waa yōontí ndɔsɔmuee ti-nimbiin tí jɔ̄m̀ tɔb. Ŋkaŋkalimu gbantee, Laaban yati dee, ní úǹ yati sá tin yáàyee. Jaakɔb sáǹ Laaban

ref: Jacob soon became rich and successful. He owned many sheep, goats, camels, and donkeys, as well as a lot of slaves.
text: Míǹ ní Jaakɔb ŋá ki kpántì ugaajakpaŋkpaan ki cáá tiwaŋkuti tikpil, ki cáá bitɔntɔnliibi binimpoobi nì bininjab nì igutaamu nì mmamu.

ref: Jacob heard that Laban's sons were complaining, “Jacob is now a rich man, and he got everything he owns from our father.”
text: Jaakɔb nìn gbìl̄ Laaban jipɔmbi bí ki lì yii: «Jaakɔb fōō timbi baa wanti kɔkɔ, an wanti pu ní u kpántì ugaaja nee.»

ref: Jacob also noticed that Laban was not as friendly as he had been before.
text: Jaakɔb nín kpēē Laaban nimbiinee, u dáań ki ká yii u nì u kansikin kaa ti mɔ̄ kii dooooyee an nín nìn bí puee.

ref: One day the Lord said, “Jacob, go back to your relatives in the land of your ancestors, and I will be with you.”
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí Jaakɔb yii: «Nín̄ gítí a-naanjab tiŋkin a-dumpu, man làá nīn bí a-cee.»

ref: Jacob sent for Rachel and Leah to meet him in the pasture where he kept his sheep,
text: Niinee ní Jaakɔb tú bi yíiń u-nimpoobi Raseel nì Leeya bí dāan̄ u-cee kusaaun laakin u-waŋkuti bée.

ref: and he told them: Your father isn't as friendly with me as he used to be, but the God my ancestors worshiped has been on my side.
text: Bi bàn̄ Jaakɔb ceeyee ní u bí-bi yii: «M dáań ki ká ni-baa nimbiin yii man nì u kansikin kaa ti mɔ̄ kii dooooyee. Ama m-baa Nimbɔtiu bí m-cee.

ref: years to buy your sheep and goats. During that time you kept changing my wages.
text: Nimbi ni-ba nyí m nín dū m-pɔŋŋu kɔkɔ ki túń ditundi ki tīī ni-baa puee.

ref: and that he keeps cheating me by changing my wages time after time. But God has protected me.
text: Ní an yaa bàà sá ú pà-mi puee ní ú cúú kpántí. U kpántì-mi míǹ ní áli ŋfunyɔm saalaa pu, ama Unimbɔti kaa kíí ú ŋá-mi digaŋgam.

ref: When your father said the speckled sheep would be my wages, all of them were speckled. And when he said the spotted ones would be mine, all of them were spotted.
text: U yaa lī yii: <Tiwaŋkuti tin kɔkɔ cáá atanee di sá a-paatee>, tiwaŋkuti kɔkɔ màal̀ tin cáá atanee ní. U yaa tí lī yii: <Tiwaŋkuti tin kɔkɔ cáá iluntoonee di sá a-paatee>, tiwaŋkuti kɔkɔ tí màal̀ tin cáá iluntoonee ní.

ref: Once, when the flocks were mating, I dreamed that all the rams were either spotted or speckled.
text: Ŋyunti ŋun tiwaŋkuti bíi jɔ́ḿ tɔbee ní m ká kudamintiiu poon yii ijaa in bíi jɔ́ḿ isalee sá atan atan nì iluntoon iluntoon nì ŋmàtì ŋmati.

ref: Then God's angel called me by name. I answered,
text: Kudamintiiuee poon ní Unimbɔti tuuŋŋu yíiń-mi yii: <Jaakɔb!> Ní m kíí yii: <M sèé.>

ref: and he said, “Notice that all the rams are either spotted or speckled. I know everything Laban is doing to you,
text: Ní ŋu bí-mi yii: <Yáatí kí kpèè, iŋɔjaa in bí ki jɔ́ḿ isalee kɔkɔ sá atan atan nì iluntoon iluntoon nì ŋmàtì ŋmati ní. M ŋá míǹ kun puee m ká Laaban nín ŋá-si puee.

ref: and I am the God you worshiped at Bethel, when you poured olive oil on a rock and made a promise to me. Leave here at once and return to the land where you were born.”
text: Man di sá Unimbɔti un nyántì u-ba ki dàkā-si Beeteel, laakin a nìn dū ditaŋkpal ki cóoǹ ki sītì di-pu ŋkpam ní ki pūtì ki pōoǹ-mee. Kɔŋkɔnnee yīkì kí nīn gítí a-tiŋkin, laakin bi màl̄-see.>»

ref: He treats us like foreigners and has even cheated us out of the bride price that should have been ours.
text: U dū-ti ki gíiǹ binicambi ní, kun puee u nyáfì-ti ní ki fōō animbil ki jíń.

ref: So do whatever God tells you to do. Even the property God took from our father and gave to you really belongs to us and our children.
text: An puee ti-baa gaajati tin Unimbɔti fōō u-ceeyee, timbi nì ti-biyaamu di yì. Min kɔkɔ Unimbɔti yaa bí yii á ŋée, ŋá míǹ.»

ref: Then Jacob, his wives, and his children got on camels and left
text: Niinee ní Jaakɔb yōoǹ u-biyaamu nì u-nimpoobi ki kāaǹ igutaamu pu kí yīkì.

ref: northern Syria for the home of his father Isaac in Canaan. Jacob took along all his flocks, herds, and other property.
text: U nìn yōoǹ ki kpáaǹ u-waŋkuti nì u-gaajati tin u nìn kāl̀ Padan-Aram ki kée kɔkɔ ki cáa nín gítí u-baa Isaaki cee Kanaan tiŋkin.

ref: Before Rachel left, she stole the household idols while Laban was out shearing his sheep.
text: Laaban nìn búntì kí cā u-piii kuti. Ŋyunti gbanti ní Raseel yùn̄ u-baa waaŋi.

ref: long years to earn your two daughters and another
text: Ní Jaakɔb bál̀ Laaban uaranja ki yíkì kaa kāaǹ-u.

ref: When Jacob crossed the Euphrates River and headed for the hill country of Gilead, he took with him everything he owned. Laban Catches Up with Jacob
text: U nìn kpáaǹ u-wanti tin u cááyee kɔkɔ ki cūtìi dūtì Eefiraati mɔɔkpaandi ní ki dū u-ba ki túĺ Galaadi jool watil pú.

ref: Three days later Laban found out that Jacob had gone.
text: Jaakɔb yíkìi sáǹ iwiin ita boon ní bi dāa nín tùkū Laaban.

ref: So he took some of his relatives along and chased after Jacob for seven days, before catching up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
text: Niinee ní Laaban yōoǹ u-nabiyaamu ní bi nín jì Jaakɔb boon. Bi cōom̀ iwiin iluli ní ki tin nín cúú-u Galaadi jool pu.

ref: But God appeared to Laban in a dream that night and warned, “Don't say a word to Jacob. Don't make a threat or a promise.”
text: Ama kunyeeuee ní Unimbɔti nyántì u-ba ki dàkā Laaban uaranja kudamintiiu poon ní ki bí-u yii: «Á cúú a-ba kí taa lì Jaakɔb tiba nì tiba, kí taa ŋá-u tikpitil báà tiba.»

ref: Jacob had set up camp in the hill country of Gilead, when Laban and his relatives came and set up camp in another part of the hill country. Laban went to Jacob
text: Ŋyunti ŋun Laaban dūu bàn̄ Jaakɔb ceeyee, Jaakɔb gāā u-tantiu Galaadi jool pu, ní Laaban nì u-nabiyaamu mun gāā bi-tantib Galaadi jool gbanti pu.

ref: and said: Look what you've done! You've tricked me and run off with my daughters like a kidnapper.
text: Ní Laaban bālifì Jaakɔb yii: «Ba ŋá ní a ŋá mimmee? Ba pu ní a kíil̀-mi ki kpáaǹ m-bisalib ki búntì kii kujaau ní a cūtì ki jáń ki cúuń-bee?

ref: Why did you sneak away without telling me? I would have given you a going-away party with singing and with music on tambourines and harps.
text: Ba pu ní a kíil̀-mi ki sáǹ dibalin míǹ kaa kāaǹ-mi? M bàà kàantí-si nì ifaal ní kí yàntī bí gāā ilan kí bɔ̀ɔ̀ ŋgaŋgamu nì tibati.

ref: You didn't even give me a chance to kiss my own grandchildren and daughters goodbye. That was really foolish.
text: Aa yāntì m kpàl̄ m-bisalib nì m-naantiibi ki kāaǹ-bi. Min a ŋá nee sá digbaandi ní.

ref: I could easily hurt you, but the God your father worshiped has warned me not to make any threats or promises.
text: M bàà cáá nsan kí ŋá-ni tin kaa ŋān̄ee, ama fool kunyeeu ní a-baa Nimbɔtiu fu sòon̄ nì mi ki bí-mi yii: <Á cúú a-ba kí taa lì Jaakɔb tiba nì tiba, kí taa ŋá-u tikpitil báà tiba.>

ref: I can understand why you were eager to return to your father, but why did you have to steal my idols?
text: Kɔŋkɔnnee a-landɔkɔŋu gítì a-baa cindi pu ní a yíkì ki kùǹ, kin ba pu ní a gíti yù man waaŋi?»

ref: Jacob answered, “I left secretly because I was afraid you would take your daughters from me by force.
text: Ní Jaakɔb kíí Laaban yii: «M fāŋkì ní ki dākafì yii m yaa kāaǹ-si, an yaa yíì a làá fōō a-bisalib.

ref: If you find that any one of us has taken your idols, I'll have that person killed. Let your relatives be witnesses. Show me what belongs to you, and you can take it back.” Jacob did not realize that Rachel had stolen the household idols.
text: Ama un cee ní a yaa ká a-waaŋee, bí kpɔ̀-u. Ti-nabiyaamu kɔkɔ di bí nee. Dāan̄ m-cee kí kpèè a yaa ká a-wanti tiba á yòon̄.» Jaakɔb kaa nìn nyí yii Raseel di yùn̄ awaa.

ref: Laban searched the tents of Jacob, Leah, and the two servant women, but did not find the idols. Then he went to Rachel's tent.
text: Ní Laaban nín kóò Jaakɔb tantiun ki kpēē, ní ki kɔ́ Leeya yɔun ki kpēē, ní ki kɔ́ inimpuyumbu liitil cee ki kpēē kaa ká báà tiba. U nyáń Leeya ceeyee ní ki nín kóò Raseel tantiun.

ref: She had already hidden them in the cushion she used as a saddle and was sitting on it. Laban searched everywhere and did not find them.
text: Raseel nìn yōoǹ awaayee ki dūu bál̀ ugutaamu gakal taapu ní ki kāl̀ an pu, ní Laaban kɔ́ ki kpēē u-tantiu poon kɔkɔ ní kaa ká tiba.

ref: Rachel said, “Father, please don't be angry with me for not getting up; I'm having my period.” Laban kept on searching, but still did not find the idols.
text: Ní Raseel bí u-baa yii: «M nín ká-si kaa yíkèe, án taa wà-si, di sá yii m ká m-ba ní.» Ní Laaban kpēē kaa ká awaa.

ref: Jacob became very angry and said to Laban: What have I done wrong? Have I committed some crime? Is that why you hunted me down?
text: Niinee Jaakɔb fōō Laaban pu diŋuul ní ki yíkìi nyáń u-pu, ní ki bí-u yii: «Tilati ní m yīntiǹ-si? Ikpiti ilai ní m ŋá-si ní a jìǹní m-boon nì dibaatandi míǹ?

ref: After searching through everything I have, did you find anything of yours? If so, put it here, where your relatives and mine can see it. Then we can decide what to do.
text: A nín kpēē m-wanti kɔkɔee, a ká a-wanti tiba bí poonaaa? A yaa ká, á nyāntiń-tì man nib nì si mun nib nimbiin bí sòoǹ kí tīī-ti.

ref: If a wild animal killed one of your sheep or goats, I paid for it myself. In fact, you demanded the full price, whether the animal was killed during the day or at night.
text: Tiba di yaa cúú a-waŋkuluuku kubɔee, maa cāabìń-si diwaŋkpil, see ḿ nyàab̀ kí téeǹ kun an cúúyee yil. A-waŋkuti tin bi yù ŋwiimpuee nì tin bi yù kunyeeuee, a bàlifī-mi ní ḿ téeǹ-si.

ref: I sweated every day, and I couldn't sleep at night because of the cold.
text: Ŋwiimpuee, ŋwìiń ŋmóò-mi ní kí kpɔ̀, ní kunyeeuee, tiwool ń kɔ́ kí dóó-mi ní ŋgeem ń wāŋkì m-nimbiliŋin.

ref: If the fearsome God worshiped by Abraham and my father Isaac had not been on my side, you would have sent me away without a thing. But God saw my hard work, and he knew the trouble I was in, so he helped me. Then last night he told you how wrong you were. Jacob and Laban Make an Agreement
text: An yaa kaa sá m-baa Isaaki nì m-naanja Abraam Nimbɔtiu, Unimbɔti un m-baa Isaaki jáaḿ ki fàŋkée pu, a bàà làá yàntī ḿ nīn fàtīi gítí nì iŋaŋkulim ní. Ama Unimbɔti ká m-falaau nì m-tundi din m túńee. An pu ní fool nyeekuee, u fu sòon̄ ki kpáafì-si.»

ref: Laban said to Jacob, “Leah and Rachel are my daughters, and their children belong to me. All these sheep you are taking are really mine too. In fact, everything you have belongs to me. But there is nothing I can do to keep my daughters and their children.
text: Ní Laaban kíí Jaakɔb yii: «Binimpoobi nee sá man bisalib ní, ní bi-biyaamu sá man biyaamu. Ipii nee sá man piii ní, tin kɔkɔ a pée wāĺ nee sá man wanti ní. Díǹ pu nee, ba ní m làá ŋá kí tīī m-bisalib nì bi-biyaamu mun bi màl̄ee?

ref: So I am ready to make an agreement with you, and we will pile up some large rocks here to remind us of the agreement.”
text: An ŋmāntì man nì si tí sòoǹ kí bóob́ kí pòon̄ tɔb, án nīn sá kudaaŋŋu man nì si kansikin.»

ref: After Jacob had set up a large rock,
text: Niinee ní Jaakɔb píí ditaŋkpal ki cóoǹ án nīn sá-bi kudaaŋŋu.

ref: he told his men to get some more rocks and pile them up next to it. Then Jacob and Laban ate a meal together beside the rocks.
text: Ní ki bí u-nabiyaamu yii bí tòò ataŋkpee kí bíĺ dikpúkul. Bi tōō ki bíl̀ee ní bi-kɔkɔ kāl̀ di-pu ki jíń tijin.

ref: Laban named the pile of rocks Jegar Sahadutha. But Jacob named it Galeed.
text: Ní Laaban pú ditaŋkpakpukulee diyindi u-sooŋyi poon yii Yeegar-Saaduta, ní Jaakɔb mun yíiń-dì u-sooŋyi poon yii Galeedi.

ref: Laban said to Jacob, “This pile of rocks will remind us of our agreement.” That's why the place was named Galeed.
text: Ní Laaban bí yii: «Ditaŋkpakpukul nee di sá dipoondi din dōoǹ man nì si kansikinee daaŋku.» An pu ní bi pú mpaan gbanti diyindi yii Galeedi, taapu di sá yii: <Kudaaŋŋu taŋkpakpukul>.

ref: Laban also said, “This pile of rocks means that the Lord will watch us both while we are apart from each other.” So the place was also named Mizpah.
text: Bi gíti pú niin diyindi yii Miisipa, taapu di sá yii: <Unimbɔti ń nīn bí ki wāĺ-ti.> Di sá Laaban nìn bí yii: «Unimbɔti ń nīn bí ki wāĺ man nì si kansikin ti yaa kaa dāa gíti bí ki wāĺ tɔbee.

ref: Then Laban said: If you mistreat my daughters or marry other women, I may not know about it, but remember, God is watching us!
text: A yaa láa cáá binimpoobi ki kútì ní ki ŋúb́ man bisalib ki sàantée, báà ubɔ yaa kaa dāa bí ki wāĺ tin a bí ki ŋáańeeyaaa, á nīn nyí yii Unimbɔti bí ki wāĺ tin bí ki jītí man nì sii kansikinee.»

ref: Both this pile of rocks and this large rock have been set up between us as a reminder. I must never go past them to attack you, and you must never come past them to attack me.
text: Ditaŋkpakpukul nee nì ditaŋkpal din cɔ́ nee di sá-ti kudaaŋŋu yii man kaa làá lákatí ditaŋkpakpukul nee pu kí póotí a-pu, si mun ń taa lákatí ditaŋkpakpukul nee nì ditaŋkpacooŋkaal nee pu kí póotí man pu kí ŋá tikpitil.

ref: My father Nahor, your grandfather Abraham, and their ancestors all worshiped the same God, and he will make sure that we each keep the agreement. Then Jacob made a promise in the name of the fearsome God his father Isaac had worshiped.
text: Abraam nì Naakɔɔr Nimbɔtiu un sá bi-baa Nimbɔtiuee ń dāá sòoǹ man nì sii kansikin bɔti.» Ní Jaakɔb pūtì Unimbɔti un u-baa Isaaki nìn jáaḿ ki fàŋkée.

ref: Jacob killed an animal and offered it as a sacrifice there on the mountain, and he invited his men to eat with him. After the meal they spent the night on the mountain.
text: Boonee ní Jaakɔb ŋá saraa ubɔ dijool pu niin ní ki yíiń u-nabiyaamu bi kpáfìi jíń tijin ní ki gbíntì dijool pu niin ki dōoǹ.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Kutaa wúntì an dáá bɔ̀ǹee ní Laaban yíkì ki kpàl̄ u-bisalib nì u-naantiibi ki kāaǹ-bi, ki mēē ki bīiǹ bi-pu ní ki fātìi nín kùǹ u-dumpu. Jaakɔb bɔ́ntì kí tūŋkì Eesayuu

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Ní Jaakɔb mun nín nìkì u-saŋŋu. U cée ní Unimbɔti tuuŋyi cílìń u-pu nsanni.

ref: Jacob sent messengers on ahead to Esau, who lived in the land of Seir, also known as Edom.
text: U ká-iee ní ki téeń yii: «Unimbɔti janjanaapaal sèé!» An pu ní u pú niin diyindi yii Maanayiim, taapu di sá yii: <Ajanjanaapaa ali>.

ref: Jacob told them to say to Esau, “Master, I am your servant! I have lived with Laban all this time,
text: Niinee ní Jaakɔb lēē binib ki tú u-maan Eesayuu cee Eedoom satin, Seeyiir tiŋki poon.

ref: and now I own cattle, donkeys, and sheep, as well as many slaves. Master, I am sending these messengers in the hope that you will be kind to me.”
text: U nìn kpáafì-bi ki bí yii: «Ni yaa bàn̄ee ní tùkù m-kpiliu Eesayuu yii: <Mama a-tɔntɔnliu Jaakɔb di túnní bí tùkù-si yii, doooo m nín búntì Laaban ceeyee, niin ní m kāl̀ ki dāa sāā díǹ.

ref: When the messengers returned, they told Jacob, “We went to your brother Esau, and now he is heading this way with
text: M cáá inaa nì mmamu nì ipii nì iŋoobu nì bitɔntɔnliibi bininjab nì binimpoobi. An pu ní m túnní bí dāá tùkù sii m-kpiliu yii á dàkà-mi a-ŋanti kí fōō-mi.>»

ref: Jacob was so frightened that he divided his people, sheep, cattle, and camels into two groups.
text: Ní binib bin u túee fātìi gítìń u-cee ní ki tùkū-u yii: «Ti bàn̄ ki ká a-maan Eesayuu. U mun bí nsanni ki túŋkiń-si u nì binib alaataa anaa.»

ref: He thought, “If Esau attacks one group, perhaps the other can escape.”
text: Niinee ní tifaandi kɔ́ Jaakɔb ní u-pɔbil bíil̀. Ní u cúú u-nib nì u-piii nì u-ŋoobui nì u-naai nì u-gutaamui ki yākatì akiti ali.

ref: Please rescue me from my brother. I am afraid he will come and attack not only me, but my wives and children as well.
text: M pátì kí fōō tiŋan tin a ŋá-mi nee nì tibulcin tin a dàkā man a-tɔntɔnliu nee, kun puee ŋkpandɔ baba ní m nìn ŋúb́ ki dūtì Jɔɔdan buŋŋu nee. Ama díǹ pu nee, m yākatì m-nib nì m-waŋkuti akiti ali ní nee.

ref: But you have promised that I would be a success and that someday it will be as hard to count my descendants as it is to count the grains of sand along the seashore.
text: Míǹ puee m gbāam̀-si, á fīī-mi m-maan Eesayuu ŋaan. M fàŋkí ú taa láa kpɔ̀ man nì m-nimpoobi nì m-biyaamu.

ref: After Jacob had spent the night there, he chose some animals as gifts for Esau:
text: Di sá yii sii di líì-mi dimákal yii: <M làá bìin̄ a-boon kí yàntī a-naantiibi ń bùtī kii tiŋku gbandi bulee, bí taa ŋmā kí kàaǹ-bi.>»

ref: And Jacob told them to be sure to say that he was right behind them. Jacob hoped the gifts would make Esau friendly, so Esau would be glad to see him when they met.
text: Míǹ mbaantiim ní u tùkū u-tɔntɔnliu uliliiti nì utataati nì u-tɔntɔnliibi bin kɔkɔ u lēē tiwaŋkuti ki tīī bí lìntī u-nyɔkɔpuee, ní ki bí-bi yii: «Ni yaa tú Eesayuu nsannee, min ní ni làá lèe dee.

ref: Jacob said, “I have seen God face to face, and I am still alive.” So he named the place Peniel.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bí-u yii: «M gbāam̀-si, tùkù-mi a-yindi.» Ní u bí-u yii: «Ba pu ní a là kí béé m-yindi?» Niin niin ní u bīiǹ Jaakɔb boon.

ref: Jacob put servants in charge of each herd and told them, “Go ahead of me and keep a space between each herd.”
text: ki lēē igutaamu in cáá mbiyaamu ŋáaḿee nì i-biyaamu mmuŋku nì saalaa, ki lēē inasal imuŋku ili nì inajaa saalaa, ki lēē mmansalimu mmuŋku nì mmanjamu saalaa.

ref: Then he said to the servant in charge of the first herd, “When Esau meets you, he will ask whose servant you are. He will want to know where you are going and who owns those animals in front of you.
text: U nìn yākatì tiwaŋkutee akiti akiti ki dūu tīī u-tɔntɔnliibi ní ki bí-bi yii: «Lìntīmaan kí nīn cá kí nīn pá tɔb boon ki dátiǹ tɔb.»

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: Ní u kpáafì un līīyee yii: «M-maan Eesayuu yaa tú-si nsanni ní ki yaa bālifì-si yii: <A-kpiliu sá ŋma ní a cá la? Ŋma di yì tiwaŋkuti tin līī a-nyɔkɔpu nee?>,

ref: Jacob also told the men in charge of the second and third herds and those who followed to say the same thing when they met Esau.
text: á kíí-u yii: <An sá a-tɔntɔnliu Jaakɔb di yì. U túnní-mi ḿ cáań kí tīī u-kpiliu Eesayuu ní. Úǹ u-ba páń boon ki dòoń.>»

ref: Jacob's men took the gifts on ahead of him, but he spent the night in camp. Jacob's Name Is Changed to Israel
text: Ní ní kūtì kí lì yii: <A-tɔntɔnliu Jaakɔb u-ba páń boon ki dòoń.>» Di sá u lī u-neen yii: «M làá dū tiwaŋkuti nee kí pɔ̄-u kí sɔ̀ŋkì u-pɔbil, boonee ní kí dāá nín ká-u. An yaa yíì u táá sɔ̄ m-saai kí fōō-mi.»

ref: Jacob got up in the middle of the night and took his wives, his eleven children, and everything he owned across to the other side of the Jabbok River for safety.
text: Kunyeeu gbanti ní Jaakɔb yōoǹ u-nimpoobi liitil nì inimpuyumbu liitil ki kpáaǹ u-biyaamu saalaa nì kibee ki dūtì Yaabɔki buŋŋu laakin pú kaa nyóòyee.

ref: Afterwards, Jacob went back and spent the rest of the night alone. A man came and fought with Jacob until just before daybreak.
text: U cáá u-yab kɔkɔ ki dūtì mbun ki kpáaǹ tin kɔkɔ u cááyee.

ref: When the man saw that he could not win, he struck Jacob on the hip and threw it out of joint.
text: Jaakɔb cáá-bi ki dūtì míǹ ki dóò, ki kíǹ u-babee ní unaaciin ubɔ dɔ́miń kunyeeu ki cìb̄-u dimool ní bi mɔ̀n̄ áli ntaafaabɔmbɔm.

ref: They kept on wrestling until the man said, “Let go of me! It's almost daylight.” “You can't go until you bless me,” Jacob replied.
text: Unaaciinee kpēē yii waa làá ŋmā kí líí Jaakɔbee ní u fáá u-nantaŋkpal ní u-kpìiŋŋu yɔ̄tì, ama ní u nì Jaakɔb gbíntìi bíi mɔ̀.

ref: The man said, “From now on, your name will no longer be Jacob. You will be called Israel, because you have wrestled with God and with men, and you have won.”
text: Ní unaaciinee bālifì-u yii: «Bi yíì-si yii ba ní?» Ní u kíí-u yii: «Bi yíì-mi yii Jaakɔb ní.»

ref: Jacob said, “Now tell me your name.” “Don't you know who I am?” he asked. And he blessed Jacob.
text: Ní unaaciinee bí-u yii: «Baa làá ti yíiń-si Jaakɔb, bi làá nīn kíǹ ki yíì-si yii Isirayeel ní. Di sá yii si nì Unimbɔti mɔ̀n̄ ní si nì binib mun mɔ̀n̄, ní a pɔ́ɔkiǹ bi-kɔkɔ.»

ref: The sun was coming up as Jacob was leaving Peniel. He was limping because he had been struck on the hip,
text: Ní Jaakɔb pú niin diyindi yii Peeniyeel, taapu di sá yii: <Unimbɔti nimbiin>, di sá u lī yii: «M ká Unimbɔti yéè kpìrrr ama ní kaa kpíì.»

ref: and the muscle on his hip joint had been injured. That's why even today the people of Israel don't eat the hip muscle of any animal.
text: Kutaa wúntì ŋwìiń nyáń ní Jaakɔb yíkì Peeniyeel. U-kpìiŋŋu puee, u nìn dúlifí ní.
