text: ki lēē igutaamu in cáá mbiyaamu ŋáaḿee nì i-biyaamu mmuŋku nì saalaa, ki lēē inasal imuŋku ili nì inajaa saalaa, ki lēē mmansalimu mmuŋku nì mmanjamu saalaa.

ref: Then he said to the servant in charge of the first herd, “When Esau meets you, he will ask whose servant you are. He will want to know where you are going and who owns those animals in front of you.
text: U nìn yākatì tiwaŋkutee akiti akiti ki dūu tīī u-tɔntɔnliibi ní ki bí-bi yii: «Lìntīmaan kí nīn cá kí nīn pá tɔb boon ki dátiǹ tɔb.»

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: Ní u kpáafì un līīyee yii: «M-maan Eesayuu yaa tú-si nsanni ní ki yaa bālifì-si yii: <A-kpiliu sá ŋma ní a cá la? Ŋma di yì tiwaŋkuti tin līī a-nyɔkɔpu nee?>,

ref: Jacob also told the men in charge of the second and third herds and those who followed to say the same thing when they met Esau.
text: á kíí-u yii: <An sá a-tɔntɔnliu Jaakɔb di yì. U túnní-mi ḿ cáań kí tīī u-kpiliu Eesayuu ní. Úǹ u-ba páń boon ki dòoń.>»

ref: Jacob's men took the gifts on ahead of him, but he spent the night in camp. Jacob's Name Is Changed to Israel
text: Ní ní kūtì kí lì yii: <A-tɔntɔnliu Jaakɔb u-ba páń boon ki dòoń.>» Di sá u lī u-neen yii: «M làá dū tiwaŋkuti nee kí pɔ̄-u kí sɔ̀ŋkì u-pɔbil, boonee ní kí dāá nín ká-u. An yaa yíì u táá sɔ̄ m-saai kí fōō-mi.»

ref: Jacob got up in the middle of the night and took his wives, his eleven children, and everything he owned across to the other side of the Jabbok River for safety.
text: Kunyeeu gbanti ní Jaakɔb yōoǹ u-nimpoobi liitil nì inimpuyumbu liitil ki kpáaǹ u-biyaamu saalaa nì kibee ki dūtì Yaabɔki buŋŋu laakin pú kaa nyóòyee.

ref: Afterwards, Jacob went back and spent the rest of the night alone. A man came and fought with Jacob until just before daybreak.
text: U cáá u-yab kɔkɔ ki dūtì mbun ki kpáaǹ tin kɔkɔ u cááyee.

ref: When the man saw that he could not win, he struck Jacob on the hip and threw it out of joint.
text: Jaakɔb cáá-bi ki dūtì míǹ ki dóò, ki kíǹ u-babee ní unaaciin ubɔ dɔ́miń kunyeeu ki cìb̄-u dimool ní bi mɔ̀n̄ áli ntaafaabɔmbɔm.

ref: They kept on wrestling until the man said, “Let go of me! It's almost daylight.” “You can't go until you bless me,” Jacob replied.
text: Unaaciinee kpēē yii waa làá ŋmā kí líí Jaakɔbee ní u fáá u-nantaŋkpal ní u-kpìiŋŋu yɔ̄tì, ama ní u nì Jaakɔb gbíntìi bíi mɔ̀.

ref: The man said, “From now on, your name will no longer be Jacob. You will be called Israel, because you have wrestled with God and with men, and you have won.”
text: Ní unaaciinee bālifì-u yii: «Bi yíì-si yii ba ní?» Ní u kíí-u yii: «Bi yíì-mi yii Jaakɔb ní.»

ref: Jacob said, “Now tell me your name.” “Don't you know who I am?” he asked. And he blessed Jacob.
text: Ní unaaciinee bí-u yii: «Baa làá ti yíiń-si Jaakɔb, bi làá nīn kíǹ ki yíì-si yii Isirayeel ní. Di sá yii si nì Unimbɔti mɔ̀n̄ ní si nì binib mun mɔ̀n̄, ní a pɔ́ɔkiǹ bi-kɔkɔ.»

ref: The sun was coming up as Jacob was leaving Peniel. He was limping because he had been struck on the hip,
text: Ní Jaakɔb pú niin diyindi yii Peeniyeel, taapu di sá yii: <Unimbɔti nimbiin>, di sá u lī yii: «M ká Unimbɔti yéè kpìrrr ama ní kaa kpíì.»

ref: and the muscle on his hip joint had been injured. That's why even today the people of Israel don't eat the hip muscle of any animal.
text: Kutaa wúntì ŋwìiń nyáń ní Jaakɔb yíkì Peeniyeel. U-kpìiŋŋu puee, u nìn dúlifí ní.

ref: The two servant women, Zilpah and Bilhah, together with their children went first, followed by Leah and her children, then by Rachel and Joseph.
text: U dū inimpuyumbuee liitil nì bi-biyaamu ki līntì nyɔkɔpu, ní ki dū Leeya nì u-biyaamu ki páaǹ, ní ki dū Raseel nì u-jipɔɔn Jooseefi ki lúŋkì.

ref: Jacob himself walked in front of them all, bowing to the ground seven times as he came near his brother.
text: Ní úǹ u-ba līntì nyɔkɔpu. U nìn gbāaǹ ŋfunyɔm miluli ní ki nín bàn̄ u-maan.

ref: But Esau ran toward Jacob and hugged and kissed him. Then the two brothers started crying.
text: Niinee Eesayuu sáǹ ki túŋkì-u, ki ŋàan̄ ki cìb̄-u ní bi-kɔkɔ bíi sūǹ.

ref: When Esau noticed the women and children he asked, “Whose children are these?” Jacob answered, “These are the children the Lord has been kind enough to give to me, your servant.”
text: Eesayuu ká binimpoobi nì mbiyaamuee ní u bālifì Jaakɔb yii: «Binib bilab di pá a-boon nan ní?» Ní u kíí-u yii: «An sá mbiyaamu mun Unimbɔti ŋá man a-tɔntɔnliu tiŋan ki pú-mee.»

ref: Then the two servant women and their children came and bowed down to Esau.
text: Ní inimpuyumbuee nì bi-biyaamu sútì ki pìl̄ Eesayuu ní ki gbāaǹ u-nimbiin.

ref: Next, Leah and her children came and bowed down; finally, Joseph and Rachel also came and bowed down.
text: Ní Leeya nì u-biyaamu mun sútì ki pìl̄-u ní ki gbāaǹ u-nimbiin, ní Jooseefi nì u-na Raseel mun sútì ki pìl̄-u ní ki mun gbāaǹ u-nimbiin.

ref: Esau asked Jacob, “Why did you send those herds I met along the road?” “Master,” Jacob answered, “I sent them so you would be friendly to me.”
text: Ní Eesayuu bālifì-u yii: «A làá ŋá ba tiwaŋkuti tin m tú nsanni an fù bìti bitee ní?» Ní Jaakɔb kíí-u yii: «M nín là kí ká disaasɔl a-ceeyee pu ní m tīiń-si tiwan gbanti.»

ref: “But, brother, I already have plenty,” Esau replied. “Keep them for yourself.”
text: Ní Eesayuu kíí-u yii: «M-nabiki, m cáá tigaajati tikpil, dū a-wanti kí kpāafì.»

ref: “No!” Jacob said. “Please accept them as a sign of your friendship for me. When you welcomed me and I saw your face, it was like seeing the face of God.
text: Ní Jaakɔb kíí-u yii: «Aayee, m gbāam̀-si, a yaa nyí yii a súń m-saai, á fōō tiwan tin m tīī-si nee. Di sá yii a nín fōō-mi tiŋan m wál̀ a-nimbiinee, an bí kii Unimbɔti nimbiin ní m wál̀ee.

ref: Please accept these as gifts I brought to you. God has been good to me, and I have everything I need.” Jacob kept insisting until Esau agreed.
text: Míǹ puee m gbāam̀-si, fōō tiwan tin m tīī-si nee, kun puee Unimbɔti bīiǹ m-boon ní báà tiba kaa lóò-mi.» U páb́ Eesayuu mimmee ní u dāa fōō.

ref: “Let's get ready to travel,” Esau said. “I'll go along with you.”
text: Ní Eesayuu bí-u yii: «Tí yīkì kí nīn cá, m làá céeǹ-si.»

ref: But Jacob answered, “Master, you know traveling is hard on children, and I have to look after the sheep and goats that are nursing their young. If my animals travel too much in one day, they will all die.
text: Ní Jaakɔb kíí-u yii: «M-kpiliu, a nyí yii mbiyaamu pɔ̀fì ní m cáá ipii nì iŋoobu nì inaa in dáá màl̄ mpɔmee. Ti yaa làá kpáaǹ-i kí còom̀ malaa malaa, ŋwimbambaanti ní i-kɔkɔ làá kpú.

ref: Why don't you go on ahead and let me travel along slowly with the children, the herds, and the flocks. We can meet again in the country of Edom.”
text: Míǹ puee sii lìntī kí nīn cá, man a-tɔntɔnliu nì tiwaŋkuti nì mbiyaamu ń nīn còomìń soooo soooo ki dòoń, kí dāá bàń a-cee Seeyiir tiŋkin.»

ref: Esau replied, “Let me leave some of my men with you.” “You don't have to do that,” Jacob answered. “I am happy, simply knowing that you are friendly to me.”
text: Ní Eesayuu bí-u yii: «M làá yàntī binib bin nóoń-mee dijandi ń gbíntí a-cee sii nì bi ń táá kí nīn dòoń.» Ní Jaakɔb kíí-u yii: «Ba pu ní a làá gíti ŋá míǹ? Ḿ pée ká sii m-kpiliu cee diyimɔɔl di sá.»

ref: But Jacob went to Succoth, where he built a house for himself and set up shelters for his animals. That's why the place is called Succoth. Jacob Arrives at Shechem
text: Ní Jaakɔb úǹ búntì Sukooti tiŋkin, ki bàn̄ ki máń u-diiŋŋi ní ki gāā u-waŋkuti tibìń. An pu ní bi yíì niin yii Sukooti, taapu di sá yii <Tibìń.> Jaakɔb bàn̄ Kanaan tiŋkin

ref: After leaving northern Syria, Jacob arrived safely at Shechem in Canaan and set up camp outside the city.
text: Jaakɔb nyánní Padan-Aram ki bàn̄ Siikeem, Kanaan tiŋkin nì ŋgbampɔn ní ki gāā u-tantib kitiŋ caŋin ki kāl̀.

ref: The land where he camped was owned by the descendants of Hamor, the father of Shechem. So Jacob paid them
text: U nìn dá ntampaan ŋun pu u gāā u-tantibee Amɔɔr biyaamu cee. Amɔɔr gbanti di nìn sá Siikeem baa. U nìn dá ntampaan gbanti animbil jitɔmbi dilaataal ní.

ref: then he set up his tents and built an altar there to honor the God of Israel.
text: U nìn máń nsaraafan niin, ní ki pú ntampaan gbanti diyindi yii <Eel, Isirayeel Nimbɔtiu>.

ref: Dinah, the daughter of Jacob and Leah, went to visit some of the women who lived nearby.
text: Dalbee, Jaakɔb bisal Diina un Leeya màl̄ee cūtì kí màntì kitiŋ kin poon bi nìn kóòyee sapɔmbi pu.

ref: She was seen by Hamor's son Shechem, the leader of the Hivites, and he grabbed her and raped her.
text: Ní Amɔɔr un sá Eefi tiŋki bɔtiuee jipɔɔn Siikeem ká-u ní ki cúú-u mpɔn ki dōoǹ nì u ki bíil̀-u.

ref: But Shechem was attracted to Dinah, so he told her how much he loved her.
text: An ŋá mimmee ní u-naŋki kɔ́ Jaakɔb bisal Diina tikpil ki jíń mpaan u-pɔbilin. Di sá u nìn bíi sòoǹ nì usapɔɔnee kí gíiǹní u-landɔkɔŋu u-cee.

ref: Shechem even asked his father to arrange for him to marry her.
text: Ní Siikeem bí u-baa Amɔɔr yii: «Cùú mɔ̀nnì usapɔɔn nee kí tīī-mi, m là kí cáá-u.»

ref: Meanwhile, Jacob heard what had happened. But his sons were out in the fields with the cattle, so he did not do anything at the time.
text: Jaakɔb gbìl̄ yii Siikeem dōoǹ nì u-bisal Diina ki bíil̀-u, ama waa nìn lī tiba, di sá yii u-jipɔmbi nìn dáá bíń tisatin kunaakpaau ní u nìn cí yii bí bàń.

ref: just as Jacob's sons were coming in from work. When they learned that their sister had been raped, they became furiously angry, because nothing is more disgraceful than rape, and it must not be tolerated.
text: Jaakɔb jipɔmbi nín nyánní tisatin ki bāań ki gbìl̄ tin jítèe ní an wùn̄-bi tikpil ní bi fōō diŋuul tiŋan pu. Di sá yii Siikeem nín dōoǹ nì Jaakɔb bisalee, an sá disiibikpaandi ní Isirayeel yab cee, ki sá-bi dikoobikpaandi.

ref: Hamor said to Jacob and his sons: My son Shechem really loves Dinah. Please let him marry her.
text: Ní Amɔɔr bí-bi yii: «Ni-bisal Diina kɔ́ m-jipɔɔn Siikeem tikpil, an puee m gbāam̀-ni, ní dū-u kí tīī-u ú cáá.

ref: Why don't you start letting your families marry into our families and ours marry into yours?
text: Tí kɔ́maan tɔbin, nimbi ń nīn tìì-ti ni-sapɔmbi ti cāab̀, ti mun ń nīn tìì-ni ti-yab ni cāab̀.

ref: You can share this land with us. Move freely about until you find the property you want; then buy it and settle down here.
text: Ní gbíntí kí nīn kā ti-cee, ní ti-tiŋki ń kpántí ni-yaka ní nīn kóò, kí nīn tɔ́ ni-ba kí bàntì kí kpántí bitindambi.»

ref: Ask anything, no matter how expensive. I'll do anything, just let me marry Dinah.”
text: Gíímaan-mi kucootiiu nì tinimpucaŋwan pɔndi tikpil ḿ pà. M làá tīī-ni tin ni lèe kɔkɔ. Tin pée páb́ee di sá ní dū usapɔɔn kí tīī-mi ḿ cáá.»

ref: Jacob's sons wanted to get even with Shechem and his father because of what had happened to their sister.
text: Ní Jaakɔb jipɔmbi kíí Amɔɔr nì u-jipɔɔn Siikeem nì iciin poon, kun puee u nìn dōoǹ nì bi-niisal Diina ki bíil̀-u.

ref: So they tricked them by saying: You're not circumcised! It would be a disgrace for us to let you marry Dinah now.
text: Bi kíí-bi yii: «An kaa ŋáań míǹ! Taa ŋūǹ kí dū ti-niisal kí tīī un kaa gíí dikpancandee ticati, an sá-ti icoo ní.

ref: But we will let you marry her, if you and the other men in your tribe agree to be circumcised.
text: Kí wàatī tí gbìl̄ kí tīī nì nee, asee ní yàntī bin kɔkɔ sá bininjab ni-kansikinee ń gīfì akpancan kii timbi nín gíí puee.

ref: Then your families can marry into ours, and ours can marry into yours, and we can live together like one nation.
text: Mimmee ti ŋūǹ kí tīī-ni ti-sapɔmbi ní cáá, ní ti mun ń cáá ni-yab, ní tí gbíntí ni-tiŋkin ní ti-kɔkɔ ń kpāfì kí ŋá kitimbaantii nib.

ref: But if you don't agree to be circumcised, we'll take Dinah and leave this place.
text: Ama ni yaa yíì yii naa làá gīfèe, ti làá fōō ti-niisal ní kí yīkì.»

ref: Hamor and Shechem liked what was said.
text: Bi nín sòon̄ puee fáatì Amɔɔr nì u-jipɔɔn Siikeem.

ref: Shechem was the most respected person in his family, and he was so in love with Dinah that he hurried off to get everything done.
text: Unaacimpɔɔnee nín nìn néeń Jaakɔb bisal tikpil puee yāntì u kíĺ ki bíi ŋáań tin bi bālifì yii ú ŋée malaa. U-baa dicaŋku poonee, úǹ ní bi nìn pée tɔ́ ki jítiǹ báà ŋma.

ref: The two men met with the other leaders of their city and told them:
text: Ní Amɔɔr nì u-jipɔɔn Siikeem cūtìi kāl̀ bi-tiŋki punyɔkɔki caŋin ní ki tùkū bi-tiŋki nib yii:

ref: These people really are friendly. Why not let them move freely about until they find the property they want? There's enough land here for them and for us. Then our families can marry into theirs, and theirs can marry into ours.
text: «Binib nee cáá ti-pu nlandɔkɔmɔnti. Kitiŋ dɔ̄ ki wāā ki tīī-bi bí kàl̄ kí bàntì. Ti ŋūǹ kí cáá bi-bisalib, ní ti mun ŋūǹ kí dū ti-bisalib kí pɔ̄-bi ticati.

ref: We have to do only one thing before they will agree to stay here and become one nation with us. Our men will have to be circumcised just like theirs.
text: Kí wàatī bí kíí kí nīn kā ti-tiŋki pu ti-kɔkɔ ń kpántí kitimbaantii nibee, asee tí yàntī bin kɔkɔ sá bininjab ti-kansikinee ń gīfì akpancan kii bíǹ nín gíí puee.

ref: Just think! We'll get their property, as well as their flocks and herds. All we have to do is to agree, and they will live here with us.
text: Ti yaa gbìl̄ ki tīī nì bi bí nīn kóò ti-ceeyee, bi-waŋkuti kɔkɔ nì bi-gaajati kaa kpántì ti-yati deeee?»

ref: Every grown man followed this advice and got circumcised. Dinah's Brothers Take Revenge
text: Bin kɔkɔ nìn bí niinee fōō tibɔti tin Amɔɔr nì u-jipɔɔn Siikeem cáańee ki kíí, ní kitiŋ poon bininjab kɔkɔ gífì akpancan.

ref: Three days later the men who had been circumcised were still weak from pain. So Simeon and Levi, two of Dinah's brothers, attacked with their swords and killed every man in the town,
text: Bi gífì míǹ iwiin ita dal ŋyunti ŋun bi dáá kā iween puee ní Jaakɔb jipɔmbi bili, Simeeyɔn nì Leefii bin sá Diina niijabee yōoǹ bi-tàataajimmu ki ŋá soooo ki kɔ́ kitiŋin ubɔ kaa béè ní ki kūū bininjab kɔkɔ.

ref: Jacob's other sons came and took everything they wanted. All this was done because of the horrible thing that had happened to their sister.
text: Ní Jaakɔb jipɔmbee kɔ́ kitiŋin ki fúkúl̀ anikpilee wanti ní ki kóoǹ bi-wanti kɔkɔ, kun puee bi bíil̀ bi-niisal.

ref: They took sheep, goats, donkeys, and everything else that was in the town or the countryside.
text: Bi nìn cúú bi-piii nì bi-ŋoobui nì bi-naai nì bi-mammu nì tin kɔkɔ nìn pée bí kitiŋ poon nì tisatinee.

ref: After taking everything of value from the houses, they dragged away the wives and children of their victims.
text: Bi nìn kóoǹ bi-gaajati kɔkɔ pílpil ki kpáaǹ bi-nimpoobi nì bi-biyaamu nì tin kɔkɔ nìn pée bí bi-diiŋŋinee.

ref: Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, “Look what you've done! Now I'm in real trouble with the Canaanites and Perizzites who live around here. There aren't many of us, and if they attack, they'll kill everyone in my household.”
text: Ní Jaakɔb bí Simeeyɔn nì Leefii yii: «Ni kɔ́ǹ-mi tibɔtin! Ni náań kudɔ̀ŋu ki bíl̀ man nì kitiŋ nee nib kansikin ní nee, an dàkà yii Kanaan yab nì Peeriisi yab. Man nib kaa náaǹ. Bíǹ ŋūǹ kí kpāfì kí dāá jáǹ-mi ní kí kúntí m-dicaŋku kɔkɔ.»

ref: They answered, “Was it right to let our own sister be treated that way?”
text: Ní bi kíí bi-baa yii: «Mimmee bi cáá nsan kí bíil̀ ti-niisal kii kilaakpaayeeee?»

ref: God told Jacob, “Return to Bethel, where I appeared to you when you were running from your brother Esau. Make your home there and build an altar for me.”
text: Dalbee ní Unimbɔti bí Jaakɔb yii: «Yīkì doo kí nīn jɔ́ḿ Beeteel kí cùú nīn kóò niin. Á mā nsaraafan laakin m nìn nyántì m-ba ki dàkā-si ŋyunti ŋun a nìn sáń a-maan Eesayuuyee.»

ref: Jacob said to his family and to everyone else who was traveling with him: Get rid of your foreign gods! Then make yourselves acceptable to worship God and put on clean clothes.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bí u-dicaŋku yab nì bin kɔkɔ bí u-ceeyee yii: «Nyāntímaan awaa ŋin bí ni-ceeyee, kí ŋá ni-ba circir kí kpántí ni-wampeeŋkaati.

ref: Afterwards, we'll go to Bethel. I will build an altar there for God, who answered my prayers when I was in trouble and who has always been at my side.
text: Tí yīkìmaan kí cù Beeteel. Ti yaa bàn̄ niinee, m làá mā nsaraafan kí tīī Unimbɔti un un fōō m-wimmeem ŋyunti ŋun m nìn bí dinimbinaamandi poon ní ki céeǹ-mi m-saŋkiŋŋi kɔkɔ poonee.»

ref: So everyone gave Jacob their idols and their earrings, and he buried them under the oak tree near Shechem.
text: Niinee ní bi nyántì awaa ŋin bí bi-ceeyee, ki túutì bi-falfatib ki tīī Jaakɔb ní u cáa gbìn̄ ki síb́ teereebinti subu bun yú ki pìlīǹ Siikeem tiŋkee taapu.

ref: While Jacob and his family were traveling through Canaan, God terrified the people in the towns so much that no one dared bother them.
text: Bi yíkèe ní Unimbɔti yāntì dinyoondi kɔ́ ntimu mun kɔkɔ māntì ki tú-bee, ní báà ubɔ kaa mākaǹ kí jà bi-boon.

ref: Finally, they reached Bethel, also known as Luz.
text: Ní Jaakɔb nì u-nipaaku dūu bàn̄ Luusi, an dàkà yii Beeteel, Kanaan tiŋkin.

ref: Jacob built an altar there and called it “God of Bethel,” because that was the place where God had appeared to him when he was running from Esau.
text: Ní u máń nsaraafan ní ki pú niin diyindi yii Eel-Beeteel, taapu di sá yii: <Beeteel Nimbɔtiu>, kun puee niin ní Unimbɔti nìn nyántì u-ba ki dàkā-u ŋyunti ŋun u nìn sáń u-maan Eesayuuyee.

ref: While they were there, Rebekah's personal servant Deborah died. They buried her under an oak tree and called it “Weeping Oak.” God Blesses Jacob at Bethel
text: Niin ní Deeboora un nìn lōō Reebeekee kpíì ní bi síb́-u seeni subu bun yú ki pìlīǹ Beeteelee taapu. Ní Jaakɔb pú busubu gbanti diyindi yii <Nsiim subu.>

ref: I will give you the land that I promised Abraham and Isaac, and it will belong to your family forever.
text: Kitiŋ kin m nìn tīī Abraam nì Isaakee, m làá dū-kì kí tīī sii nì a-naantiibi bin làá páanní boonee.»

ref: Jacob set up a large rock, so that he would remember what had happened there. Then he poured wine and olive oil on the rock to show that it was dedicated to God,
text: Ní Jaakɔb dū ditaŋkpal ki cóoǹ laakin Unimbɔti sòon̄ nì uee, ní ki dū ndamam nì ŋkpam ki fá di-pu án nīn sá kudaaŋŋu.

ref: Jacob and his family had left Bethel and were still a long way from Ephrath, when the time came for Rachel's baby to be born.
text: Jaakɔb nì u-dicaŋku yíkì Beeteel. Bi cá ki tóob́ Eefiraata tiŋkee ní Raseel màl̄. U-maaliŋu gbanti nìn pɔ́ɔkì tikpil.

ref: She was having a rough time, but the woman who was helping her said, “Don't worry! It's a boy.”
text: Ŋyunti ŋun u bíi yáliŋkí imatiŋween ki làá màal̀ee ní umatimmaanli bí-u yii: «Pɔ́ɔǹ a-ba, kibininjabiki ní a làá gíti màal̀.»

ref: Rachel was at the point of death, and right before dying, she said, “I'll name him Benoni.” But Jacob called him Benjamin.
text: Ŋyunti ŋun u bíi dátí u-ba kí sìntēe ní u pú kibiki kin u màl̄ee diyindi yii Been-Oonii, taapu di sá yii: <M-falaau biki.> Ama ní ki-baa yíiń-kì yii Beenjameen, taapu di sá yii: <M-ŋaŋgiil pu biki.>

ref: Rachel was buried beside the road to Ephrath, which is also called Bethlehem.
text: Raseel kpíìyee ní bi síb́-u Eefiraata dandi kpaanceetiŋu. Kitiŋ gbanti ní bi gíti yíì yii Beetileem.

ref: Jacob set up a tombstone over her grave, and it is still there.
text: Ní Jaakɔb dū ditaŋkpal ki cóoǹ dikaakul pu án nīn sá Raseel kaakul daaŋku. Di bí ki dāa sāā díǹ.

ref: Jacob, also known as Israel, traveled to the south of Eder Tower, where he set up camp.
text: Isirayeel yíkì niinee ní ki cūtìi gāā u-tantib Miigidaal-Eedeer tiŋki nyɔkɔpu.

ref: During their time there, Jacob's oldest son Reuben slept with Bilhah, who was one of Jacob's other wives. And Jacob found out about it. Jacob's Twelve Sons
text: Ŋyunti ŋun Isirayeel nìn kóò kitiŋ gbantinee ní u-jipɔɔn Rubeen dōoǹ nì u-nimpuwaaki Biila, ní u-baa gbìl̄. Jaakɔb jipɔmbi nìn sá saalaa nì bili ní.

ref: Jacob went to his father Isaac at Hebron, also called Mamre or Kiriath-Arba, where Isaac's father Abraham had lived as a foreigner.
text: Jaakɔb nìn bàn̄ u-baa Isaaki cee Mamiree, Kiiriyati-Ariba tiŋkin. Niin ní bi gíti yíì yii Eebrɔn. Abraam nì Isaaki nìn kāl̀ kitiŋ gbantin.

ref: Esau, also known as Edom, had many descendants.
text: Eesayuu un bi gíti yíì Eedoomee naantiibi yiŋŋi sèé.

ref: He married three Canaanite women: The first was Adah, the daughter of Elon the Hittite; the second was Oholibamah, the daughter of Anah and the granddaughter of Zibeon the Hivite;
text: Eesayuu cáá Kanaan tiŋkin ní binimpoobi. Ada un nìn sá Eelɔn, ueetija bisalee nì Ooliibama un nìn sá Ana bisal nì Siibeeyɔn ueefija naantiiuee.

ref: the third was Basemath, who was Ishmael's daughter and Nebaioth's sister.
text: U nìn gíti cáá Isimaayeel bisal Basmaati, Neebayɔti niisal.

ref: Esau and his three wives had five sons while in Canaan. Adah's son was Eliphaz; Basemath's son was Reuel; Oholibamah's three sons were Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
text: ní Ooliibama màl̄-u Yeewusi nì Yalaam nì Kɔɔra. Mbininjabiyaamu mun Eesayuu màl̄ Kanaan tiŋkinee dee.

ref: Esau took his children and wives, his relatives and servants, his animals and possessions he had acquired while in Canaan, and moved far from Jacob.
text: Boonee Eesayuu yōoǹ u-nimpoobi nì u-jipɔmbi nì u-bisalib nì binib bin kɔkɔ pée bí u-dicaŋkunee, nì u-waŋkuti kɔkɔ nì tigaajati tin kɔkɔ u kāl̀ Kanaan tiŋkin ki kée ki yíkìi búntì kitiŋ kiba poon ki wāatì u-naal Jaakɔb.

ref: He did this because the land was too crowded and could not support him and his brother with their flocks and herds.
text: Di sá yii bi nìn cáá tigaajati tikpil, ní kitiŋ kin poon bi kóòyee kaa nìn gíti bà-bi, kun puee bi-waŋkuti nìn būtì tikpil.

ref: That's why Esau made his home in the hill country of Seir.
text: Míǹ pu ní Eesayuu, un bi gíti yii Eedoomee, cūtìi kóòń Seeyiir jool pu. Eesayuu naantiibi

ref: Esau and Adah's oldest son was Eliphaz, and the clans that descended from him were Teman, Omar, Zepho, Kenaz,
text: Eesayuu maaliŋu poon bin sá adican kpilibee yiŋŋi di sá Teeman nì Oomaar nì Seefoo nì Keenaasi

ref: Korah, Gatam, and Amalek. These and Esau's other descendants lived in the land of Edom.
text: nì Kɔɔra nì Gatam nì Amaleeki. Bi nìn kóò Eedoom tiŋkin ní. Eesayuu nì u-nimpuu Ada jipɔŋkpiliu Eeliifaasi jipɔmbi dee.

ref: The clans that descended from Esau and Basemath's son Reuel were Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah.
text: Eesayuu jipɔɔn Reewuyeel maaliŋu poon bin sá adican kpilibee yiŋŋi di sá Naati nì Seera nì Sama nì Miisa. Bi nìn kóò Eedoom ní ki sá Eesayuu nì u-nimpuu Basmaati naantiibi.

ref: The clans that descended from Esau and Oholibamah the daughter of Anah were Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
text: Eesayuu nimpuu Ooliibama, Ana bisal jipɔmbi bin sá adican kpilibee yiŋŋi di sá Yeewusi nì Yalaam nì Kɔɔra.

ref: All of these clans descended from Esau, who was known as Edom.
text: Eedoom yab dicaŋŋi kpilib dee, ní bi-kɔkɔ sá Eesayuu naantiibi.

ref: Seir was from the Horite tribe that had lived in Edom before the time of Esau. The clans that had descended from him were Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,
text: Bin nìn péé péé ki kāl̀ Eedoom tiŋkinee di sá uoorja Seeyiir naantiibi. Bi-yiŋŋi di sá Lootan nì Soobaal nì Siibeeyɔn nì Ana

ref: Lotan's sons were Hori and Heman; his sister was Timna.
text: Lootan jipɔmbi di sá Oori nì Eemam ní u-niisal sá Tiimina.

ref: Shobal's sons were Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam.
text: Soobaal jipɔmbi di sá Alfan nì Manaati nì Eebaal nì Seefoo nì Oonam.

ref: Zibeon's sons were Aiah and Anah—the same Anah who found an oasis in the desert while taking the donkeys of his father out to pasture.
text: Siibeeyɔn jipɔmbi di sá Aya nì Ana. Ana gbanti di sá un dāa nìn ká dinyimbɔbil diba kuteeun ŋyunti ŋun u nìn bíi kpàá u-baa Siibeeyɔn mammuee.

ref: Anah's children were Dishon and Oholibamah.
text: Ana jipɔɔn di sá Diisɔn ní u-bisal sá Ooliibama.

ref: Dishon's sons were Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran.
text: Diisan jipɔmbi di sá Eemidan nì Eesban nì Yitiran nì Keeran.

ref: Ezer's sons were Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan.
text: Eeseer jipɔmbi di sá Biiliyan nì Saafan nì Yaakan.

ref: Dishan's sons were Uz and Aran.
text: Diisan jipɔmbi di sá Usi nì Aran.

ref: The clans of the Horites were Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,
text: Oori yab bin nìn sá adican kpilibee di sá Lootan nì Soobaal nì Siibeeyɔn nì Ana

ref: Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan, and they lived in the land of Seir.
text: nì Diisɔn nì Eeseer nì Diisan. Bíǹ di nìn sá Oori yab dicaŋŋi kpilib Seeyiir tiŋkin ki dī nì bi-dicaŋŋi nín sá puee.

ref: The clans that descended from Esau took their names from their families and the places where they lived. They are Timna, Alvah, Jetheth,
text: Eesayuu maaliŋu poon bɔtiibi nì bi-naakɔti nì bi-timmu nì bi-yiŋŋi di sá Tiimina nì Aliwa nì Jeeteeti

ref: Oholibamah, Elah, Pinon,
text: nì Ooliibama nì Eela nì Piinɔn

ref: Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar,
text: nì Keenaasi nì Teeman nì Miibsaar

ref: Magdiel, and Iram. These clans descended from Esau, who was known as Edom, the father of the Edomites. They took their names from the places where they settled.
text: nì Magidiyeel nì Iram. Eedoom tiŋki bɔtiibi nín sá puee dee, ubɔti kamaa nì u-tiŋki kin pu u kóò ki yèe. Eesayuu di sá Eedoom yab naanja.

ref: Jacob lived in the land of Canaan, where his father Isaac had lived,
text: Jaakɔb nìn kóò Kanaan tiŋkin laakin u-baa nìn kāl̀ee ní.

ref: “They're not here anymore,” the man replied. “I overheard them say they were going to Dothan.” Joseph left and found his brothers in Dothan.
text: Ní uninjee bí-u yii: «Bi yíkì doo, di sá yaadalee m nìn gbìl̄ bi bíi lì yii: <Tí yīkìmaan doo kí nīn cá Dootan.>» Ní Jooseefi nín jì bi-boon Dootan ní ki tin ká-bi niin.

ref: Jacob loved Joseph more than he did any of his other sons, because Joseph was born when Jacob was very old. Jacob had even given Joseph a fancy coat
text: Bi-baa Isirayeel nìn néeń Jooseefi ki jítiǹ u-jipɔmbi bin kíǹee kɔkɔ, kun puee u nìn màl̄-u u-tikpiti poon ní. U nìn ŋáĺ-u dibɔkutimɔntiil diba.

ref: which showed that Joseph was his favorite son, and so Joseph's brothers hated him and would not be friendly to him.
text: U-mambi nín dáań ki ká yii bi-baa néeń-u ki jítiǹ bíǹ kɔkɔee ní bi kíǹ ki ná-u kaa ti sòoǹ nì u an mɔ̄.

ref: One day, Joseph told his brothers what he had dreamed, and they hated him even more.
text: Dalbee Jooseefi dámintì kudamintiiu kubɔ ní ki dūu máafì u-mambi. Niin ní kináŋ mɔ̄tìi nín pɔ́ɔkì.

ref: Joseph said, “Let me tell you about my dream.
text: U tùkū-bi yii: «Ní pīlifìmaan kudamintiiu kun m dámintèe!

ref: We were out in the field, tying up bundles of wheat. Suddenly my bundle stood up, and your bundles gathered around and bowed down to it.”
text: M dámintì yii ti bí kusaau ki bōob̀ amɔpin, ní man yal yáatìi yúl̀, ní nimbi mɔpiŋŋi māntìi tú man mɔpindi ní ki lítì di-taapu.»

ref: His brothers asked, “Do you really think you are going to be king and rule over us?” Now they hated Joseph more than ever because of what he had said about his dream.
text: Niinee ní u-mambi kíí-u yii: «Mimmee a dàkafī yii sii di làá nīn sá ti-bɔtiu deeyaaa, àá sii di làá nīn sá ti-kpiliu ní timbi ń nīn bí sii taapu ní?» U-damintiiku gbanti nì u nín sòon̄ puee yāntì bi-náŋki mɔ̄tìi wíiǹ u-pu.

ref: Joseph later had another dream, and he told his brothers, “Listen to what else I dreamed. The sun, the moon, and eleven stars bowed down to me.”
text: Boonee ní u gíti dámintì ní ki tí dūu máafì u-mambi yii: «M tí dámintì kudamintiiu kubɔ. M dámintì yii ŋwìiń nì uŋmal nì iŋmalbijaa saalaa nì ubɔ dòoń ki gbàantí m-nimbiin.»

ref: When he told his father about this dream, his father became angry and said, “What's that supposed to mean? Are your mother and I and your brothers all going to come and bow down to you?”
text: Ní u dūu lī u-baa nì u-mambi cee, ní u-baa bíi wòoǹ-u yii: «Ba damintiiku kúǹ dee! Mimmee man nì a-na nì a-mambi làá dāá gbàan̄ sii nimbiinaaa?»

ref: Joseph's brothers were jealous of him, but his father kept wondering about the dream. Joseph Is Sold and Taken to Egypt
text: Ní u-mambi mɔ̄tìi díkí u-pu, ama u-baa úǹ dū an bɔtee ki ŋá u-taalin. Jooseefi mambi dū-u ki nyáfì

ref: One day when Joseph's brothers had taken the sheep to a pasture near Shechem,
text: Dalbee ní Jooseefi mambi búntì Siikeem tiŋkin kí kpàà bi-baa piii.

ref: his father Jacob said to him, “I want you to go to your brothers. They are with the sheep near Shechem.” “Yes, sir,” Joseph answered.
text: Ní Isirayeel bí Jooseefi yii: «Kéè a-mambi bíń Siikeem ki kpàá ipiiyaa! Dāan̄ ḿ tɔ̄-si bi-cee.» Ní u kíí u-baa yii: «Too, m sɔ̀.»

ref: His father said, “Go and find out how your brothers and the sheep are doing. Then come back and let me know.” So he sent him from Hebron Valley. Joseph was near Shechem
text: Ní u-baa bí-u yii: «Á cù kí kpèè yii a-mambi pɔ̄ɔ̄ɔɔ, bi-waŋkuti mun bí mana, kí gítiń kí tùkù-mi.» Eebrɔn tantakalin ní bi nìn bí ní Jooseefi baa tú-u ní u yíkìi búntì Siikeem tiŋkin u-mambi cee.

ref: and wandering through the fields, when a man asked, “What are you looking for?”
text: U búntèe ní ki bàn̄ ki tú ki yìiǹ tisatin ní uninja ubɔ dāa ká-u ní ki bālifì-u yii: «A nyàab̀ ba?»

ref: Joseph answered, “I'm looking for my brothers who are watching the sheep. Can you tell me where they are?”
text: Ní u kíí-u yii: «M nyàab̀ m-mambi ní. M gbāam̀-si, a yaa nyí laakin bi bí ki kpàá bi-waŋkutee, á dàkà-mi.»

ref: But before he got there, they saw him coming and made plans to kill him.
text: Bi nín ká-u u páaǹní ndan kaa láá bāań-bee, ní bi sòon̄ ki bɔ́ntì yii bí kpɔ̀-u.

ref: They said to one another, “Look, here comes the hero of those dreams!
text: Bi nìn tùkù ki nàatī tɔb yii: «Kpèè tidamintiil daanee di dòońee.

ref: Reuben heard this and tried to protect Joseph from them. “Let's not kill him,” he said.
text: Rubeen gbìl̄ míǹ ki là kí fīī-u bi-ŋaanee ní ki lī yii: «Tí taa lēē u-maŋfal.»

ref: “Don't murder him or even harm him. Just throw him into a well out here in the desert.” Reuben planned to rescue Joseph later and take him back to his father.
text: U nìn bí-bi yii: «Ní taa kpɔ̀maan-u, ní dū-u kí tɔ̄ dilɔkɔgindi din bí kuteeun nee poon kí taa ŋá-u tiba.» U lī míǹ ki là yii bi yaa dū-u ki tɔ́ dilɔkɔgindinee, boonee úǹ ń dāá ŋá soooo kí nyāntí-u kí cáá-u kí kpìnnì kí tin tīī u-baa.

ref: When Joseph came to his brothers, they pulled off his fancy coat
text: Ŋyunti ŋun Jooseefi bāań bi-ceeyee ní bi pēetì-u u-bɔkutimɔntiil din u pēēyee.

ref: As Joseph's brothers sat down to eat, they looked up and saw a caravan of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead. Their camels were loaded with all kinds of spices that they were taking to Egypt.
text: Bi ŋá mimmee ní ki cūtìi kāl̀ ki bíi jīǹ tijin. Bi dāa kpēēyee, Isimaayeel naakookun yab di páń tɔb pu ki nyánní Galaadi tiŋkinee. Bi-gutaamui nìn tú ikpafi nì talaalub ní bi nìn cáa cáǹ Eejipiti kí nyāfì.

ref: So Judah said, “What will we gain if we kill our brother and hide his body?
text: Ní Juuda bí u-nabiyaamu yii: «Ti yaa bál̀ ki kūū ti-naalee, ba ní ti páatì poon?

ref: Let's sell him to the Ishmaelites and not harm him. After all, he is our brother.” And the others agreed.
text: Tí dū-u kí nyāfiǹ Isimaayeel yab kí taa kpɔ̀-u, kun puee u sá ti-nabiki, ní ti sá fatikubaanti.» Ní u-nabiyaamu gbìl̄ ki tīī nì u.

ref: When the Midianite merchants came by, Joseph's brothers took him out of the well, and for
text: Madiyan nyampoobi jītée ní bi nyántìń Jooseefi dilɔkɔgindi poon ní ki dū-u ki nyáfiǹ Isimaayeel yab. Bi nìn nyáfì-u animbil jitɔmbi mmuŋku ní, ní bíǹ cáá-u ki búntì Eejipiti.

ref: When Reuben returned to the well and did not find Joseph there, he tore his clothes in sorrow.
text: Ŋyunti ŋun Rubeen dāa cūtì kí nyāntí Jooseefi dilɔkɔgindinee, u bàn̄ee u kpá, ní an wùn̄-u tikpil ní u dáaĺ tiwan tin u pēēyee ki cátì.

ref: Then he went back to his brothers and said, “The boy is gone! What am I going to do?”
text: Ní u nín fàtī u-naalib cee ní ki bí-bi yii: «Jooseefi gíti kpá dilɔkɔgindin! Kin man làá ŋá m-ba la?»

ref: Joseph's brothers killed a goat and dipped Joseph's fancy coat in its blood.
text: Niinee ní bi kūū uŋɔja ní ki dū Jooseefi bɔkutil ki tɔ́ fatikun.

ref: After this, they took the coat to their father and said, “We found this! Look at it carefully and see if it belongs to your son.”
text: Bi nìn dū dibɔkutimɔntiilee ki nākaǹ bí cáá tīī bi-baa kí lì yii: «Kpèè kun ti píińee, kpèè tiŋan yii an sá a-jipɔɔn bɔkutilaaa, an kaa sá?»

ref: Jacob knew it was Joseph's coat and said, “It's my son's coat! Joseph has been torn to pieces and eaten by some wild animal.”
text: Bi-baa fōō ki kpēē ki béè dibɔkutilee ní ki fáá mbiil yii: «M-biki Jooseefi bɔkutil dee! Upeecuncuti ubɔ cúú m-biki ki ŋmɔ́, Jooseefi ní an lafun cúú ki tɔ̄ntì!»

ref: Jacob mourned for Joseph a long time, and to show his sorrow he tore his clothes and wore sackcloth.
text: Niinee ní Jaakɔb dáaĺ tiwan tin u pēēyee ki cátì ki dū kutuku ki gbìn̄ u-caŋ, ní ki dū iwiin kupaau ki súń u-biki kpool.

ref: All of Jacob's children came to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted. “No,” he said, “I will go to my grave, mourning for my son.” So Jacob kept on grieving.
text: U-jipɔmbi nì u-bisalib kɔkɔ nìn pɔ́ɔǹ kí ŋméeǹ-u, ama ní waa kíí bí ŋméeǹ-u, ní ki lì yii: «M làá nīn pée sūǹ m-jipɔɔn kpool míǹ ní kí dāá kpú kí bà-u ditaŋkpiilin.» Míǹ ní Jooseefi baa lafun súń u-pu.

ref: Meanwhile, the Midianites had sold Joseph in Egypt to a man named Potiphar, who was the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Ŋyunti gbantee Madiyan yab bíǹ dūu bàn̄ Eejipiti ní ki mun nìkī Jooseefi ki nyáfiǹ Pootifaar, ujanjakpil un cí Faaroo cindee.

ref: About that time Judah left his brothers in the hill country and went to live near his friend Hirah in the town of Adullam.
text: Ŋyunti gbantee, Juuda yíkì u-nabiyaamu cee ki búntì Adulaam tiŋkin kijapaai kiba cee, bi yíì-u yii Yiira.

ref: While there he met the daughter of Shua, a Canaanite man. Judah married her,
text: Niin ní Juuda ká ukanaanja Suuwa bisal ki cáá ní u nì u kpáfì.

ref: and they had three sons. He named the first one Er;
text: Unimpuee cáá dipool ki màl̄ kibininjabiki, ní Juuda pú-kì diyindi yii Eer.

ref: The third one was born when Judah was in Chezib, and she named him Shelah.
text: Ní u fātìi tí cáá dipool ki màl̄ kibininjabiki ní ki pú-kì diyindi yii Seera. Juuda nìn bí Keesiib tiŋkin ní u-nimpuu màl̄ kibiki gbanti.

ref: Later, Judah chose Tamar as a wife for Er, his oldest son.
text: Juuda jipɔŋkpiliu Eer wíiǹee ní u cáá unimpu ki tīī-u. Bi yíì unimpuee yii Taamaar.

ref: But Er was very evil, and the Lord took his life.
text: Juuda jipɔŋkpiliu Eer nìn sá unikpiti ti-Dindaan nimbiin, an puee ní ti-Dindaan yāntì u kpíì.

ref: So Judah told Onan, “It's your duty to marry Tamar and have a child for your brother.”
text: Ní Juuda bí Oonan yii: «Fōō a-maan nimpuu ukpiipu kii mara nín pée sá puee, kí màal̀ kí téeǹ u-yil u-dicaŋku ń taa kúntí.»

ref: Onan knew the child would not be his, and when he had sex with Tamar, he made sure that she would not get pregnant.
text: Ama Oonan nín nyí yii u-maan nimpuu biyaamu mun u làá màal̀ee kaa dāa sá u-biyaamuee, ní ŋyunti ŋun u yaa bíi dɔ̀ nì unimpuee ní u nyāntí ki féeń kitiŋ kaa là kí màal̀ kí téeǹ u-maan yil.

ref: The Lord wasn't pleased with Onan and took his life too.
text: An bɔtee kaa nìn mɔ́ɔkiǹ ti-Dindaan, ní u yāntì u mun kpíì.

ref: Judah did not want the same thing to happen to his son Shelah, and he told Tamar, “Go home to your father and live there as a widow until my son Shelah is grown.” So Tamar went to live with her father.
text: Ní Juuda bí u-coopu Taamaar yii: «Cù a-baabi cee kí nīn kā dikpiipool ki cí ŋyunti ŋun m-jipɔɔn Seera mun làá dāá wíiǹee.» Di sá u nìn lī u-neen yii: «Seera mun ń taa kpú kii u-mambee.» Ní Taamaar nín kùǹ u-baabi.

ref: Some years later Judah's wife died, and he mourned for her. He then went with his friend Hirah to the town of Timnah, where his sheep were being sheared.
text: An dāa bàn̄ ŋyunti ŋubɔee ní Juuda nimpuu Suuwa bisal kpíì. Juuda kāl̀ dikpool jítèe ní u nì u-bɔɔ Yiira uadulaanja búntì Tiimina tiŋkin, bin bíi cēē u-piii kutee cee.

ref: Tamar found out that her father-in-law Judah was going to Timnah to shear his sheep.
text: Ní bi tùkū Taamaar yii: «A-cooja cá Tiimina kí cā u-piii kuti.»

ref: She also realized that Shelah was now a grown man, but she had not been allowed to marry him. So she decided to dress in something other than her widow's clothes and to cover her face with a veil. After this, she sat outside the town of Enaim on the road to Timnah.
text: Niinee ní u pēetì dikpiipool wanti tin u pēēyee ní ki dū ŋŋaabu ki bīiǹ u-nimbiin ki kpáaǹ u-wunti, ní ki cūtìi kāl̀ Eeniyayiim tiŋki sampuyakatii, laakin bi dì ki cá Tiimina tiŋkinee. Di sá u dáań ki ká yii Seera wíiǹ ní baa dɔ́miń ki yōoǹ-u ki tīī-u.

ref: When Judah came along, he did not recognize her because of the veil. He thought she was a prostitute
text: Juuda ká Taamaar kā nsampuyakatee ní u dākafì yii an sá kinimpulaakpaai, kun puee u nìn bīī tiwan u-nimbiin.

ref: “One of my young goats,” he answered. “What will you give me to keep until you send the goat?” she asked.
text: Ní Juuda kíí-u yii: «M làá lēeń m-ŋɔcaŋkpuŋku poon diŋɔbil diba kí tīiń-si.» Ní unimpuee bí-u yii: «See á tīī-mi tiba ḿ yóoń m-cee toolima. A yaa láa tīiń-mi diŋɔbilee, á fōō a-wanti.»

ref: “What do you want?” he asked in return. “The ring on that cord around your neck,” was her reply. “I also want the special walking stick you have with you.” He gave them to her, they slept together, and she became pregnant.
text: Ní Juuda bālifì-u yii: «Ba ní a là ḿ tīī-si á yóoń a-cee toolima?» Ní unimpuee bí-u yii: «Tīī-mi a-jaliiki kin a cáa nyìitēe nì ki-ŋmiiŋu nì ŋkpandɔ ŋun a ŋúb́ee». Ní Juuda tīī-u an wanti gbanti kɔkɔ ní ki dōoǹ nì u ní dipool kɔ́.

ref: After returning home, Tamar took off the veil and dressed in her widow's clothes again.
text: Ní unimpuee fātìi nín kùǹ u-dumpu ní ki lēē ŋŋaabu ŋun u nìn dūu bīiǹee ní ki gíiǹ dikpiipool wanti ki pēeǹ.

ref: Judah asked his friend Hirah take a goat to the woman, so he could get back the ring and walking stick, but she wasn't there.
text: Ní Juuda dū diŋɔbil ki tú u-bɔɔ uadulaanja ú cáá tīī unimpuee kí fōoń u-wanti tin u dūu yóoǹ u-cee toolimee. U-bɔɔ bàn̄ee ní kaa ká unimpuee.

ref: Hirah asked the people of Enaim, “Where is the prostitute who sat along the road outside your town?” “There's never been one here,” they answered.
text: Ní u bālifì binib bin bí niinee yii: «Kinimpulaakpaai kin kàal̀ Eeniyayiim tiŋkin doo, nsan caŋinee bí la?» Ní bi kíí-u yii: «Kinimpulaakpaai kiba pée kpá doo.»

ref: Hirah went back and told Judah, “I couldn't find the woman, and the people of Enaim said no prostitute had ever been there.”
text: Ní Juuda bɔɔ gítì ki tùkū-u yii: «Maa ká-u, ní niin yab mun bí-mi yii: <Kinimpulaakpaai kiba pée kpá doo.>»

ref: “If you couldn't find her, we'll just let her keep the things I gave her,” Judah answered. “And we'd better forget about the goat, or else we'll look like fools.”
text: Ní Juuda bí u-bɔɔ yii: «Yàntī ú nīn cáa cá. Tí taa dī tiwan gbanti boon icoo ń dāá cúú-ti. Kéè man tīī-si diŋɔbil á cáá tīī-u ní a cūnnì kaa ká-ueeee!»

ref: About three months later someone told Judah, “Your daughter-in-law Tamar has behaved like a prostitute, and now she's pregnant!” “Drag her out of town and burn her to death!” Judah shouted.
text: Iŋmal ita boonee ní bi dɔ́miń ki tùkū Juuda yii: «A-coopu Taamaar ŋá tilaakpaal ki cáá dipool.» Ní Juuda bí yii: «Bí nyāntí-u kí cáá-u kí wà ŋŋmi.»

ref: As Tamar was being dragged off, she sent someone to tell her father-in-law, “The man who gave me this ring, this cord, and this walking stick is the one who got me pregnant.”
text: Ŋyunti ŋun bi nyántì-u ki cáǹ kí wà ŋŋmee ní u tú bí tùkù u-cooja yii: «Tiwan tin m ŋúb́ nee, uninja un tīī-mi dipoolee di yì tiwan gbanti. M gbāam̀-si, kpèè kijalii kin bi cáa nyìitī tiwanee nì ki-ŋmiiŋu nì ŋkpandɔ nee, a nyí un yì tiwan gbanteeee?»

ref: “Those are mine!” Judah admitted. “She's a better person than I am, because I broke my promise to let her marry my son Shelah.” After this, Judah never slept with her again.
text: Juuda béè tiwanee ní ki bí yii: «U cáá ibaamɔn ki jítiǹ man. An bàà sá ḿ dū-u kí tīī m-jipɔɔn Seera, ní maa ŋá míǹ». Ní Juuda kaa ti pée gbēē-u.

ref: Tamar later gave birth to twins. But before either of them was born, one of them stuck a hand out of her womb. The woman who was helping tied a red thread around the baby's hand and explained, “This one came out first.”
text: Ŋyunti ŋun u bíi màal̀ee ní jaasibee kansikin ubɔ nyántìń u-ŋal, ní umatimmaanli dū gulfaman ki kúĺ, ní ki bí yii: «Un bí nee di péé ki nyáń.»

ref: At once his hand went back in, and the other child was born first. The woman then said, “What an opening you've made for yourself!” So they named the baby Perez.
text: Ní kibikee fātìi gíiǹ ki-ŋal ki kɔ́ǹ, ní ki-nabiki nyánní, ní umatimmaanli bí yii: «A ŋá mpɔn ki tùl̄ ki nyánnáa!» Ní bi pú-kì diyindi yii Peereesi, taapu di sá yii: <U tùl̄ ki nyáń.>

ref: When the brother with the red thread was born, they named him Zerah.
text: Boonee ní u-nabiki kin ŋal bi bàà dū gulfaman ki kúĺee mun nyánní. Ní bi pú-kì diyindi yii Seera, taapu di sá yii: <Un jìl̀ee.>

ref: The Ishmaelites took Joseph to Egypt and sold him to Potiphar, the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Isimaayeel yab cáá Jooseefi ki cūnnì Eejipitee ní Faaroo janjakpiliu Pootifaar, ueejipitija dá-u bi-cee. Pootifaar gbanti di nìn sá bin cí Faaroo cindee kpiliu.

ref: So Joseph lived in the home of Potiphar, his Egyptian owner. Soon Potiphar realized that the Lord was helping Joseph to be successful in whatever he did.
text: Jooseefi kpiliu nìn dáań ki ká yii ti-Dindaan yú u-boon, ki yàntī tin kɔkɔ u ŋáańee cá nyɔkɔpu.

ref: Potiphar liked Joseph and made him his personal assistant, putting him in charge of his house and all of his property.
text: An puee Jooseefi nìn ká diyimɔɔl u-kpiliu cee u dū-u ki kpántì úǹ u-ba tɔntɔnliu, ní ki dū-u ki ŋá ukpil ú nīn kpèé u-cindi poon tuuŋŋi nì tin kɔkɔ u cááyee.

ref: Potiphar left everything up to Joseph, and with Joseph there, the only decision he had to make was what he wanted to eat. Joseph was well-built and handsome,
text: U ká mimmee ní ki dū tikɔkɔ nì tikɔkɔ ki ŋá Jooseefi ŋaan kaa ti pée kpèé báà tiba, asee tijin tin u làá jēe baba pu ní u dàkafī. Jooseefi nì u-kpiliu nimpuu bɔti Jooseefi nìn yáà unil ní ki nìn ŋān̄ unaacimpɔɔn an kaa cáá bí lì.

ref: and Potiphar's wife soon noticed him. She asked him to make love to her,
text: An dāa bāań ŋyunti ŋubɔee ní u-kpiliu nimpuu dū u-nimbiliŋ ki mántì u-pu ní ki bí-u yii: «Dāan̄ kí dòon̄ nì mi.»

ref: but he refused and said, “My master isn't worried about anything in his house, because he has placed me in charge of everything he owns.
text: Ní u yíì, ní ki bí u-kpiliu nimpuu yii: «Kpèè, man ní m-kpiliu dū dicindi tundi kɔkɔ ki ŋúbiǹ. U cáá m-pu dimákal, an puee waa ti kpèé tiba dicindi poon.

ref: No one in my master's house is more important than I am. The only thing he hasn't given me is you, and that's because you are his wife. I won't sin against God by doing such a terrible thing as this.”
text: Ubɔ kaa jítiǹ-mi dicindi nee poon. U pú-mi nsan ḿ ŋá tikɔkɔ, asee sii baba ní maa máaǹ kí gbēē, kun puee a sá u-nimpuu. An puee maa ŋūǹ kí ŋá ikpitikpaan gbanti kí yìntīǹ Unimbɔti.»

ref: She kept begging Joseph day after day, but he refused to do what she wanted or even to go near her.
text: Baabadal unimpuee pée páb̀ Jooseefi ní yii ú dòon̄ nì u, ama ní úǹ kaa pílifíǹ-u.

ref: One day, Joseph went to Potiphar's house to do his work, and none of the other servants were there.
text: Dalbee Jooseefi kɔ́ kudii poon ki bíi tūǹ ditundi báà ubɔ nì ubɔ kpá.

ref: Potiphar's wife grabbed hold of his coat and said, “Make love to me!” Joseph ran out of the house, leaving his coat there in her hands.
text: Niinee ní unimpuee cúú Jooseefi bɔkutil ní ki bí-u yii: «Dòon̄ nì mi!» Ní Jooseefi sáǹ ki nyáń mmɔŋki ki yāntì u-bɔkutil unimpuee ŋaan.

ref: she called in her servants and said, “Look! This Hebrew has come just to make fools of us. He tried to rape me, but I screamed for help.
text: ní u yíiń dicindin nib, ní ki bí-bi yii: «Dāá kpèèmaan! Bi cáań-ti ueebruja ú nīn cáá-ti ki gbíilíǹ ní! U dɔ́miń kí bàà dòon̄ nì man dee ní m fáá mbiil.

ref: And when he heard me scream, he ran out of the house, leaving his coat with me.”
text: U nín gbìl̄ m fáá mbiilee ní u sáǹ ki nín nyēē ki fá u-bɔkutil.»

ref: Potiphar's wife kept Joseph's coat until her husband came home.
text: Ní u dū Jooseefi bɔkutilee ki bíl̀ ki kɔ́ĺ u-ba u-cal ń bàń.

ref: Then she said, “That Hebrew slave of yours tried to rape me!
text: U-cal Pootifaar dāa bāańee ní u gíiǹ ki téeǹ míǹ mbaantiim yii: «Ueebruja uyumbu un a cáań-tee dɔ́miń kí dòon̄ nì mi.

ref: But when I screamed for help, he left his coat and ran out of the house.”
text: Ŋyunti ŋun m fáá mbiilee ní u sáǹ ki fá u-bɔkutil m-cee.»

ref: and threw Joseph in the same prison where the king's prisoners were kept. While Joseph was in prison,
text: Ní u yāntì bi cúú Jooseefi ki cáa tɔ́ sarka laakin ubɔti nìn pìintí bin yīntiǹ-uee. Jooseefi bí sarka Jooseefi kpántì usarkadaan.

ref: the Lord helped him and was good to him. He even made the jailer like Joseph so much that
text: Ama ti-Dindaan nìn yú Jooseefi boon sarkan, ki dàkā-u u-ŋanti ní ki yāntì u ká diyimɔɔl ujanjakpil un kpèé bisarkadambee cee.

ref: he put him in charge of the other prisoners and of everything that was done in the jail.
text: Ujanjakpilee nìn dū bisarkadambi kɔkɔ ní ki ŋúbiǹ Jooseefi. Úǹ di nìn kpèé atuun ŋin kɔkɔ bi nìn tūǹee.

ref: The jailer did not worry about anything, because the Lord was with Joseph and made him successful in all that he did.
text: Ujanjakpilee kaa nìn gíti kpèé atuun ŋin u nìn dūu ŋúbiǹ Jooseefee, kun puee ti-Dindaan nìn yú Jooseefi boon ní ki yàntī u-tuuŋŋi kɔkɔ còom̄-u tiŋan.

ref: While Joseph was in prison, both the king's personal servant and his chief cook made the king angry. So he had them thrown into the same prison with Joseph.
text: Ní u yāntì bi cáá-bi ki pīiǹ sarka ujanjakpil un kpèé u-sarkadambee cee, laakin bi nìn pīiǹ Jooseefee.

ref: They spent a long time in prison, and the official in charge of the palace guard, made Joseph their servant.
text: Ní ujanjakpilee lēē Jooseefi ú nīn bí bi-cee ki tútúǹ-bi atuun. Bi nìn yúntì sarka poon ŋwaam. Jooseefi lēē bisarkadambi bili damintiiti taapu

ref: One night each of the two men had a dream, but their dreams had different meanings.
text: Bi bí sarkan niin ní kunyeeu kubɔee, un pūul̀ Eejipiti bɔtiu kpɔnɔbee nì un bàtī ndaam ki tìì-uee liitil dámintì, báà ŋma nì u-damintiiku nì ku-taapu.

ref: The next morning, when Joseph went to see the men, he could tell they were upset,
text: Kutaa wúntèe Jooseefi dɔ́miń bi-cee ní ki ká yii an kaa mɔ̄ǹ-bi.

ref: and he asked, “Why are you so worried today?”
text: Ní u bālifì-bi yii: «Ba ŋá ní ni kā míǹ díǹ an kaa mɔ̄ǹ-ni ní?»

ref: “We each had a dream last night,” they answered, “and there is no one to tell us what they mean.” Joseph replied, “Doesn't God know the meaning of dreams? Now tell me what you dreamed.”
text: Ní bi kíí-u yii: «Ti dámintì ní ubɔ kaa bí kí tùkù-ti taapu.» Ní Jooseefi bí-bi yii: «Unimbɔti di kaa tùkù tidamintiil taapuɔɔɔ? Míǹ puee māafìmaan-mi ni nín dámintì puee.»

ref: The king's personal servant told Joseph, “In my dream I saw a vine
text: Ní bin bàtī ndaam ki tìì ubɔtee kpiliu máafì Jooseefi u-damintiiku yii: «M dámintì yii busubu bun bi yíì fiinyee di yú m-nimbiin,

ref: with three branches. As soon as it budded, it blossomed, and its grapes became ripe.
text: ki cáá apan ata. Ní busubuee púkuḿ ki lòon̄ abil ní abil mántì.

ref: I held the king's cup and squeezed the grapes into it, then I gave the cup to the king.”
text: Ní m ŋúb́ Faaroo sambinyɔkaaki m-ŋaan, ki kītì fiinyi biliŋee ki ŋmáaḿ ŋi-nyim ki ŋá Faaroo sambinyɔkaakin ní ki dūu tīī-u.»

ref: Joseph said: This is the meaning of your dream. The three branches stand for three days,
text: Ní Jooseefi bí-u yii: «A-damintiiku taapu sèé. Asupan atee, taapu sá iwiin ita.

ref: and in three days the king will pardon you. He will make you his personal servant again, and you will serve him his wine, just as you used to do.
text: An kíǹ iwiin ita Faaroo làá nyāntí-si sarkan kí gíiǹ a-tundi kí ŋúbíǹ-si, ní á tīī-u tinyɔkaal kii a nín nìn pée kpààa tìì-u puee.

ref: But when these good things happen, please don't forget to tell the king about me, so I can get out of this place.
text: Ama m gbāam̀-si, bi yaa fātìi fōō-si ní a-pɔbil sɔ̄ŋkèe, a mun ń téetí kí ŋá-mi tiniti kí sòoǹ m-bɔti kí tùkù Faaroo kí nyāntí-mi sarka nee poon.

ref: I was kidnapped from the land of the Hebrews, and here in Egypt I haven't done anything to deserve being thrown in jail.
text: Di sá yii bi cúuń-mi mpɔn ní Eebru yab tiŋkin. Doo nee muee, maa ti ŋá tiba ní bi dū-mi ki tɔ́ sarka.»

ref: When the chief cook saw that Joseph had given a good meaning to the dream, he told Joseph, “I also had a dream. In it I was carrying three breadbaskets stacked on top of my head.
text: Bin pūul̀ ubɔti kpɔnɔbee kpiliu nín ká yii Jooseefi tùkū u-bɔɔ u-damintiiku taapu an ŋān̄ee, ní u mun bí Jooseefi yii: «Ma mun dámintì yii bikpɔnɔmɔntiibi tikabiti tita ŋɔ́kɔ́ tɔb pu ní m tú.

ref: The top basket was full of all kinds of baked things for the king, but birds were eating them.”
text: Ní kukabiku kun tɔ́kɔ́ yilpuee poonee, bikpɔnɔmɔntiibi bin kɔkɔ ubɔti ŋmóòyee kā, ní inoon dòoń ki jīǹ an kpɔnɔbee m-yil pu.»

ref: Joseph said: This is the meaning of your dream. The three baskets are three days,
text: Ní Jooseefi bí-u yii: «Kpèè, a-damintiiku taapu sèé. Tikabiti titee, taapu di sá iwiin ita.

ref: and in three days the king will cut off your head. He will hang your body on a pole, and birds will come and peck at it.
text: An kíǹ iwiin ita Faaroo làá nyāntí-si sarkan, kí lēē a-yil, kí cáá a-wunti kí lúntí ndɔ pu, ní inoon ń dāá jī a-nanti.»

ref: Three days later, while the king was celebrating his birthday with a dinner for his officials, he sent for his personal servant and the chief cook.
text: Iwiin ita dalee, an dūu ŋmāntiǹ Faaroo maaliwiiŋŋu jiŋaaliu, ní u yíiń u-tɔntɔnliibi kɔkɔ jiŋaal. U yāntì bi nyántì bin bàtī ndaam ki tìì-uee kpiliu nì bin pūul̀-u kpɔnɔbee kpiliu ki cáań u-tɔntɔnliibi kɔkɔ nimbiin.

ref: He put the personal servant back in his old job
text: Ní Faaroo gíiǹ bin bàtī ndaam ki tìì-uee kpiliu tundi ki ŋúbiǹ-u, ní úǹ bātì ndaam ki tīī-u.

ref: and had the cook put to death. Everything happened just as Joseph had said it would,
text: Ama ní u yāntì bi dū bin pūul̀-u kpɔnɔbee kpiliu ki lúntì ndɔ pu, kii Jooseefi nín nìn tùkū-bi bi-damintiiti taapu puee.

ref: Two years later the king of Egypt dreamed he was standing beside the Nile River.
text: Abin ali boonee ní Faaroo dámintì kudamintiiu kubɔ, yii u yú dimɔɔl din bi yíì Niilee caŋin.

ref: Suddenly, seven fat, healthy cows came up from the river and started eating grass along the bank.
text: U yú mimmee ní ki ká inasal iluli, i bí màlmal ki ŋān̄, ki nyánní dimɔɔlee poon ki yú digbándi ki ŋmóò timool.

ref: Then seven ugly, skinny cows came up out of the river and
text: Boonee ní nnagɔŋgɔmu ŋkpiimu mululi mun nyánní dimɔɔl gbantin ki páaǹní in béeyee boon ki dɔ́miń ki yú ki kɔ́ĺ-i dimɔɔgbandi.

ref: ate the fat, healthy cows. When this happened, the king woke up.
text: Ní nnagɔŋgɔmu ŋkpiimuee dū inasal in bí màlmal ki ŋān̄ee ki nátì. Niinee ní Faaroo cúu fīntì.

ref: The king went back to sleep and had another dream. This time seven full heads of grain were growing on a single stalk.
text: Boonee ní Faaroo fātìi gèen̄ ní ki gíti tí dámintì yii idifin iluli in nyìm̀ ki ŋān̄ee túuń dikakabaantiil pu.

ref: Later, seven other heads of grain appeared, but they were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: U kpēēyee ní ki ká ndifiŋkpiimu mululi mun ugbuŋkoon fáá mu fáaḿee nyáń ki kútì idifin in béeyee pu.

ref: The thin heads of grain swallowed the seven full heads. Again the king woke up, and it had only been a dream.
text: Ní ndifiŋkpiimu mun fáaḿee dū idifin in nyìm̀ ki ŋān̄ee ki nátì. Niinee ní Faaroo tí cúu fīntì ní ki ká yii an sá kudamintiiu.

ref: The next morning the king was upset. So he called in his magicians and wise men and told them what he had dreamed. None of them could tell him what the dreams meant.
text: Kutaa wúntèe ubɔti pɔbil bíil̀, ní u tú bi yíiń Eejipiti tiŋki poon bibɔɔbi nì biciŋfitiibi kɔkɔ. Bi bāańee ní ubɔti kàan̄ u nín dámintì puee ki tùkū-bi, ama bi-kansikin báà ubɔ kaa ŋúń ki lēē taapu ki tùkū-u.

ref: The king's personal servant said: Now I remember what I was supposed to do.
text: Niinee ní bin bàtī ndaam ki tìì Faarooyee kpiliu bí ubɔti yii: «Ti-baa, diyìntil din m yīntèe, m làá bìitī di-pu díǹ.

ref: When you were angry with me and your chief cook, you threw us both in jail in the house of the captain of the guard.
text: Di sá dalbee, a nìn fōō timbi a-tɔntɔnliibi pu diŋuul ki cáá man nì bin pūul̀-si kpɔnɔbee kpiliu ki tɔ́ sarkan ujanjakpil un kpèé a-sarkadambee dumpu.

ref: One night we both had dreams, and each dream had a different meaning.
text: Man nì u nìn dámintì kunyabaantiiu poon ní báà ŋma nì u-damintiiku nì ku-taapu.

ref: A young Hebrew, who was a servant of the captain of the guard, was there with us at the time. When we told him our dreams, he explained what each of them meant,
text: Ti nì ueebrunaacimpɔɔn ubɔ nìn bí sarkan niin, u sá ujanjakpil yumbuu. Úǹ ní ti nìn kàan̄ ti-damintiiti ki tùkū ní u lēē báà ŋma damintiiku taapu nín sá puee ki tùkū-u.

ref: and everything happened just as he said it would. I got my job back, and the cook was put to death.
text: U nín tùkū-ti ti-damintiiti taapu puee, míǹ ní an jítì. A fātìi gíiǹ ditundi din m nìn pée tūǹee ki ŋúbiǹ-mi, ní ki yāntì bi lúntì bin pūul̀ a-kpɔnɔbee kpiliu ndɔ pu.» Jooseefi lēē Faaroo damintiiti taapu

ref: The king sent for Joseph, who was quickly brought out of jail. He shaved, changed his clothes, and went to the king.
text: Niinee ní Faaroo tú bi yíiń Jooseefi. Ní bi nyántì-u kusarkadiin malaa, ní u kōol̀ ki kpántì u-wampeeŋkaati ní ki nín cá ubɔti nimbiin.

ref: The king said to him, “I had a dream, yet no one can explain what it means. I am told that you can interpret dreams.”
text: U bàn̄ Faaroo ceeyee ní Faaroo bí-u yii: «M dámintì kudamintiiu kubɔ ní báà ubɔ kaa ŋúń ki tùkū-mi ku-taapu. Ama m gbìl̄ yii bi yaa kàan̄ kudamintiiu ki tùkū-see, a ŋūǹ kí lēē ku-taapu.»

ref: “Your Majesty,” Joseph answered, “I can't do it myself, but God can give a good meaning to your dreams.”
text: Ní Jooseefi kíí Faaroo yii: «An kaa sá man di ŋūǹ kí ŋá tiba, Unimbɔti di làá tùkù-si a-damintiiku taapu ní án sɔ̀ŋkì a-pɔbil.»

ref: The king told Joseph: I dreamed I was standing on the bank of the Nile River.
text: Ní Faaroo bí Jooseefi yii: «M dámintì yii m yú dimɔɔgbandi ní.

ref: I saw seven fat, healthy cows come up out of the river, and they began feeding on the grass.
text: M yúee ní ki ká inasal iluli, i bí màlmal ki ŋān̄, ki nyánní dimɔɔlin ki dɔ́miń ki yú digbándi ki ŋmóò timool.

ref: Next, seven skinny, bony cows came up out of the river. I have never seen such terrible looking cows anywhere in Egypt.
text: Boonee ní nnagɔŋgɔmu ŋkpiimu mululi páaǹní. Doooo nee, maa láá pée ká nnakpiimu míǹ Eejipiti tiŋki kɔkɔ poon ki ká.

ref: But you couldn't tell it, because these skinny cows were just as skinny as they were before. At once, I woke up.
text: Ní a yaa ká-iee, aa lì yii i náĺ tiba gba. I nín pée bí pu ŋgɔŋgɔmu ŋkpiimu puee, míǹ ní i gbíntìi bí. Ní m cúu fīntì.

ref: I also dreamed that I saw seven heads of grain growing on one stalk. The heads were full and ripe.
text: Boonee ní m gíti dámintì yii didikakabaantiil di lòon̄ idifin iluli ní i nyìm̀ ki ŋān̄.

ref: Then seven other heads of grain came up. They were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: Ní ndifiŋkpiimu mululi mun ugbuŋkoon fáá mu fáaḿee mun nyáń ki kútì idifin ipeekaayee pu.

ref: These heads of grain swallowed the full ones. I told my dreams to the magicians, but none of them could tell me the meaning of the dreams.
text: M yúee ní ndifiŋkpiimuee dū idifin in nyìm̀ ki ŋān̄ee ki nátì. M kàan̄ míǹ ki tùkū bibɔɔbi, ama bi-kansikin báà ubɔ kaa ŋúń ki béè taapu ki tùkū-mi!»

ref: Joseph replied: Your Majesty, both of your dreams mean the same thing, and in them God has shown what he is going to do.
text: Ní Jooseefi bí Faaroo yii: «Ti-baa, a-damintiitee liitil pée sá kudamintibaantiiu ní. Unimbɔti di là kí dàkà ubɔti tin u là kí ŋée.

ref: The seven good cows stand for seven years, and so do the seven good heads of grain.
text: Inasal iluli in ŋān̄ee, taapu di sá abin aluli, ní idifin in ŋān̄ee mun taapu sá abin aluli. Niin sá kudamintibaantiiu ní.

ref: The seven skinny, ugly cows that came up later also stand for seven years, as do the seven bad heads of grain that were scorched by the desert wind. The dreams mean there will be seven years when there won't be enough grain.
text: Nnagɔŋgɔmu ŋkpiimu mululi mun páaǹní ipeekaayee boonee, taapu di sá abin aluli mun. Ní ndifiŋkpiimu mululi mun ugbuŋkoon fáá mu fáaḿee taapu sá ŋkùm abin aluli.

ref: It is just as I said—God has shown what he intends to do.
text: Kii m nín bàà tùkū ubɔti puee, Unimbɔti di lēē min u là kí ŋée ki dàkā-u.
